nodes.label: Akahama et al., 2002_Au
nodes.display_label: Akahama et al., 2002 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holmes et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 145 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Unit-cell volumes of Bi, Pt, and Au were measured simultaneously by
powder XRD. Pressure was determined from the Pt EOS. The bcc phase of
Bi was observed to be stable up to the maximum pressure of 222 GPa.
Vinet and Birch–Murnaghan EOS were both tested; the Vinet EOS provided
a marginally better fit based on rms residuals.

This study provides a primary static EOS for bcc Bi at 300 K to
megabar pressures. The atomic volume V0 was determined by iterative
fitting to minimize rms residuals, not taken from ambient-pressure
crystallography.

Although Au and Pt compression data are discussed, the Au EOS values
reported here are explicitly tied to the Pt pressure scale and are
secondary to the main goal of calibrating Bi.

The authors demonstrate that the Au pressure scale underestimates
pressure relative to Pt above ~30 GPa, diverging by ~20 GPa at
145 GPa.

nodes.label: Akahama et al., 2004_Ag
nodes.display_label: Akahama et al., 2004 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 300 μm (150 μm anvil face, 8.5° bevel angle)
fcc Ag reflections (5–8 peaks used per pattern)

Ag and Au samples were simultaneously compressed to ~150 GPa with no PTM, leading to measurable but relatively small differential stresses. Nonhydrostatic stress correction was applied using measured at-values obtained from gamma-plots. A Vinet EOS fit to corrected Ag compression data yields K0 = 89.8(1.3) GPa and K0' = 5.92(7). Constraining K0 to the ultrasonic value of 98(1.6) GPa gives K0' = 5.47(2). Volume correction uncertainties produce up to ±4 GPa pressure uncertainty for Ag at 150 GPa.

The published Ag EOS is one of the few high-pressure Ag calibrations extending to 150 GPa. Both corrected and uncorrected datasets show consistent trends. K0 = 89.8 GPa is preferred for direct comparison within this study, while K0 = 98 GPa (ultrasonic anchored) provides consistency with ultrasonic-based standards.

nodes.label: Akahama et al., 2006_Al_BM_k0fixed
nodes.display_label: Akahama et al., 2006 | Al | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Akahama and Kawamura 2004; Holmes et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 222 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pressures were determined from the EOS of Pt (Holmes et al., 1989) using in situ Pt lattice parameters.
No pressure transmitting medium was used; however, Al itself (lower shear modulus) acted as an efficient
pressure medium, limiting uniaxial stress. Standard deviation of Pt-derived pressures is ~0.6 GPa for the
preferred Vinet fit with K0 fixed at 72.7 GPa and K0' = 4.83. The fcc–hcp transition begins at V/V0 = 0.509(1),
corresponding to 217 ± 10 GPa.

EOS parameters are for fcc-Al at 297 K, fitted to the Vinet formulation with K0 fixed to the ultrasonic/
low-pressure value 72.7 GPa and K0' = 4.83(3). This EOS is proposed as suitable for use as a pressure
standard up to the multimegabar range. Above ~217 GPa, hcp-Al appears and coexists with fcc-Al over a
broad pressure interval; the EOS here describes the fcc branch only.

nodes.label: Akahama et al., 2014_Mo
nodes.display_label: Akahama et al., 2014 | Mo | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holmes et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 410 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 25 μm (Run 1), 20 μm (Run 2); with 250 μm and 450/350 μm bevels
Mo (200) for volume; Pt (111) for pressure

Unit-cell volumes of Mo and Pt were measured simultaneously. Pressure is taken
from the Pt Vinet EOS. The Mo lattice constants were extracted primarily from
the (200) reflection to minimize nonhydrostatic effects, which remained small.
Standard deviation in Mo lattice parameters ranged from 0.0005–0.0036 Å.
Uniaxial stress analysis (Fig. 5 and 6) shows limited nonhydrostatic distortion
thanks to the Re gasket confinement.

The bcc phase of Mo remained stable up to 410 GPa.
The EOS agrees with ultrasonic data to within 2.5% at 200 GPa and 3.3% at 400 GPa,
but differs significantly from shock-derived EOSs (7–11% lower pressures).
The authors conclude the Pt pressure scale is reliable in the multimegabar regime.

nodes.label: Aleksandrov et al., 1987_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Aleksandrov et al., 1987 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Polynomial | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Piermarini et al., 1975; Mao et al., 1978; Jayaraman, 1986 | Max Pressure: 42 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: Diamond at high pressures Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high-pressure scale.

doi_lines.text: Diamond at high pressures Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high-pressure scale.

rows.value:
Pressure determination relies on the ruby R1 fluorescence scale, which the
authors identify as internally inconsistent at high pressures. Above ~35–40 GPa,
deviation from hydrostatic conditions is observed due to helium solidification, leading to
crystal degradation (rocking-curve broadening) and Raman line broadening. X-ray lattice
parameter measurements are limited to a single (440) reflection and are affected by non-
hydrostatic stress. Absolute pressure values above ~40 GPa are model-dependent and tied to
an a prior diamond EOS rather than an independently validated pressure scale.

Aleksandrov et al. (1987) combine Raman scattering and x-ray diffraction to derive the diamond
equation of state up to ~42 GPa and directly determine the Grüneisen parameter for the LTO(T)
mode (γ₀ ≈ 0.965), finding it density-independent up to ~40 GPa. The work demonstrates that
discrepancies between experimental EOS data, ultrasonic measurements, and theory arise
primarily from systematic errors in the ruby pressure scale, and proposes a revised high-
pressure calibration based on an a priori diamond EOS.

nodes.label: Aleksandrov et al., 1987_diamond
nodes.display_label: Aleksandrov et al., 1987 | Diamond | Raman | Akahama06_Equation | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Aleksandrov et al., 1987 | Max Pressure: 42 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: Title: Diamond at high pressures: Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high pressure scale.

doi_lines.text: Title: Diamond at high pressures: Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high pressure scale.

rows.value:
Pressure determination relies on the contemporaneous ruby R1 fluorescence scale, which the
authors explicitly identify as internally inconsistent at high pressures. Above ~35–40 GPa,
deviation from hydrostatic conditions is observed due to helium solidification, leading to
crystal degradation (rocking-curve broadening) and Raman line broadening. X-ray lattice
parameter measurements are limited to a single (440) reflection and are affected by non-
hydrostatic stress. Absolute pressure values above ~40 GPa are model-dependent and tied to
an a priori diamond EOS rather than an independently validated pressure scale.

Aleksandrov et al. (1987) combine Raman scattering and x-ray diffraction to derive the diamond
equation of state up to ~42 GPa and directly determine the Grüneisen parameter for the LTO(T)
mode (γ₀ ≈ 0.965), finding it density-independent up to ~40 GPa. The work demonstrates that
discrepancies between experimental EOS data, ultrasonic measurements, and theory arise
primarily from systematic errors in the ruby pressure scale, and proposes a revised high-
pressure calibration based on an a priori diamond EOS.

nodes.label: Anderson et al., 1989_Au
nodes.display_label: Anderson et al., 1989 | Au | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Neighbours and Alers, 1958; Chang and Himmel, 1966 | Max Pressure: not specified GPa | PTM: not applicable

rows.value: Calculation from publishedUltrasonic + thermal expansion (no direct DAC compression)

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
This study does not report new static compression data.
Instead, it derives a thermodynamically consistent equation
of state for gold by combining ultrasonic elastic constants,
thermal expansivity, and specific heat data.

The isothermal bulk modulus KT0 at 300 K is obtained from
elastic constants using standard thermodynamic relations.
Pressure derivatives are constrained by consistency
between (∂KT/∂P)T, thermal pressure, and anharmonic terms.

The authors argue that thermodynamic consistency favors
(∂KT/∂P)T ≈ 5.5 at ambient conditions, lower than some
earlier values. This choice leads to a nonzero
(∂KT/∂T)V and significant anharmonic corrections to the
thermal pressure at high temperature.

The EOS is expressed as:
  P(V,T) = P_BM(V,300) + P_TH(V,T),
where P_BM is a third-order Birch–Murnaghan isotherm
and P_TH includes explicit anharmonic volume dependence.

This YAML represents the **reference 300 K isotherm only**.
The full thermal EOS (anharmonic Mie–Grüneisen–type
formulation) should be stored as a separate linked entry.

nodes.label: Antonangeli et al., 2008_Co
nodes.display_label: Antonangeli et al., 2008 | Co-hcp | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 90 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 150 μm flats beveled from 300 μm at 8°

Compression of hcp Co measured quasi-hydrostatically in Ne to 90 GPa.
Diffraction showed no fcc contamination. Pressure measured by ruby and 
confirmed by Pt EOS. Compression curve matches previous EOS studies and 
is well fit by a single BM3 equation. Main result of this study is the 
detection of an inversion in dc/a/dP at ~75 GPa, attributed to collapse 
of the magnetic moment.

This study does NOT determine a new EOS for hcp Co. Instead, it confirms 
that previously published BM3 EOS parameters (K0 = 199±6 GPa, K'0 = 3.6±0.2) 
adequately describe the compression. The scientific contribution is the 
high-precision mapping of the c/a ratio, revealing a magnetoelastic anomaly 
near 75 GPa.

nodes.label: Anzellini et al., 2014_Re
nodes.display_label: Anzellini et al., 2014 | Re | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Loubeyre et al., 1993 | Max Pressure: 144 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Three runs were conducuted, run 1 was calibrated using ruby to ~37 GPa with 300 um culet (Dewaele et al., 2004; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007). Run 2 was calibrated using He to ~65 GPa with 150 um culet (Loubeyre et al., 1994). Run 3 was calibrated using W and ruby with 100 um culet (Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007). All runs had He PTM.

nodes.label: Barnett et al., 1973_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Barnett et al., 1973 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | LinearShift | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 10 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.value: ruby R1 and R2 fluorescence lines

This study establishes ruby fluorescence as a practical pressure
sensor in the diamond anvil cell. The pressure dependence of the
ruby R-lines is shown to be approximately linear over the explored
pressure range. The reported pressure coefficient (dλ/dP ≈ 0.365 Å/kbar)
is an experimental measurement intended for comparison and method
validation, not a finalized pressure scale.

Absolute pressure calibration is explicitly stated to be uncertain
above ~40 kbar, and no analytic pressure scale equation is proposed.

Barnett et al. (1973) is a foundational instrumentation paper.
It introduces ruby fluorescence as a pressure sensor and
demonstrates its precision and practicality.

nodes.label: Baty et al., 2024_Pd
nodes.display_label: Baty et al., 2024 | Pd | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Angular-dispersive powder XRD measurements of fcc-Pd were
conducted using He as a pressure-transmitting medium.
Unit-cell volumes were obtained from Pawley refinements.
No structural phase transition was observed up to 80 GPa.

The EOS parameters reported here correspond to the authors’
BM3 description of their own experimental data, which is
consistent with higher-pressure EOS results from Frost et al. (2023).

The reported EOS parameters (V0 = 14.72(4) Å^3/atom,
K0 = 190(3) GPa, K0' = 5.3(2)) describe the 300 K static
compression data collected in this study.

This EOS should be treated as a mid-pressure (≤80 GPa)
experimental reference. For ultra-high-pressure applications
(>100 GPa), Frost et al. (2023) provides a more appropriate
primary Pd EOS.

nodes.label: Bell et al., 1986_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Bell et al., 1986 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Gray 1972; Heinz and Jeanloz 1984 | Max Pressure: 180 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: ruby R1 luminescence line; Au(111,200); Cu(111,200)

Unit-cell volumes of Au and Cu were measured by Mo Kα XRD in the same DAC
experiments as ruby fluorescence. Pressures were obtained from shock-wave–
reduced EOS of Au and Cu (Gray, 1972; Heinz & Jeanloz, 1984). Ruby R1 shifts
were spatially averaged over the X-ray beam footprint to match the XRD
sampling volume. The close agreement of Au- and Cu-derived pressures
validates the ruby calibration to at least 1.8 Mbar.

This study extends the working ruby pressure scale by more than a factor
of two relative to Mao et al. (1978), confirming the same functional form
with an updated prefactor. The equation used here is explicitly that of
Mao et al. (1978), revalidated and extended using Au and Cu standards.
Extrapolation beyond ~1.8 Mbar is discussed but not independently verified. The equation and parameters is identical to Mao et al., 1978

nodes.label: Brown et al., 1999_NaCl-B1
nodes.display_label: Brown 1999 | NaCl-B1 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 25 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: Thermodynamic pressure scale derived from static compression, Hugoniot, and quasiharmonic modeling

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Brown (1999) constructed a quasiharmonic thermodynamic pressure scale using static compression, Hugoniot data, Debye thermal energy, and a volume-dependent Gruneisen parameter.

The original Brown (1999) study did not publish a standard analytic Vinet EOS parameterization. Instead, the EOS was represented through spline-based thermodynamic modeling and tabulated P-V-T data. Shen and Smith (2026) later fit a Rydberg-Vinet EOS to the tabulated 300 K isotherm, producing the parameters listed here.

Rydberg-Vinet EOS parameters:
  K0 = 24.325 ± 0.046 GPa
  K0' = 5.079 ± 0.012

These values were obtained by Shen and Smith (2026)
from fitting the 300 K isotherm tabulated in Brown (1999).

Brown (1999) constructed the NaCl pressure scale using:
  - spline-fit zero-Kelvin compression curve
  - quasiharmonic thermal pressure model
  - Hugoniot reduction
  - Debye thermal energy formalism
  - volume-dependent Gruneisen parameter

The original Brown EOS is not itself a Vinet EOS so category is 3.

nodes.label: Burrage et al., 2019_Ta
nodes.display_label: Burrage et al., 2019 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Anzellini et al., 2014 | Max Pressure: 310 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Ta was measured in non-hydrostatic compression using Re as an internal pressure marker. The bcc phase of Ta was stable to the highest pressure of 310 GPa, corresponding to V/V0 = 0.581. The authors compare two Re calibrations and conclude that the Dubrovinsky et al. (2012) Re EOS overestimates pressure at ultrahigh pressure; the Anzellini et al. (2015) Re calibration is used for the preferred non-hydrostatic Ta EOS.

EOS parameters are from Table 1, "Non-hydrostatic I using Anzellini et al. EOS for Re".
All fit parameters in the study are based on the Rydberg-Vinet equation of state.

The study also reports:
- Non-hydrostatic II using Dubrovinsky et al. (2012) Re EOS:
  K0 = 166.1 ± 1.4 GPa, K0_prime = 6.05 ± 0.01, pressure range 0–422 GPa.
- Quasi-hydrostatic neon-medium experiment using Au pressure marker:
  K0 = 201.0 ± 2.4 GPa, K0_prime = 3.21 ± 0.11, pressure range 0–80 GPa.
- Shock-compression fit:
  K0 = 189.4 ± 1.8 GPa, K0_prime = 3.90 ± 0.05, pressure range 0–230 GPa.

The quasi-hydrostatic experiment used neon as the pressure medium and Au as
the pressure marker, while the ultrahigh-pressure toroidal DAC experiment was
non-hydrostatic and used Re as the pressure marker.

nodes.label: Burrage et al., 2019_Ta_NePTM
nodes.display_label: Burrage et al., 2019 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Quasi-hydrostatic Ta EOS measured using neon pressure-transmitting medium and Au as the internal pressure marker. The authors note that some uniaxial stress likely develops after Ne solidification above ~5 GPa, therefore the experiment is described as quasi-hydrostatic.

EOS parameters are from Table 1, labeled:
"Quasi-hydrostatic (neon-medium)".

Au pressure standard from Dorogokupets and Dewaele (2007).

Comparison with the non-hydrostatic experiment showed only a ~2.5 GPa
pressure difference at 80 GPa, which the authors state is within the
differential stress/yield strength of Ta at these conditions.

nodes.label: Burrage et al., 2019_Ta_dubrovin
nodes.display_label: Burrage et al., 2019 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012 | Max Pressure: 422 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Non-hydrostatic Ta EOS derived using the Dubrovinsky et al. (2012) Re pressure calibration. The authors conclude this Re EOS significantly overestimates pressure at ultrahigh pressure, producing a substantially higher pressure estimate than the Anzellini et al. (2015) calibration at equivalent compression.

EOS parameters are from Table 1, labeled:
"Non-hydrostatic II using Dubrovinsky et al. EOS for Re".

The reported maximum pressure reaches 422 GPa using the Dubrovinsky
Re calibration, compared to 310 GPa using the Anzellini et al. (2015)
Re EOS for the same compression data.

The study states that the Dubrovinsky et al. Re EOS overestimates
pressure in the multimegabar regime and therefore omits this calibration
from preferred comparison with shock and ultrasonic data.

nodes.label: Campbell and Heinz 1990_KCl
nodes.display_label: Campbell and Heinz, 1991 | KCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 56 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
B2 phase measured 2–56 GPa at room T; Birch–Murnaghan fit used Jeanloz finite-strain extrapolation since B2 phase is not stable at 0 GPa. V0 derived by normalization to 
B1 phase molar volume; uncertainty dominated by pressure determination (5–15%).

Room-temperature compression of KCl B2 (CsCl-type) measured to 56 GPa. Birch–Murnaghan finite strain fit yields K0=28.7(6) GPa, K0′=4 (fixed). V0 extrapolated from finite strain analysis with V0^B2 / V0^B1 = 0.8483(57). No PTM was used, ruby fluorescence used for pressure calibration, and nonhydrostatic conditions possible.

nodes.label: Campbell et al., 2009_Ni
nodes.display_label: Campbell et al., 2009 | Ni | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Decker 1971; Fei et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 65 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: laser heated in DAC; annealed to relax deviatoric stress

Campbell et al. measured Ni and NiO simultaneously under identical P–T
conditions to minimize systematic pressure-scale errors. The isothermal
295 K BM3 EOS parameters (V0, K0, K0') are directly listed in Table 1.
No stress correction was required at high T due to annealing.

Only fcc–Ni was observed. No phase transitions occurred over the P–T
range of this study. These parameters represent the 295 K isotherm used
in Ni–NiO fO2 calculations.

nodes.label: Campbell et al., 2023_Bi
nodes.display_label: Campbell et al., 2023 | Bi | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2008 | Max Pressure: 259 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Three independent DAC experiments (Runs A–C) using neon as a soft
pressure medium reduce deviatoric stress relative to earlier work.
Only bcc Bi-V data above 10 GPa are used for EOS fitting. Lineshift
analysis indicates very small uniaxial stress (αt ≪ 1% of total
pressure), supporting near-hydrostatic conditions.

The bcc phase of Bi is not stable at ambient pressure; therefore V0 is
not directly known. The authors report two experimental fits:
(1) free V0: V0 = 31.67 ± 0.19 Å^3, K0 = 38.2 ± 1.5 GPa, K0' = 5.8 ± 0.06
    (preferred and used here);
(2) fixed V0 = 32.23 Å^3 (from DFT), giving K0 = 34.0 ± 0.2 GPa and
    K0' = 6.0 ± 0.02.---called b
Both fits yield nearly identical P–V curves above 10 GPa.

Pressure determination uses Cu (Runs A, B) and Ne (Run C), with Ne
calibrated internally to Cu to minimize pressure-scale chaining.
This study supersedes Akahama et al. (2002) by extending the pressure
range to ~260 GPa under softer, better-controlled stress conditions.

nodes.label: Chidester et al., 2021_KCl_BM
nodes.display_label: Chidester et al., 2021 | KCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 167 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
The 300 K isothermal EOS of B2-KCl was obtained by fitting
room-temperature data from Dewaele et al. (2012) together
with new high-P data collected prior to heating. A third-order
Birch–Murnaghan EOS was found to better describe the
low-pressure B2 data than a Rydberg–Vinet form.

The 200 reflection of KCl shows strong sensitivity to
nonhydrostatic strain and was excluded above ~45 GPa;
above ~130 GPa it is no longer observable.

This YAML represents the reference 300 K EOS used as the
baseline for the full Mie–Grüneisen–Debye thermal EOS
developed in this study.

The authors also report an alternative Rydberg–Vinet
reference EOS (V0 = 34.3(5) cm^3/mol, K0 = 13(1) GPa,
K0' = 6.2(1)), but favor the Birch–Murnaghan form because
it better captures the B1–B2 transition behavior and yields
lower systematic residuals at low pressure.

Thermal parameters reported separately are:
γ0 = 2.9(4), q = 1.0(1), θD = 235 K.

nodes.label: Chijioke et al., 2005_Al
nodes.display_label: Chijioke et al., 2005 | Al | XRD | Vinet-Extended | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Mitchell and Nellis 1981; McQueen et al., 1970 | Max Pressure: 200 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.value: High-pressure equations of state of Al, Cu, Ta, and W

doi_lines.text: High-pressure equations of state of Al, Cu, Ta, and W

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Hugoniot data sources include Mitchell & Nellis (1981) and McQueen et al. (1970),
as summarized in Table I of Chijioke et al. (2005).
Thermal pressure corrections follow Wang et al. (2002).
Strength corrections use experimental results compiled by Asay et al. (1986).

The maximumdeviations of the data from the Vinet fits are 0.8 GPa for Ta, 0.5 GPa for W, 0.1 GPa for Cu, and 0.2 GPa for Al.

"Hugoniot data were reduced to isotherms using calculated thermal pressures. For these four metals,
available experimental results permitted corrections of Hugoniot data for shock-induced strength as
a function of shock pressure. High-pressure shock-wave data are extended to zero pressure using
ultrasonically determined bulk moduli."

nodes.label: Cynn and Yoo 1998_Ta
nodes.display_label: Cynn and Yoo 1999 | Ta | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Heinz and Jeanloz 1984 | Max Pressure: 174 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 300 μm (low P), beveled 300→100 μm with 7° bevel (high P)

Angle-resolved synchrotron XRD was used to measure the compression of bcc-Ta up to 174 GPa. Pressures were determined using an internal Au calibrant with the Heinz & Jeanloz (1984) 3rd-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS (V0 = 10.215 cm^3/mol, K0T = 166.6 GPa, K' = 5.5). Two DAC configurations were used: 300-μm flats for pressures to 50 GPa and beveled anvils (300→100 μm, 7°) for higher pressures. Pressure uncertainties were 0.7–2% using ruby and ~1.5% from the Au EOS. The measured lattice parameters show no evidence of structural transitions, confirming that bcc Ta is stable to 174 GPa. The derived EOS parameters (K0T = 194.7±4.8 GPa, K' = 3.4±0.1) agree with ultrasonic B0S measurements converted to isothermal conditions (189–195 GPa). Earlier XRD results (Xu et al.) overestimated K0T due to a rapidly softening EOS fit.

This EOS is widely used as a high-pressure standard. The relatively low K' (compared to Au) means Ta compresses more uniformly with pressure and provides more stable pressure determination above 100 GPa. All EOS parameters correspond to isothermal values at ambient temperature.

nodes.label: Datchi and Canny 2004_cBN_M1
nodes.display_label: Datchi and Canny 2004 (M1) | cBN | Raman | RamanDatchi04 | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Datchi et al., 1997 | Max Pressure: 21 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.value: DAC (Raman spectroscopy at simultaneous high P–T)

Raman measurements performed on a single c-BN crystal compressed
in neon. Pressure measured in situ using ruby and SrB4O7:Sm2+
fluorescence; explicit wavelength–pressure equations are given
in the text (Eqs. 1–2). The TO-mode frequency shift is shown to be
a coupled function of pressure and temperature.

Bulk modulus and its pressure derivative are derived quantities,
obtained by inverting a first-order Murnaghan EOS using a literature
mode Grüneisen parameter; they are not independently calibrated EOS
parameters.

This study provides the first experimental Raman calibration of c-BN
at simultaneous high pressure and temperature (to 21 GPa, 723 K).
The preferred pressure-reading formulation is given by inversion of
Eq. (8) (model 2), yielding pressure uncertainties of ~0.15 GPa
within the calibrated range.

The Raman-based pressure scale is intended for high-T DAC experiments
where ruby becomes unreliable; extrapolation beyond the measured P–T
range is discouraged.

nodes.label: Datchi et al., 1997_SrB4O7
nodes.display_label: Datchi et al., 1997 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Datchi97_Equation | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 124 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value:
Overall, uncertainties are mainly governed by the ruby primary scale 
(~3% at 100 GPa) and gold EOS (~1–2%), while spectroscopic precision is high. 
Datchi et al. (1997) show that the SrB₄O₇:Sm²⁺ (0–0) fluorescence line has 
extremely low temperature sensitivity, with negligible shift up to 500 K and 
only a very small nonlinear shift (~0.06 nm) up to 900 K—far smaller than 
ruby’s temperature dependence. Thermal broadening remains limited, though 
signal intensity decreases significantly above 700 K, restricting practical 
use to about 900 K. The study also provides an updated calibration of ruby’s 
R₁ temperature shift, linear up to 600 K and nonlinear at higher temperatures.
The authors propose an improved in situ pressure–temperature metrology method
for diamond anvil cells. Overall accuracy is mainly limited by the ruby 
pressure scale and gold equation of state.

Additonal experiment was done in ICE PTM, no equation was derived, this experiment was mixed with Au and Al2O3, XRD was collected at the ESRF. They show good agreement Fig 3. Datchi et al. (1997) recalibrated the SrB₄O₇:Sm²⁺ (0–0) fluorescence line as an optical pressure gauge up to 124 GPa in helium and 130 GPa in ice, with temperature calibration up to 900 K. The study demonstrates strong data quality, based on 58 well-reproduced pressure measurements with minimal scatter. The pressure-induced wavelength shift is nonlinear and accurately described by a quadratic fit, while an alternative Padé-type equation reproduces pressures with a mean deviation of only 0.1 GPa. Validation against the gold equation of state shows excellent agreement (within 1.4% in helium and 2.6% in ice), confirming calibration reliability. Unlike ruby, the borate 0–0 line remains sharp, intense, and well isolated even at very high pressures, with no luminescence quenching observed up to 130 GPa, making it a more robust and accurate high-pressure sensor.

nodes.label: Decker 1971_NaCl
nodes.display_label: Decker 1971 | NaCl-B1 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 30 GPa | PTM: N/A

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pressure is calculated using a Mie–Gruneisen–Debye EOS constrained
by experimentally determined elastic constants, thermal expansion,
Debye temperature, and Gruneisen parameter. The paper provides
tabulated pressure values as a function of compression and
temperature; no closed-form pressure–volume equation is proposed.

Estimated pressure uncertainty is stated to increase with pressure,
from approximately 1.1% below 50 kbar to ~2.4% below 200 kbar.

Maoie–Gruneisen–Debye EOS is not supported in EoSAlign; so we have refit using BM for direct comparison.

This study presents a calculated P–V–T equation of state for NaCl.
All pressure values are derived from the model described in the text
and tables. The EOS is intended for interpolation within the tabulated
pressure–volume–temperature range and should not be extrapolated
beyond the limits discussed by the author.

nodes.label: Dewaele 2019_NaCl-B1
nodes.display_label: Dewaele 2019 | NaCl-B1 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 27 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 400 um to 100 x 300 (central flat x bevel)

B1-phase stable to ~27–36 GPa depending on run.
Compression quasi-hydrostatic due to He medium.
Relative volume precision ~5×10⁻⁴.

EOS parameters correspond to the Dorogokupets (2007) updated ruby scale,
which is the unified metrology used throughout the paper (Table 1).
Transition to B2 occurs near 30 GPa; this YAML covers only the B1 regime.

EOS parameters correspond to the unified ruby pressure metrology adopted by Dewaele (2019).
Data were collected in He, giving nearly hydrostatic conditions even to Mbar pressures.

nodes.label: Dewaele and Torrent 2013
nodes.display_label: Dewaele and Torrent, 2013 | Al2O3 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 165 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Ruby remains in corundum structure metastably to 165 GPa with no evidence of amorphization or irreversible changes. Peak broadening above ~80 GPa attributed to nonhydrostaticity. Luminescence shifts on decompression are minor.

Experimental Rydberg-Vinet fit parameters were obtained from three runs using
He PTM. Fit values match well with ultrasonic bulk modulus (253 ± 2 GPa).
Pressure calibration relied on ruby luminescence. Authors caution against
extrapolating beyond 165 GPa.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2000_MgO
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2000 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 53 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: ESRF ID30 (European Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Grenoble)
CO₂ laser heating to 1800–2474 K with spectroradiometric T measurement

Argon PTM produced quasi-hydrostatic conditions. Small nitrogen contamination produced fcc/hcp Ar + δ-N₂ phases.
Pressure uncertainties from Pt EOS: ΔP ≈ 0.03·P + 4×10⁻⁴·(T–T0).
Deviatoric stresses estimated ≤ 1 GPa from lattice strain analysis (Singh, 1993 method).

The ambient-temperature EOS is a third-order Birch–Murnaghan relation with fixed K0 and V0
and fitted K0′ = 3.94 ± 0.06. This value incorporates data from this study as well as
Fei (1999), Fiquet et al. (1996), and Utsumi et al. (1998), excluding Mao & Bell (1979).
High-temperature modeling includes both:
  • T-dependent Birch–Murnaghan EOS
  • Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model with Θ0 ≈ 800 K, γ0 ≈ 1.45, q ≈ 0.8 ± 0.5
Study provides 61 P–V–T points up to 53 GPa and 2500 K, enabling full thermodynamic parameterization.
Demonstrates MgO behaves as a Debye-like solid up to ~2500 K and >50 GPa.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2004_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 153 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value:
The ruby luminescence scale of Mao et al. (1986) was tested 
against XRD equations of state of six metals (Al, Cu, Ta, W, Pt, Au) 
measured up to 153 GPa in quasihydrostatic He PTM at ESRF.
It was concluded that the classical ruby scale underestimates 
pressure above 100 GPa by up to ~10 GPa. 
A revised calibration was proposed, keeping the same functional form 
but with B increased to 9.5. Agreement is within ±3 GPa at 150 GPa.

This calibration is based on static XRD EOS of six metals measured 
under quasihydrostatic conditions. 
The revised ruby scale (Eq. 1 in the paper) should be used instead of 
the classical Mao et al. (1986) scale for pressures above ~30 GPa. 
Below ~55 GPa, the two scales differ by <2%.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Ag
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 122 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom and pressure uncertainties increase from ~0.05 GPa at low pressure to ~2 GPa near 150 GPa (systematic). Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron methods show that GGA-PBE overestimates equilibrium volume, underestimates K₀, and overestimates K₀′, becoming too stiff at high compression, while LDA shows the opposite trend; overall, the Ag dataset is high quality below 100 GPa and well suited for pressure-scale calibration and DFT benchmarking.
Table IV Vinet fit (DAC PR′). V0 fixed from low-P data; K0 and K0′ are fit parameters.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008 | Max Pressure: 156 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value:
Data are obtained under near-hydrostatic conditions using helium as a
pressure medium, with explicit identification and exclusion of data affected by sample
bridging and nonhydrostatic stress (e.g., Ag and Mo above ~100 GPa). Pressure calibration
relies on updated ruby fluorescence scales and is cross-validated against ultrasonic and
reduced shock-wave EoS, reducing systematic uncertainty to ~2–3 GPa at ~150 GPa. Absolute
pressures at the highest compressions remain model-dependent through the choice of RSW
datasets and ruby calibration, but internal consistency across metals is high.

This calibration is based on static XRD EOS of four metals measured 
under quasihydrostatic conditions. 
The revised ruby scale  should be used instead of 
the classical Mao et al. (1986) scale

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Co
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Co-hcp | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 63 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom, and pressure uncertainties increase with compression (systematic ruby-scale component). Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron results show strong internal consistency; however, GGA-PBE slightly overestimates equilibrium volume and overestimates K₀′, leading to a compression curve that becomes too stiff at high pressure, while LDA underestimates V₀ and overestimates K₀. Overall, the Co-hcp dataset is high quality across the measured range and provides a robust benchmark for pressure calibration and DFT validation.
Table IV Vinet fit (DAC PR′). V0 fixed from low-P data; K0 and K0′ are fit parameters.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Fe-bcc
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Fe-bcc | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 15 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom, and pressure uncertainties increase with compression due to systematic ruby-scale effects. Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron methods show strong methodological consistency; however, GGA-PBE slightly overestimates equilibrium volume and K₀′ and becomes too stiff at high compression, while LDA underestimates V₀ and overestimates K₀. Magnetic effects (ferromagnetic ordering in α-Fe) are important for accurate theoretical modeling. Overall, the α-Fe dataset is high quality within the bcc stability field and serves as a key benchmark for pressure calibration and DFT validation.
Table IV Vinet fit (DAC PR′). V0 fixed from low-P data; K0 and K0′ are fit parameters.

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 0 K cold isotherm parameters extracted via Mie-Grüneisen-Debye decomposition: P(V,T)=P_cold(V,0K)+P_thermal(V,T). The H02 form performs poorly for Ne even as a cold isotherm: χ²=13.3 vs 8.9 for Vinet (Table II). When applied without the Debye thermal correction at 298 K, gives ~29 GPa RMS error. The H02 functional form is fundamentally inadequate for soft noble gases at strong compression — see Holzapfel 1991 which states Ne requires at minimum a third-order expansion. Do not use for pressure determination; use the Vinet entry from this paper instead.
Parameters from Table II (third row, H02). Fixed quantities during fitting: V0=22.234 A^3/atom; θ0, γ0 parameters of the MGD model (see text). Pressure range to 208.8 GPa at 298 K.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Ne_vinet
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Ne | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holzapfel 2005; Datchi et al., 1997; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 208.8 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 0 K cold isotherm parameters extracted via Mie-Grüneisen-Debye decomposition: P(V,T)=P_cold(V,0K)+P_thermal(V,T). To reproduce 298 K pressures exactly the Debye thermal pressure must be added (Θ0=75.1 K, γ1=2.442, q=0.97). In practice the Vinet 0K parameters give ~1.2 GPa RMS error when used standalone at 298 K because the thermal pressure (≈2-4 GPa) is approximately constant with compression at high pressure. Use with awareness that this is an approximation.
Parameters from Table II (first row, Vinet). Fixed quantities during fitting: V0=22.234 A^3/atom; θ0, γ0 parameters of the MGD model (see text). Pressure range to 208.8 GPa at 298 K.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2008_Ni
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2008 | Ni | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 156 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom, while pressure uncertainties increase with compression due to systematic ruby-scale effects. Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron methods show excellent internal consistency; however, GGA-PBE slightly overestimates equilibrium volume and K₀′ and underestimates K₀ at ambient pressure, leading to a compression curve that becomes too stiff at high pressure, whereas LDA underestimates V₀ and overestimates K₀. Overall, the Ni dataset is high quality across the measured range and serves as a strong benchmark for pressure calibration and DFT validation.
Table IV Vinet fit (DAC PR′). V0 fixed from low-P data; K0 and K0′ are fit parameters.

nodes.label: Dewaele et al., 2021_Ar
nodes.display_label: Dewaele et al., 2021 | Ar | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Takemura and Dewaele 2008; Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 114 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: none (ambient temperature); cryogenic run at 10 K for MGD testing

The EOS fit uses quasi-hydrostatic data from Ar crystals embedded in ArNe2 (runs 2–3)  combined with pure Ar below 5 GPa. Unit-cell volumes extracted via Rietveld refinement  (runs 1–2) and peak fitting (runs 3–5). Non-hydrostatic stress evaluated by gamma-plots  from Au lines (Fig. 6). Nonhydrostatic effects are largest below 20 GPa and minimal above  ~70 GPa. hcp phase appears at pressures as low as 4.2–13 GPa, but remains <4 vol% up to 114 GPa.  EOS parameters validated using low-T isobars in run 5.  # See Figs. 7–10.

Argon EOS was determined using angle-dispersive synchrotron XRD on single crystals and 
powders, in pure Ar and Ar–Ne mixtures. Rydberg–Vinet parameters (V0 = 38.0 Å^3/at, 
K0 = 2.65 GPa, K0' = 7.423) correspond to the static 0 K part of the equation of state, 
with thermal pressure added through a quasi-harmonic Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model:
    P(V,T) = P0(V) + Pth(V,T).
The thermal model uses:
    γ(V) = 2.20·(V/V0) + 0.5        # eq. (4)
    θD(V) = 93.3 * (1/x) * exp[2.20(1 - x)],  x = V/V0  # eq. (5)
These parameters reproduce thermal expansion and P–V data from 1–114 GPa at 296 K and 
10–296 K in cryogenic runs. hcp-Ar appears through stacking faults but remains a minor 
phase (<4%). fcc → hcp transition is stress-assisted and not thermodynamically complete 
up to at least 114 GPa.

This EOS can be used as a metrological pressure standard for experiments using Ar as a 
pressure medium at ambient or elevated temperature, as the quasi-harmonic model captures 
thermal pressure reliably up to high temperatures.

nodes.label: Ding et al., 2025_W
nodes.display_label: Ding et al., 2025 | W | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2018; Akahama and Kawamura 2010 | Max Pressure: 527 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: bcc W (110), (200), (211), (220), (310); Au peaks for pressure

W retains the bcc structure up to 527 GPa (Fig. 2). Pressures were determined using the  Au t-DAC pressure scale of Dewaele et al. (2018), validated against Fratanduono et al.  (shockless Au) and diamond Raman in some comparative runs. XRD profiles above 300 GPa  show peak broadening due to nonhydrostatic stresses typical of ultrahigh-pressure t-DAC  experiments. Uniaxial stresses estimated by peak-width method (Supplemental Fig. S4).  EOS comparison with Dewaele (2004) and Dubrovinsky (2012) indicates consistency to 250 GPa.

The W P–V data were fitted using the Rydberg–Vinet (RV) equation of state:
   P(V) = 3K0 * (1 - x) / x^2 * exp[ (3/2)(K0' - 1)(1 - x) ]
   where x = (V/V0)^(1/3).
For their global ultrahigh-pressure RV fit:
   V0 was fixed to 31.716 Å^3,
   K0 fixed to 298.6 GPa,
   K0' = 4.103 ± 0.029.
Data extend to 527(7) GPa in run 1 using a Boehler–Almax mt-DAC. W remains bcc throughout 
compression. Authors note excellent agreement with quasihydrostatic W EOS below 200 GPa 
(Dewaele 2004; Dewaele 2019). High-pressure deviations relative to shock Hugoniot EOS 
expected due to temperature and strength effects.
This EOS should be classified as a static, ultrahigh-pressure RV scale dependent on an Au 
reference scale measured in the same toroidal DAC configuration.

nodes.label: Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007_Au
nodes.display_label: Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Takemura 2001; Dewaele et al., 2004; Chijioke et al., 2005 | Max Pressure: 200 GPa | PTM: not applicable (model-based compilation)

rows.value:
This EOS is not a simple experimental fit. It is derived from a global thermodynamic
model (EMGD) constrained by shock-wave, XRD, thermochemical, and elastic data.
The cold curve is approximated using a Vinet-type formulation, but full pressure
requires thermal contributions (Grüneisen, anharmonic, electronic).
Reported parameters represent an internally consistent pressure scale.

- The Au EOS is constructed as part of a multi-calibrant internally consistent framework.
- K0 = 167 GPa and K0' ≈ 5.9 represent the preferred average of recent quasihydrostatic data.
- The formulation includes intrinsic anharmonicity and electronic contributions,
  which are required for accurate high-temperature behavior.
- Differences of up to ~4 GPa can occur relative to other scales at high P–T.
- Not directly equivalent to a standalone BM3 or Vinet fit used in static DAC studies.

nodes.label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2003_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2003 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2003; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2003 | Max Pressure: 70 GPa | PTM: Not Applicaple

rows.value: Equations of State of Cu and Ag and Revised Ruby Pressure Scale

doi_lines.text: Equations of State of Cu and Ag and Revised Ruby Pressure Scale

rows.value:
This is a ruby pressure scale based on shock-wave data and the Mao et al. (1986) ruby scale. The authors fit a power law to the shock-wave data, anchored to the Mao et al. (1986) scale at low pressure, to obtain the following equation:
    P (GPa) = A * [ (1 + Δλ/λ0)^B – 1 ]
    A = 1871 GPa, B = 10.06
    Where Cu and Ag are refit from Carter et al., 1971|

nodes.label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007_Ag
nodes.display_label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Khishchenko et al., 2001 | Max Pressure: 200 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value:
Abstract: "We have constructed semiempirical equations of state of Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, and W, which within experimental
error bars describe the available shock-wave, ultrasonic, x-ray, and thermochemical data in the temperature
range from 10–20 K up to the melting temperature and to compression x=V/V0=0.5–0.7. The
comparison of the calculated room-temperature isotherms for these metals with quasihydrostatic measurements
supports recently proposed ruby pressure scales. We recommend a new ruby pressure scale in the form ...Polynomial... with parameters A=1884 GPa and m=5.5. The cross check on independent data
confirms the obtained PVT equations of state of Ag, Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, W, MgO, and diamond. The equations
of state of these materials obtained here provide accurate and versatile means for calibrating pressure at all
temperatures below the melting point. Furthermore, they can be used for accurate tabulation of thermodynamic
properties (heat capacities, entropies) of these reference substances in a wide P-T range.

nodes.label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Polynomial | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Occelli et al., 2003; Zha et al., 2000; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 160 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value:
Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007) constructed semiempirical EOSs for several
metals and MgO from shock-wave, ultrasonic, x-ray, and thermochemical data.
These were used to cross-check and refine ruby pressure scales. The final 
recommended ruby scale was expressed in the form:
  P = 1884 * (Δλ/λ0) * [1 + 5.5*(Δλ/λ0)].
This scale agrees with recalibrated metal EOS up to ~160 GPa and provides a 
single consistent description up to ~300 GPa. Deviations are typically ≤1–2 GPa
compared to independent isotherm measurements.

Recommended ruby scale: P = 1884(Δλ/λ0)(1 + 5.5 Δλ/λ0), valid up to ~300 GPa. 
Authors emphasize improved internal consistency with Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, W, MgO,
and diamond EOS compared to earlier calibrations.

nodes.label: Dubrovinsky et al., 1998_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Dubrovinsky et al., 1998 | Al2O3 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 62 GPa | PTM: NaCl; Al2O3; MgO

rows.value: internally heated Mao–Bell and Merrill–Bassett DACs
BNL X17C beamline (synchrotron) and rotating anode Mo Kα
internal resistive wire heating up to 1700 K

EOS fits based only on data collected above 900 K to minimize deviatoric stress.
Four EOS models tested; Model II (middle values) recommended.

Thermal EOS parameters for corundum from in situ P–V–T data to 62 GPa and 1700 K.
Model II (Table 1) gives K0=255.9 GPa, K0′=4.92, V0=25.59 cm^3/mol, dK/dT = -0.021 GPa/K.
Corundum stable to 62 GPa; used as pressure medium and standard.

nodes.label: Dubrovinsky et al., 1998_Fe-hcp
nodes.display_label: Dubrovinsky et al., 1998 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 54 GPa | PTM: NaCl; MgO; Al2O3

rows.value: internally heated Mao–Bell and Merrill–Bassett DACs
BNL X17C beamline (synchrotron) and rotating anode Mo Kα
internal resistive wire heating up to 1700 K

EOS fits based only on data collected above 900 K to minimize deviatoric stress.
Four EOS models tested; Model II (middle values) recommended.

Thermal EOS parameters for ε-Fe from in situ P–V–T data to 54 GPa and 1700 K.
Model II (Table 2) gives K0=162.1 GPa, K0′=5.47, V0=6.75 cm^3/mol, dK/dT = -0.044 GPa/K.
ε–γ transition observed up to 42 GPa and 1560 K.

nodes.label: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012_Re_BM3
nodes.display_label: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012 | Re | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 165 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
BM3 EOS fitted to Re powder compressed in a He pressure medium
in a conventional DAC (Experiment no. 3). Pressures were determined
using the Au EOS of Yokoo et al. (2009). The BM3 parameters are:
  K300 = 353(3) GPa, K0' = 5.80(7), V0 = 8.87(1) cm^3/mol
Extrapolation of this BM3 EOS to 400–640 GPa underestimates pressure
by ~10% and fails to match the ultra-high-pressure data.

This entry represents the third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS for hcp-Re
derived from data up to 165 GPa in a conventional DAC (Experiment no. 3).
It is not adequate for extrapolation into the 0.5–0.6 TPa regime but is
appropriate for lower-pressure applications.

nodes.label: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012_Re_BM4
nodes.display_label: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012 | Re | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 640 GPa | PTM: Paraffin; Ne; He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Double-stage DAC with NCD semi-ball secondary anvils + conventional DAC

Fourth-order Birch–Murnaghan (BM4) EOS fitted to the combined data set:
  • Experiment no. 1: Re + Au mixture between NCD semi-ball secondary anvils,
    paraffin pressure medium; pressures 416–640 GPa from Au EOS.
  • Experiment no. 2: Pure Re between NCD semi-balls, Ne pressure medium;
    pressures 441–608 GPa determined from the Re EOS of Experiment no. 1.
  • Experiment no. 3: Re powder in He in a conventional DAC, 0–165 GPa.
The global BM4 fit yields:
  K300 = 342(6) GPa
  K0' = 6.15(15)
  K0'' = -0.029(4) GPa^-1
  V0 = 8.87(1) cm^3/mol = 29.46(1) Å^3 per unit cell
The authors show that BM3 and Vinet EOS fitted only to ≤165 GPa data
cannot reproduce the 400–640 GPa measurements, while this BM4 EOS does.

This is the recommended ultra-high-pressure EOS for hcp-Re from Dubrovinsky
et al. (2012), valid from ambient pressure to ~640 GPa.
It combines conventional DAC and double-stage DAC data and explicitly
includes the second pressure derivative of the bulk modulus, K0''.
This BM4 EOS resolves discrepancies among previous Re EOS determinations
and demonstrates that static data in the ~0.5–0.6 TPa range are essential
for such a low-compressibility material.

nodes.label: Duffy et al., 1995 MgO
nodes.display_label: Duffy et al., 1995 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Carter et al., 1971 | Max Pressure: 227 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Energy-dispersive synchrotron XRD measurements of MgO were
conducted to ultrahigh pressures without a pressure-transmitting
medium. Strong nonhydrostatic stresses were present and explicitly
quantified via lattice-strain analysis using differences between
the (200) and (220) reflections.

Pressure was determined from molybdenum diffraction lines using
an isotherm derived from shock compression data. MgO remained
in the B1 (NaCl) structure to the maximum pressure investigated.

A third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS fit to the present static
compression data yields K0 = 177(4) GPa and K0' = 4.0(1),
which is significantly stiffer than ultrasonic and quasihydrostatic
results due to nonhydrostatic stress.

The authors demonstrate that when shear strength (≥11 GPa at
227 GPa) is taken into account, a single EOS can reconcile
static, ultrasonic, and shock data. However, the parameters
reported here correspond strictly to the static DAC dataset
without stress correction.

This EOS should therefore NOT be treated as a hydrostatic
reference; it is most appropriate for studies explicitly
considering strength effects at multimegabar pressures.

nodes.label: Eremets et al., 2023
nodes.display_label: Eremets et al., 2023 | Diamond | Raman | Eremets_Equation | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 477 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: They use one expression P=A*(V-Vo)/V0+B((V-v0)/vo))^2. Compared to the Akahama & Kawamura (2010) scale, the new calibration gives systematically lower pressures above ~250 GPa, with differences reaching ~20% at ~0.5 TPa.

nodes.label: Errandonea et al., 2018_Zn
nodes.display_label: Errandonea et al., 2018 | Zn | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Syassen 2008 | Max Pressure: 16 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: ALBA MSPD-BL04; Diamond Light Source I15
none for EOS; HT used for melting studies only

Room-temperature EOS obtained from P–V data up to 16 GPa.
Zn remains hcp throughout this pressure range but shows a kink in c/a near 10 GPa,
marking an isomorphic transition between hcp and hcp′. No volume discontinuity is observed,
so all RT data included in the EOS fit. EOS agrees with earlier studies (Kenichi 1999; Schulte & Holzapfel 1996).
Thermal expansion determined separately: α = 95×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹.

This YAML describes only the 300 K hcp-Zn isothermal equation of state.
The study also reports a melting curve to 135 GPa using a Simon–Glatzel relation
and a P–V–T EOS, but these parameters are not included here.

nodes.label: Evans et al., 2005_Be_Raman
nodes.display_label: Evans et al., 2005 | Be | Raman | Raman_Polynomial | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value:
Raman peak positions obtained with 0.24 cm^-1 instrumental resolution. Above 60 GPa,
peak intensity decreases and uncertainty increases (~1 cm^-1). Ruby scale accuracy ±0.5 GPa.
Raman and pressure collected at same spot to avoid pressure gradients.

The Raman E2g mode provides a proxy for shear modulus C44. Combined with the EOS,
the authors compute C44(P), finding C44 ≈ 110.6 + 1.784 P – 0.0028 P^2 (GPa).
No structural transitions observed to 80 GPa.

nodes.label: Evans et al., 2005_Be_XRD
nodes.display_label: Evans et al., 2005 | Be | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 182 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
XRD and Au calibrant measured simultaneously at the same spot to minimize pressure gradients.
He pressure medium produced highly hydrostatic conditions, improving EOS accuracy vs earlier studies.
EOS accuracy dominated by c-axis uncertainties (0.1%).

The EOS is noticeably stiffer than earlier non-hydrostatic studies and agrees with dynamic
shock-derived curves. No phase transitions observed to 182 GPa. The increasing c/a ratio
toward 1.60 at 180 GPa suggests more isotropic bonding at high P.

nodes.label: Fedotenko et al., 2020_BM_Pd
nodes.display_label: Fedotenko et al., 2020 | Pd | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fei et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 31.2 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pure Pd metal was compressed in neon and measured by powder XRD
up to ~31 GPa to establish reference compressibility data for
comparison with Pd carbides and hydrides synthesized at HP–HT.
No structural phase transitions were observed over the pressure
range investigated.

EOS parameters are derived from a third-order Birch–Murnaghan fit
to Pd P–V data collected during compression in Ne (DAC1, Table 1;
Fig. 1). The fitted parameters are:
V0 = 58.868(2) Å^3, K0 = 157(3) GPa, K0' = 9.9(4).

The authors note that the unusually high K0' value is atypical for
transition metals and may reflect the limited pressure range
(≤31 GPa). When K0' is fixed to the value reported by Mao et al.
(1978), a higher K0 (~190 GPa) is obtained.

This Pd EOS is intended as an internal reference within the study
and should not be treated as a high-pressure primary calibration.

rows.value:
Pure Pd metal was compressed in neon and measured by powder XRD
up to ~31 GPa to establish reference compressibility data for
comparison with Pd carbides and hydrides synthesized at HP–HT.
No structural phase transitions were observed over the pressure
range investigated.

EOS parameters are derived from a third-order Birch–Murnaghan fit
to Pd P–V data collected during compression in Ne (DAC1, Table 1;
Fig. 1). The fitted parameters are:
V0 = 58.868(2) Å^3, K0 = 157(3) GPa, K0' = 9.9(4).

The authors note that the unusually high K0' value is atypical for
transition metals and may reflect the limited pressure range
(≤31 GPa). When K0' is fixed to the value reported by Mao et al.
(1978), a higher K0 (~190 GPa) is obtained.

This Pd EOS is intended as an internal reference within the study
and should not be treated as a high-pressure primary calibration.

nodes.label: Fei et al., 2016_Fe
nodes.display_label: Fei et al., 2016 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fei et al., 2007; Fei et al., 2007; Fei et al., 2007; Tange et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 280 GPa | PTM: Ne; MgO; NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: room-temperature compression for EOS; double-sided laser heating in Ne medium up to 1800 K for thermal EOS (Fe and Pt heated separately)

New hcp-Fe compression data at 300 K were collected in Ne (up to 100 GPa) and MgO (up to 205 GPa) pressure media, using Ne, NaCl-B2, Pt, and MgO as pressure markers on internally consistent scales (Fei et al., 2007; Tange et al., 2009). When pressures for previous studies (Mao et al., 1990; Dubrovinsky et al., 2000; Yamazaki et al., 2012; Sakai et al., 2014) are recalculated onto these intercalibrated scales, all room-temperature hcp-Fe compression data agree within ~2% in pressure up to 280 GPa (Figures 1–2). Publised data was fit with this study

Room-temperature EOS:
  • All 300 K hcp-Fe data (this study + Dewaele et al. 2006 + recalibrated Mao 1990, Dubrovinsky 2000,
    Yamazaki 2012, Sakai 2014) were fitted to a 3rd-order Birch–Murnaghan equation of state:
        P_300K = (3/2) K0 [ (ρ/ρ0)^(7/3) - (ρ/ρ0)^(5/3) ] *
                  { 1 + (3/4)(K0' - 4)[ (ρ/ρ0)^(2/3) - 1 ] }.
    With fixed initial density ρ0 = 8.2695 g/cm^3, the preferred fit yields:
        K0 = 172.7 ± 1.4 GPa, K0' = 4.79 ± 0.05. 
  • If ρ0 is left free, the alternative fit gives K0 = 191.44 ± 5.3 GPa, K0' = 4.52 ± 0.08, ρ0 = 8.3602 ± 0.0241 g/cm^3,
    but Fei et al. (2016) favor the fixed-ρ0 solution to remain consistent with prior density measurements and
    to minimize tradeoff between K0 and ρ0.
  • Residuals of all included data sets relative to the preferred BM3 fit are generally within ±2% in pressure
    up to ~280 GPa (Figure 2).
Experimental setup:
  • Ne PTM runs (c112, c116, c118) used 100 µm beveled culets, Re gaskets preindented to 18 µm with 60 µm
    sample chambers; Ne, NaCl-B2, and Pt were co-loaded as pressure markers. 
  • Higher-pressure MgO runs (c114, c117) used FIB-milled stepped anvils with 20 µm top faces and MgO
    discs plus cBN inserts, achieving pressures to 205 GPa.
  • XRD data were collected with a MAR CCD detector at λ = 0.3344 Å; unit-cell parameters for Fe and
    pressure standards were obtained via Fit2D processing and indexing. 
Thermal EOS:
  • Thermal pressures P_th = P - P_300K were derived from:
      – Static P–V–T data for Fe in Ne + Pt up to 100 GPa and 1800 K, and
      – Shock Hugoniot data for Fe up to ~200 GPa and 5000 K (Brown et al., 2000), referenced to the 300 K
        BM3 isotherm of this study.
  • The thermal pressure model combines quasi-harmonic Debye vibrational contributions with an empirical
    anharmonic + electronic term:
        P - P_300K = (γ_vib/V) ∫_300^T C_V,vib dT + (γ_e/V) ∫_300^T C_V,e dT,
    where γ_vib = γ0 (ρ0/ρ)^q with γ0 = 1.74, q = 0.78; C_V,e = β0 (ρ0/ρ)^k T with β0 = 0.07 J kg^-1 K^-2,
    k = 1.34; θ0 = 422 K; γ_e = 2. 
  • The resulting thermal EOS reproduces shock, static high-T XRD, and phonon DOS–derived thermal pressures
    for hcp-Fe and is used by Fei et al. (2016) to compute inner-core density and density deficit (~3.6% at
    the ICB for T_ICB = 6000 K).

rows.value: Argon crystallizes into fcc at 11.5 ± 0.5 kbar and remains fcc to 82 kbar. Sharp single-crystal diffraction maxima were obtained up to 80 kbar (minor spot diffuseness above ~80 kbar). Unit-cell volumes are accurate to ±0.02% (diffractometer) or ±0.05% (film). EOS fitted with fixed V0 and K0 derived from low-temperature measurements.

The EOS follows the exact "second-order Murnaghan" expression of Finger et al., incorporating static K0, K0', Debye-derived Pz0, and the derived q parameter. No phase transitions were observed; argon remains fcc to 82 kbar.

EOS parameters reflect the zero-Kelvin static part after correcting experimental, pressures for thermal and zero-point effects using the Debye model. Volume ratio is referenced to V0 from low-temperature experiments, not measured 293 K values. Pressure equation used is the "second-order Murnaghan" of Finger et al. (1981).

nodes.label: Fiquet et al., 1996_MgO
nodes.display_label: Fiquet et al., 1996 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 16 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: CO2 laser (used for stress relaxation only) this yaml only reports unheated EOS, if heated for stress relation change K0 to 177+-2 GPa

Room-temperature compression data for MgO were collected
before and after laser-heating cycles. Laser heating was
used to reduce deviatoric stress; EOS parameters reported
here are derived from room-temperature data only.

Pressures were determined primarily from the equation of
state of solid argon, which the authors argue better
represents the pressure at the sample position than ruby
fluorescence measured at the gasket edge.

A third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS was fit to the 300 K
compression data. When K0' is fixed at 4.5 (from Mao &
Bell, 1979), the authors obtain K0 = 164(2) GPa using
argon pressures, in good agreement with ultrasonic
measurements (Jackson & Niesler, 1982; Isaak et al., 1989).

Free fits yield unrealistically low K0' (~1.5), which the
authors attribute to the limited pressure range (≤16 GPa).

This EOS should be treated as a quasi-hydrostatic,
low-pressure MgO reference suitable for comparison with
ultrasonic and moderate-pressure DAC datasets.

Corrections for deviatoric stress, plastic work, and weak-shock heating are included.Final Au isotherm shows ~5% relative standard deviation at 800 GPa.

nodes.label: Frost et al., 2023_Pd_BM
nodes.display_label: Frost et al., 2023 | Pd | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dorfman et al., 2012 | Max Pressure: 182 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Palladium was statically compressed in neon with all sources of
hydrogen rigorously excluded to avoid hydride formation. Three
independent DAC runs are reported and show mutually consistent
P–V behavior. Palladium remains fcc to the maximum pressure
investigated (182 GPa), with no phase transitions observed.

EOS parameters are taken from Table I (BM3 fit, “This work”).
The authors also report a Vinet fit and discuss functional-form
dependence when extrapolating to high pressure.

Pressure was determined primarily using a tungsten pressure
marker (Dewaele et al., 2004); the highest-pressure run used
gold (Dorfman et al., 2012). The motivation of the study is to
resolve discrepancies in prior Pd EOS results caused by
inadvertent hydrogen contamination, which can artificially
increase lattice volume and bias fitted compressibility.

nodes.label: Fujihisa and Takemura 1996_Co
nodes.display_label: Fujihisa and Takemura 1996 | Co-hcp | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 79 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: symmetric DACs with 400 μm and 300 μm culets

Starting powder contained both hcp and metastable fcc Co.
fcc phase fully converted to hcp by 24 GPa.
No phase transition observed from hcp up to 79 GPa.
Vinet EOS fitted to normalized volumes (Fig. 3).
c/a ratio decreases continuously under pressure; fitted with a polynomial.
fcc volume found to be ~0.2% larger than hcp at identical pressures.

This EOS is one of the primary experimental references for hcp-Co at 300 K.
Later studies (Yoo 2000; Antonangeli 2008) confirm similar values:
K0 ≈ 199 GPa, K0' ≈ 3.6. V0 not explicitly measured in this paper and is
taken from Yoo 2000

nodes.label: Goncharov et el., 2005_cBN_Raman
nodes.display_label: Goncharov et al., 2005 | cBN | Raman | Raman_Goncharov | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 40 GPa | PTM: Ar; Al2O3

rows.value:
Raman TO-mode frequencies measured to 40 GPa and 1750 K (laser heating),
and to 2300 K at 26 GPa (internal heating). Pressure at 300 K was measured
using ruby fluorescence and assumed constant upon heating; thermal
pressure was estimated to be ≤1.5 GPa.

The pressure scale is constructed by separating quasiharmonic (volume-
dependent) and intrinsic anharmonic contributions to the TO-mode shift.
This is the first experimentally constrained pressure scale valid above
1000 K for c-BN.

Pressure scale given by Eq. (2):
  P = (K(T)/b(T)) * [ (ω/ω0(T))^b(T) - 1 ].

Temperature-dependent parameters:
  K(T) = 324.5 - 6.13×10⁻3 T - 0.94×10⁻6 T²  (GPa)
  ω0(T) = 1060.2 - 0.010 T - 1.40×10⁻5 T²  (cm⁻¹)
  b(T) = 3.47 - 8.61×10⁻5 T - 1.06×10⁻7 T²

This Raman scale is intended for high-temperature use and should not
be extrapolated beyond ~40 GPa without independent EOS constraints.

nodes.label: Goncharov et el., 2007_cBN_Raman
nodes.display_label: Goncharov et al., 2007 | cBN | Raman | Raman_Goncharov | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Goncharov et al., 2007; Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 65 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value:
Raman TO-mode frequencies measured up to ~3300 K and ~65 GPa.
Pressure determination relies on the independently measured
XRD-based thermal EOS from this study. The Raman scale is intended
for high-temperature use where ruby becomes unreliable.

Raman pressure scale constructed by combining measured TO-mode
frequencies with the thermal EOS of c-BN. This scale complements
ruby at extreme temperatures and is internally consistent with
the XRD dataset of Goncharov et al. (2007).

High-temperature XRD measurements used to construct a thermal EOS
of c-BN. Pressures were measured using ruby fluorescence before and

intended for high-T pressure determination rather than ultra-high

Third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS with temperature-dependent elastic

nodes.label: Gorman et al., 2023_Ta
nodes.display_label: Gorman et al., 2023 | Ta | XRD | Vinet-Extended | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 2300 GPa | PTM: none

rows.value: Laser-driven ramp compression with VISAR + Lagrangian analysis

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Quasi-isentropic ramp compression. Corrections applied for deviatoric stress, plastic work heating, and initial shock-state heating using a Mie–Grüneisen framework and strength corrections. Thermal pressure correction between the isentrope and 298 K isotherm reported to be small (~5 GPa at 2 TPa).

Absolute pressure-density isentrope measured to 2.30 TPa and ~53 g/cm^3. Final EOS constrained using ramp compression, shock Hugoniot, DAC, thermal expansion, and DFT-MD data. Reported uncertainty of approximately 2% at 1 TPa. EOS intended for use up to 2.3 TPa and 5000 K.

The publication constructs a full thermodynamic Helmholtz free-energy EOS
including cold, ionic, and electronic contributions, then fits resulting
isotherms to the extended Vinet form for convenient application.
