rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
The BE2 equation (second-order Birch form) provides a reasonable empirical fit to
the measured P–V data of Al under compression, but begins to deviate above ~50 GPa.

The authors report that the BE2 equation can fit the static compression data with
K0 = 72.7 GPa and K0' = 4.14. However, they emphasize that K0' differs greatly
from the thermodynamic value and therefore the BE2 form is physically inconsistent.
For this reason, the BE2 EOS is NOT recommended for extrapolation, and the authors
conclude that the Holzapfel H11 “simple solid” EOS provides a significantly better
description of Al to 220 GPa.

nodes.label: Guigue et al., 2020_Pd
nodes.display_label: Guigue et al., 2020 | Pd | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Takemura and Dewaele 2008; Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pure Pd compression was measured in neon to establish a reference
EOS for comparison with PdH and PdD. Volumes were obtained by
Le Bail refinement of fcc Pd (Fm-3m). No phase transition was
observed for Pd up to 100 GPa.

EOS parameters are taken from Table I (Pd in Ne). The reported
Vinet parameters are: V0 = 14.75(4) Å^3, K0 = 162(2) GPa,
K0' = 6.2(9).

Pressure was determined using either a gold pressure marker
(Takemura & Dewaele, 2008) or ruby fluorescence below ~40 GPa.
This Pd EOS serves as an internal reference baseline for quantifying
volume expansion and isotopic effects in PdH and PdD.

nodes.label: Hanfland and Syassen 2002
nodes.display_label: Hanfland and Syassen 2002 | Ta | XRD | H02 | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 69 GPa | PTM: N2; Na

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Angle-dispersive synchrotron XRD was used to measure Ta P–V data up to 69 GPa. Nitrogen (to 35 GPa) and sodium (to 69 GPa) were used as quasihydrostatic media. Diffraction peak widths in Na remained at the instrumental limit, demonstrating extremely hydrostatic conditions. Ta peak widths were slightly larger due to built-in microstrain in the commercial Ta powder.
The P–V data were fit with the Holzapfel (H02) EOS (n = 5), yielding B0 = 207.6 ± 1.5 GPa and B0' = 2.85 ± 0.10. The fitted ambient a0 = 3.3135 Å agrees with the independently measured a0 = 3.3139(3) Å.
The authors showed that the H02 fit describes both their data (0–69 GPa) and the high-pressure Au-calibrated data of Cynn & Yoo (70–174 GPa) within uncertainties, providing a single consistent EOS for Ta across the entire bcc stability range (to 174 GPa).

The H02 formulation was chosen over Birch-Murnaghan because BM3 gives unphysical curvature at small volumes (B' < 4). The parameters reported here reproduce Ta’s compression smoothly across all known experimental datasets and are commonly used when a ruby-calibrated EOS is required. The value V0 = 18.191 Å^3/atom corresponds to a0 = 3.3135 Å for bcc Ta.

nodes.label: Haung et al., 2016_Mo
nodes.display_label: Haung et al., 2016 | Mo | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Sokolova et al., 2013 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Room-temperature compression measured up to 80 GPa using Ne PTM.
The bcc structure was preserved at all pressures up to at least 94 GPa.
EOS fit performed only on cold-compression P–V data (Fig. 2).

This YAML includes only the 300 K Birch-Murnaghan EOS for bcc-Mo determined
from cold-compression data. The study also reports a complete thermal EOS using
both thermodynamic and Mie–Grüneisen–Debye inversion methods, with additional
parameters (γ0, q, αKT, θ0), but those are excluded here by request.
The 300 K EOS values are:
   V0 = 31.22 ± 0.08 Å³ (unit cell),
   K0 = 273 ± 15 GPa,
   K0' = 3.6 ± 0.4.

nodes.label: Heiz and Jeanloz_Au
nodes.display_label: Heinz and Jeanloz 1984 | Au | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 70 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pressures determined by ruby fluorescence; hydrostatic below 10 GPa using 4:1 MeOH–EtOH,
with <10% pressure gradients above 10 GPa. Finite-strain (Eulerian) analysis shows linear
F–f relation and good agreement with ultrasonic moduli. Third-order BM equation of
state is statistically favored; fourth-order terms unjustified. Data accurately reproduce
the Jamieson et al. 300 K isotherm within ~1–2% up to 70 GPa.

Static compression measurements for Au to 70 GPa using a Mao–Bell DAC.
BM3 fit yields K0T = 167 ± 11 GPa and K0T′ = 5.5 ± 0.8.
V0 uses 10.215 cm³/mol from Table III.
This study is the foundational experimental confirmation of the gold 300 K equation
of state used for pressure calibration and is consistent with prior ultrasonic and Hugoniot data.

nodes.label: Hemley et al., 1989_Ne
nodes.display_label: Hemley et al., 1989 | Ne | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; McQueen et al., 1970 | Max Pressure: 110 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Neon crystallizes in the fcc structure at 4.7 GPa and remains fcc to at least 110 GPa (Fig. 1). Unit-cell parameters and molar volumes were determined from energy-dispersive synchrotron XRD. Pressures were determined from ruby fluorescence (primary) and W XRD (secondary) for cross-checking. 
The temperature reduction to 0 K uses a Mie-Grüneisen–Debye model with Debye temperature θ and zero-point + thermal pressure terms P_zp(V) and P_th(V). The 300-K isotherm agrees closely with electronic-structure calculations and is significantly softer than pure pair-potential models.

This paper reports high-pressure X-ray diffraction measurements of solid neon up to 110 GPa at 300 K, showing that neon remains an insulating face-centered cubic (fcc) solid throughout this range while undergoing large compression (down to ~28% of its original volume) . The experimentally determined pressure–volume equation of state agrees well with electronic structure calculations but is not accurately described by traditional two-body (pair) interatomic potentials, which overestimate stiffness at high pressures. Additionally, the study validates and extends the ruby fluorescence pressure scale beyond 100 GPa, contributing to improved high-pressure measurement techniques.

nodes.label: Hirao et al., 2022 Fe-hcp
nodes.display_label: Hirao et al., 2022 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021; Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 354 GPa | PTM: He; None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: multiple hcp-Fe reflections (e.g., 010, 002, 011, 012, 110, 013, 112, 021 at highest P)

hcp-Fe is stable from ~15 GPa to 354 GPa at 298 K. Pressures were determined
mainly from the latest Pt EOS of Fratanduono et al. (2021), with a revised
ruby scale used in low-pressure, quasi-hydrostatic runs. EOS parameters were
obtained by least-squares fitting of P–V data to the Vinet form, varying V0,
K0, and K0'. Nonhydrostatic effects are minimized by small beam size and
evaluated via Pt lattice strains; pressure uncertainty is estimated to be ±1%.

The resulting EOS gives volumes at 365 GPa that are ~2.3% larger than those
from previous hcp-Fe EOSs once placed on the same Pt pressure scale, implying
that inner-core Fe densities are correspondingly lower than previously
inferred. This Fe EOS is referenced to the quasi-absolute Pt scale derived
from shockless dynamic compression.

nodes.label: Hirao et al., 2022 Ni
nodes.display_label: Hirao et al., 2022 | Ni | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021; Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 368 GPa | PTM: MEW

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
fcc-Ni remains stable with no structural transition up to 368 GPa at 298 K.
P–V data from five runs were fit to the Vinet EOS with fixed V0 and refined
K0 and K0'. Nonhydrostatic stress was evaluated via gamma plots for Ni and Pt;
the derived uniaxial stress components (~0.9 GPa at 368 GPa for Ni) are within
the quoted pressure uncertainty, so nonhydrostatic effects are considered
negligible for the EOS determination.

The new Ni EOS yields volumes at 365 GPa ~1.5% larger than those from
extrapolated previous EOSs on older Pt/ruby scales, implying densities ~1.5%
lower at inner core pressures. Combined with the Fe EOS in this study, it
allows direct estimation of Fe–Ni alloy densities at Earth inner-core
pressures without large extrapolation from lower P.

nodes.label: Hirose et al., 2008_Au
nodes.display_label: Hirose et al., 2008 | Au | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Speziale et al., 2001 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Laser annealing (room T run, to ~1300 K for several minutes after pressure increments); high-T runs to 1600–2330 K by Nd:YAG laser, double-sided heating (see notes)

Simultaneous Au and MgO volumes measured from 11 to 140 GPa and 300–2330 K.
Room-T volumes measured after annealing. High-T measurements performed with double-sided laser heating; temperature gradients within XRD area estimated <10%. In high-T run #3, Au and MgO peaks overlapped, increasing uncertainty in volumes at the highest pressure. At 140 GPa, diffraction peaks overlap led to larger scatter in Au volume (see Table 1, Fig. 2–3).

Room-T and high-T equations of state were derived with MgO as the pressure scale (Speziale et al., 2001). See Table 2 for detailed EOS parameters.
- Room-temperature V0 and K0 fixed at 67.85 Å^3 and 167 GPa.
- K0' (K0_prime) fit as 5.58(2).
- High-T EOS parameters determined from DAC data (fit #1) and from combined data (fit #2).
- Culet size was 90 μm in the room-T run; 200 or 300 μm in high-T runs.
- Pressure transmitting medium was NaCl.
- For room-T run, sample was laser-annealed after each compression step (~1300 K).
- For high-T, Nd:YAG laser with double-sided heating, sample held at 1600–2330 K.
- All pressures referenced to the MgO scale of Speziale et al. (2001).
- Uncertainty in pressure at high-T includes contributions from both volume and temperature.
- The high-T EOS (fit #2) is consistent with MgO pressure scale up to 140 GPa and 3000 K.

nodes.label: Holmes et al., 1989_Pt
nodes.display_label: Holmes et al., 1989 | Pt | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 660 GPa | PTM: not applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Pt was shock-compressed (Ta flyer / Ta standard) and the Pt Hugoniot was
characterized by a linear Us–up relation over 32–660 GPa. A first-principles
(LMTO) cold curve plus thermal terms was used to compute the Hugoniot and
a 300 K isotherm; agreement between calculated and measured Hugoniot
qualifies the 300 K isotherm to ~10% in pressure at a given density.

The paper provides an analytic “universal EOS” representation of the
*theoretical* 300 K isotherm in Vinet form.

Universal EOS (Vinet form) used for the 300 K isotherm:
  P300(X) = PT * [(1 - X)/X^2] * exp[η(1 - X)],
  where X = (V/V0)^(1/3), PT = 3*BT, and η = 1.5*(B' - 1).

Holmes et al. (1989) report the equilibrium volume V0 in atomic units
(a.u.^3 per atom), where 1 a.u. is the Bohr radius (a0 = 0.529177 Å).
Conversion to crystallographic units was performed as:

  1 a.u.^3 = (0.529177 Å)^3 = 0.1481847 Å^3

The reported value V0 = 101.9 a.u.^3/atom therefore corresponds to:

  V0 = 15.095 Å^3/atom

Platinum crystallizes in the fcc structure with Z = 4 atoms per
conventional unit cell, so the unit-cell volume used here is:

  V0 = 60.38 Å^3/unit cell

This value is consistent with ambient-pressure lattice parameters
of fcc Pt (a ≈ 3.92 Å) and is used as the reference volume for the
Vinet (universal) 300 K isotherm derived in this study.

nodes.label: Holzapfel 2001_Ag
nodes.display_label: Holzapfel et al., 2001 | Ag | XRD | AP2 | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 500 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Parameters V0, K0, and K0' at TR = 300 K are the primary input values
listed in Table 1 for Ag (based on literature constraints as compiled in this paper).
The AP2 form (Eq. 13) is used for representing isotherms in the framework developed here.

This paper develops a comprehensive and thermodynamically consistent set of equations of state (EOS) for Cu, Ag, and Au over wide pressure (up to ≥500 GPa) and temperature ranges by combining shock-wave data, ultrasonic measurements, and theoretical modeling. Using a refined Mie–Grüneisen framework with a modified pseudo-Debye–Einstein model for thermal effects, the authors resolve longstanding discrepancies between dynamic (shock) and static data by imposing new constraints on the volume dependence of the Grüneisen parameter. They show that commonly used EOS forms (e.g., Birch or Vinet) can lead to inaccuracies at high compression, while their preferred AP2 formulation provides a more reliable representation consistent with both experimental datasets.

listed in Table 1 for Au (based on literature constraints as compiled in this paper).
The AP2 form (Eq. 13) is used for representing isotherms in the framework developed here.

This paper develops a comprehensive and thermodynamically consistent set of **equations of state (EOS)** for Cu, Ag, and Au over wide pressure (up to ≥500 GPa) and temperature ranges by combining shock-wave data, ultrasonic measurements, and theoretical modeling. Using a refined Mie–Grüneisen framework with a modified pseudo-Debye–Einstein model for thermal effects, the authors resolve longstanding discrepancies between dynamic (shock) and static data by imposing new constraints on the volume dependence of the Grüneisen parameter. They show that commonly used EOS forms (e.g., Birch or Vinet) can lead to inaccuracies at high compression, while their preferred AP2 formulation provides a more reliable representation consistent with both experimental datasets.

nodes.label: Holzapfel 2001_Cu
nodes.display_label: Holzapfel et al., 2001 | Cu | XRD | AP2 | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 600 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Parameters V0, K0, and K0' at TR = 300 K are the primary input values
listed in Table 1 for Cu (based on literature constraints as compiled in this paper).
This work provides EOS forms intended for wide P–T ranges using a Mie–Grüneisen
framework; the AP2 form (Eq. 13) is used for isotherm representation.

This paper develops a comprehensive and thermodynamically consistent set of **equations of state (EOS)** for Cu, Ag, and Au over wide pressure (up to ≥500 GPa) and temperature ranges by combining shock-wave data, ultrasonic measurements, and theoretical modeling. Using a refined Mie–Grüneisen framework with a modified pseudo-Debye–Einstein model for thermal effects, the authors resolve longstanding discrepancies between dynamic (shock) and static data by imposing new constraints on the volume dependence of the Grüneisen parameter. They show that commonly used EOS forms (e.g., Birch or Vinet) can lead to inaccuracies at high compression, while their preferred AP2 formulation provides a more reliable representation consistent with both experimental datasets.

nodes.label: Holzapfel 2003_Al203
nodes.display_label: Holzapfel 2003 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | HP02Ruby | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: McSkimin and Andreatcg 1972; Lowrie and Gonas 1964; Simmons and Wang 1971; Sarrao et al. 1994; Zha et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.value:
The study refines the ruby pressure scale by comparing "nearly hydrostatic"
DAC XRD data on diamond (He PTM) and Ta with ultrasonic and shock-wave EOS results,
and by honoring the absolute MgO-based calibration to 55 GPa.
The revised scale lowers A relative to earlier values and significantly increases curvature (B),
while introducing an additional parameter C to capture high-pressure behavior.
Best estimates - A = 1820(30) GPa, B = 14(2), C = 7.3 (reasonable range C = 5–10).

Propose 3 sets of parameters. Another based on Ta and dimaond A=1790, B=17 C=9.5. Thee other for diamond with Kp of 3.4 --> A=1850, B=12, C=6. This Yaml file assume the diamond data with Kp of 3.7. The authers viewed this as the best fit to the data

nodes.label: Holzapfel 2005_Al
nodes.display_label: Holzapfel 2005 | Al | XRD | AP2 | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holzapfel 2005; Holzapfel 1996; Occelli et al., 2003; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; | Max Pressure: Not Applicable GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
The EOS parameters are the AP2-fit values (Vo_fit, Ko_fit, K0'fit) taken from the
upper section of Table 1 in Holzapfel (2005), evaluated after adopting the HP05 ruby
scale with parameters A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, and C = 7.5. These fit values represent
the EOS derived purely from the single-crystal XRD data recalculated using the updated
ruby scale. Literature (Ho96 / DLM04) values for Al (V0_lit = 0.016605 nm^3,
K0_lit = 73 GPa, K0'_lit = 4.3) serve as a reference set against which the fit values
were compared. For Al, Vo_fit and K0_fit differ more noticeably from the literature
values, consistent with the discussion in Holzapfel (2005) about larger uncertainties
for this calibrant.

nodes.label: Holzapfel 2005_Al203
nodes.display_label: Holzapfel 2005 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | HP02Ruby | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Occelli et al., 2003; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; | Max Pressure: Not Applicable GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.value:
The study refines a practical ruby pressure scale by simultaneously fitting high-pressure
single-crystal XRD data on diamond, Cu, Au, W, Al, Pt, and Ta (mostly in He as PTM)
together with ruby R1 luminescence shifts, using AP2 EOS forms for the calibrants.
V0 and K0 are fixed to literature/ultrasonic values, and K0' for each calibrant plus the
ruby parameters A, B, and C are co-fitted.
The modified Freund–Ingalls (HP02-type) form provides a better representation of the
ruby line shift than the older modified Murnaghan form, especially at higher pressures.
The recommended parameters are A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, C = 7.5, with an estimated
uncertainty of ±25 GPa in A; corresponding bounds imply B ≈ 13.0–16.3 and C ≈ 5.5–9.3.

This YAML captures the 2005 refit of the HP02/modified Freund–Ingalls ruby scale using AP2 EOS forms for Cu, Au, diamond, W, Al, Pt, and Ta. The preferred parameters (A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, C = 7.5) are obtained from a correlated fit with V0 and K0 fixed to literature values and K0' and ruby A, B, C treated as free parameters. Holzapfel also reports alternative ruby parameter sets based on different assumptions and datasets (e.g., polynomial fits and other EOS forms), but this file adopts the AP2-based global fit that the author identifies as the most consistent realization of a practical pressure scale in the 1–300 GPa range.

nodes.label: Jacobson et al., 2008_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Jacobsen et al., 2008 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Zha et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 118 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value:
This revised helium-specific ruby scale was constructed so that pressures derived
from ruby fluorescence match those inferred from the primary MgO scale (Zha et al. 2000)
during single-crystal MgO compression in He. The previous ruby scale (Mao et al. 1986)
underestimates pressures by 5–8% above 60–80 GPa in helium environments.

This calibration is valid only for experiments performed in helium as a pressure 
medium. It should not be used for non-He media (Ne, Ar, KCl, MEW, etc.) because the 
correction is specific to the soft stress environment of He in DACs. Jacobsen et al. 
proposed this ruby correction to reconcile He-based MgO measurements with the 
primary MgO scale.

nodes.label: Jacobson et al., 2008_MgO_hydro
nodes.display_label: Jacobsen et al., 2008 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Sun et al., 2005 | Max Pressure: 118 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: hk0 and hkl reflections; ≥8 reflections per frame

Helium medium provides nearly hydrostatic conditions up to ~60 GPa.
At higher pressures, minor deviations arise due to ruby calibration in He,
leading to an anomalously low K′ = 3.74 compared with earlier MgO references.
A revised ruby calibration specific to He was proposed in the paper. Switch to diamond Raman at high pressure

This EOS is derived from three single-crystal MgO + He diffraction runs.
The authors concluded that the low K′ reflects ruby metrology issues in helium,
not intrinsic elastic behavior.

nodes.label: Jacobson et al., 2008_MgO_nonhydro
nodes.display_label: Jacobsen et al., 2008 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: KCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: hk0 and hkl reflections; nonhydrostatic broadening visible above ~40 GPa

KCl medium becomes non-hydrostatic at relatively low pressures.
MgO compression in KCl produces significantly higher K0 and K′ than in helium.
This KCl-derived EOS agrees well with independent ultrasonic and Brillouin data,
supporting K′ ≈ 4.0 as the intrinsic value for MgO.

This EOS is used in the paper to demonstrate that the anomalously low K′ = 3.74
from the He-medium data is not intrinsic to MgO but arises from ruby scale issues.
The KCl-derived EOS provides an upper bound on MgO stiffness under non-hydrostatic
compression and matches the expected elastic constants much better.

nodes.label: Jamieson et al., 1982_Au
nodes.display_label: Jamieson et al., 1982 | Au | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Walsh et al., 1957; McQueen and Marsh, 1960; Altshuler et al., 1958; Jones et al., 1966 | Max Pressure: 83 GPa | PTM: not applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: EOS parameters derived by fitting BM3 to 300 K slices of Hugoniot-derived isochores (Table 3). Not a direct static compression EOS.

Jamieson et al. (1982) construct P–T–η isochores using shock Hugoniot
data and a Mie–Grüneisen framework. This EOS is a secondary derivation
performed by fitting a third-order Birch–Murnaghan equation to
the 300 K isotherm extracted from the isochore tables.

nodes.label: Jamieson et al., 1982_MgO
nodes.display_label: Jamieson et al., 1982 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Carter et al., 1971 | Max Pressure: 93 GPa | PTM: not applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

Jamieson et al. intentionally favor Hugoniot-based MgO
isochores due to inconsistencies among static EOS datasets.
This BM3 fit reflects a secondary reinterpretation at 300 K.

nodes.label: Jamieson et al., 1982_Pt
nodes.display_label: Jamieson et al., 1982 | Pt | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: McQueen et al., 1970; Morgan, 1974 | Max Pressure: 91 GPa | PTM: not applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: EOS derived from fitting BM3 to the 300 K isotherm extracted from Table 4 (Pt isochores). Jamieson et al. do not publish a static compression EOS for Pt.

Pt isochores are calculated using Hugoniot data reduced
via a Mie–Grüneisen formalism. The reported EOS here
is a post-processed static-equivalent fit.

nodes.label: Jeanloz et al., 1991_Re
nodes.display_label: Jeanloz et al., 1991 | Re | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 170 GPa | PTM: ME; None

rows.value: Static strength and equation of state of rhenium at ultra-high pressures

doi_lines.text: Static strength and equation of state of rhenium at ultra-high pressures

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Static DAC experiments were conducted under nonhydrostatic conditions (with and without a 4:1 methanol:ethanol medium), leading to significant shear stresses and preferred orientation in Re. The DAC XRD P–V data are systematically biased (volumes too large at a given pressure) relative to a hydrostatic 300 K isotherm.The reported equation of state is a fourth-order Eulerian finite-strain EOS for Re at 300 K, obtained by combining ultrasonic measurements (K0T, K0T') with shock Hugoniot data reduced using a Mie–Grüneisen formalism.

This paper investigates the static strength and equation of state of rhenium (Re) at pressures up to ~120 GPa using diamond-anvil cell X-ray diffraction, revealing that rhenium is one of the strongest known polycrystalline metals under extreme compression . By analyzing pressure–volume data alongside ultrasonic and shock-wave measurements, the authors show that a fourth-order equation of state is required to accurately describe Re’s compression behavior, and that nonhydrostatic stresses in experiments lead to systematic deviations in measured volumes. These deviations are used to quantify shear stresses, which reach ~10–15 GPa at high pressures, corresponding to ~4% of the shear modulus—consistent with theoretical expectations.

nodes.label: Jephcoat et al., 1988_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Jephcoat et al., 1988 | Al2O3 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 175 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value:
Ruby (Al2O3:Cr3+) compressed in a DAC to 175 GPa at room temperature.
No reconstructive transition observed. Pressure measured by X-ray–induced ruby fluorescence.
Weakening and broadening of the R1–R2 luminescence at high pressure produced large uncertainties.
Unit-cell volume at highest pressure corresponded to V/Vo = 0.71, consistent with a Birch–Murnaghan EOS
using K0=254 GPa and K0′=4.3.

The study confirms stability of corundum structure to 175 GPa at room T.
The c/a ratio remains ~constant, indicating isotropic compression.
EOS comparison: their measured P–V curve matches a 3rd-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS with ultrasonic values (254(6) GPa, 4.3).
A PIB model calculation is also in close agreement at 175 GPa.

nodes.display_label: Jing et al., 2013 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Datchi97_Equation | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 127 GPa | PTM: None

nodes.label: Knittle et al., 1989_cBN
nodes.display_label: Knittle et al., 1989 | cBN | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978; Barnett et al., 1973 | Max Pressure: 115 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
First direct experimental determination of the 300 K EOS of cubic BN
to ultrahigh pressures. Pressures determined using ruby fluorescence;
volumes from single diffraction peak (111) due to weak higher-order
reflections at high pressure. Despite nonhydrostatic effects above
~15–20 GPa, the large compression range (to 115 GPa) allows K0 and K0'
to be constrained with reasonable precision.

EOS obtained by fitting static compression data with a third-order
Birch–Murnaghan equation of state (Eulerian finite strain).
Reported parameters: K0 = 369 ± 14 GPa, K0' = 4.0 ± 0.2.
Reference volume V0 corresponds to the ambient-pressure lattice
parameter a0 = 3.615 ± 0.002 Å.

Authors demonstrate consistency between this static EOS, first-principles
calculations, and independent shock Hugoniot data, supporting the
reliability of the ruby pressure calibration at ultrahigh pressures.

nodes.label: Ko et al., 2021_Cu
nodes.display_label: Ko et al., 2021 | Cu | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 49 GPa | PTM: He gas

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Helium PTM greatly improved hydrostatic conditions. Heat-treated Cu had 
fewer microstructural defects, yielding more uniform Debye rings and more 
consistent P–V data.

Only the heat-treated polycrystalline Cu sample is retained in the EoSAlign
database because Ko et al. (2021) show that this dataset provides the most
reliable and internally consistent equation of state. Among the three sample
types studied (polycrystalline untreated, polycrystalline heat-treated,
and single crystal), the heat-treated polycrystal exhibited: 
  (1) the most hydrostatic conditions due to helium PTM, 
  (2) the lowest microstructural defect density, 
  (3) the most uniform and symmetric diffraction rings, and 
  (4) the smallest residuals in the Birch–Murnaghan fit.
The untreated polycrystalline sample shows softening attributed to defect
populations, and the single-crystal sample shows orientation effects and
nonuniform intensities. For these reasons, the heat-treated polycrystalline
Cu dataset is considered the authoritative EOS representation in this study.

nodes.label: Kraus et al., 2016_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Kraus et al., 2016 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Kraus et al., 2016; Dewaele et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: quasi-hydrostatic (from referenced DAC studies)

rows.value:
Ruby R1-line calibration derived by Kraus et al. (2016) using the
newly determined 298 K isotherm of Cu as an absolute pressure reference.
The calibration relies on quasi-hydrostatic DAC data from Dewaele et al. (2004),
with pressure recalculated using the Kraus Cu isotherm.

The functional form follows the standard ruby power-law:
    P = (A/B) * [ (1 + Δλ/λ0)^B – 1 ]
where A = 19.151 Mbar and B = 10.603.

Uncertainty is dominated by the Cu isotherm uncertainty (~3% at 150 GPa)
and possible nonhydrostatic stress in the referenced DAC measurements.

This ruby calibration supersedes Mao et al. (1986) at high pressure and
is consistent with Holzapfel (2010) and Chijioke et al. (2005) within
stated uncertainties. Above ~150 GPa the calibration is unconstrained
by data and should be treated as extrapolative.

nodes.label: Kraus et al., 2016_Cu
nodes.display_label: Kraus et al., 2016 | Cu | XRD | Vinet-Extended | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 450 GPa | PTM: none

rows.value: Pulsed-power ramp compression with VISAR + Iterative Lagrangian Analysis (ILA)

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Z pulsed-power accelerator at Sandia National Laboratories
Plastic work heating corrected; isentrope corrected to 298 K using a Mie–Grüneisen Debye thermal model.
Absolute stress–density path derived from shockless ramp compression. Strength and deviatoric stress explicitly corrected. Fits intended for interpolation, not extrapolation beyond reported range.

nodes.label: Lazicki et al., 2012_Be
nodes.display_label: Lazicki et al., 2012 | Be | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 93 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 150 x 350 µm (Be+He), 70 x 300 µm (high-P NaCl cells)

The Be-in-He measurements (Be+He_1, He_2, He_3) provide the most  hydrostatic and lowest-scatter P–V data, extending from ambient pressure  to 93 GPa without observable nonhydrostatic peak broadening. These  measurements determine V0 directly and yield the preferred Rydberg–Vinet  EOS parameters (V0, K0, K0'). The NaCl-based compression data are  slightly stiffer due to nonhydrostatic stresses and were not used for  determining V0.  # Fig. 2 and Table III.

EOS fitting:
  The Rydberg–Vinet form used (Eq. 1 in the paper):
     P = 3 K0 X^-2 (1 - X) exp[1.5 (K0' - 1)(1 - X)],
     where X = (V/V0)^(1/3).
  V0, K0, K0' obtained from the normalized stress vs Eulerian strain 
  plot (inset of Fig. 2). The Be-in-He dataset determines V0 = 8.133 Å^3/atom.

Structure:
  Beryllium retains the α-Be (hcp) structure up to at least 205 GPa 
  and 4000 K (no evidence for β-Be or bcc-Be).  # Fig. 1 and sections II–III.

High-T data:
  P–V–T measurements up to 82 GPa and 2630 K are fit separately using 
  a quasiharmonic Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model; these parameters were 
  not included here because you asked for the EOS from this paper "only"
  and strictly the cold compression values.

nodes.label: Lazicki et al., 2012_Be_innacl
nodes.display_label: Lazicki et al., 2012 | Be | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Ono et al., 2006; Sakai et al., 2011 | Max Pressure: 192 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 150 x 350 µm (Be+He), 70 x 300 µm (high-P NaCl cells)

Pressure determination relies on the NaCl equation of state and is further affected by strong thermal gradients during laser heating, leading to uncertainties of approximately ±5 GPa at high temperature. Temperature measurements from pyrometry carry an additional uncertainty of about ±100 K. Structural parameters such as lattice constants and the c/a ratio show increased dispersion due to heterogeneous stress distributions across crystallites.

Beryllium experiments conducted in a NaCl pressure medium (runs Be_1–Be_5) used laser-heated diamond anvil cells with NaCl serving as both a pressure-transmitting medium and thermal insulator, while pressure was determined from the NaCl (B1/B2) equation of state and, in some cases, supplemented by MgO at high temperature. These measurements extended to ~205 GPa and ~4000 K and provided P–V–T data for constructing the high-pressure equation of state and thermal expansion of Be.

nodes.label: Leger et al., 1990_SrB4O7
nodes.display_label: Leger et al., 1990 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 108 GPa | PTM: ME; None

rows.value: additonal experiment was done in ICE PTM, no equation was derived, this experiment was mixed with Au and Al2O3, XRD was collected at the ESRF. They show good agreement Fig 3.

nodes.label: Lei et al., 2013_Al203_P1
nodes.display_label: Lei et al., 2013(P1) | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971; Mao et al., 1986; Zou et al., 1982 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value:
This study revisits the ruby fluorescence pressure scale by explicitly correcting
non-hydrostatic stress effects in the DAC X-ray diffraction experiments used by
Mao et al. (1978, 1986) to calibrate the classic MBSS78 and MXB86 scales.
The authors model the influence of pressure gradients and differential stress on
measured d-spacings, expressing the error in d/d0 in terms of the yield strength
of the pressure transmitting medium (PTM) and the shear modulus of the standard
material. Using these corrections, they recalculate the Cu and Ag compression
data in Ar, and Mo in 4:1 methanol–ethanol, then refit the ruby scale with
functional form I:
  P = (A/B)[(1 + Δλ/λ0)^B - 1].
The main P1 scale, based on corrected Cu+Ag-in-Ar data, yields A = 1904 GPa
(fixed) and B = 9.827. A secondary P2 scale from corrected Mo-in-ME (where this data comes from is not cited) data gives
B = 8.761. The authors show that P1 agrees well with several recent ruby scales
(DLM04, CNSS05, DO07, DTLM08) below ~100 GPa and therefore regard P1 as the
preferred revised ruby pressure scale.

This YAML records the P1 ruby fluorescence pressure scale proposed by Liu et al. (2013), which corrects the MXB86 calibration for non-hydrostatic stresses in DAC XRD data and refits the standard Mao-form (functional form I). The parameter A = 1904 GPa is inherited from earlier ruby scales and held fixed, while B = 9.827 is obtained from corrected Cu and Ag compression data in Ar. The alternative P2 scale, based on Mo in 4:1 ME (A = 1904, B = 8.761), is not used here but is relevant for assessing systematic uncertainties associated with PTM strength and elastic-property mismatches between standards and ruby.

nodes.display_label: Lei et al., 2013(P2) | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Carter et al., 1971; Not Specified | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.value:
This study revisits the ruby fluorescence pressure scale by explicitly correcting
non-hydrostatic stress effects in the DAC X-ray diffraction experiments used by
Mao et al. (1978, 1986) to calibrate the classic MBSS78 and MXB86 scales.
The authors model the influence of pressure gradients and differential stress on

of the pressure transmitting medium (PTM) and the shear modulus of the standard

The main P1 scale, based on corrected Cu+Ag-in-Ar data, yields A = 1904 GPa
(fixed) and B = 9.827. A secondary P2 scale from corrected Mo-in-ME data (where this data comes from is not cited) gives
B = 8.761. The authors show that P1 agrees well with several recent ruby scales
(DLM04, CNSS05, DO07, DTLM08) below ~100 GPa and therefore regard P1 as the

nodes.label: Ling-Yun et al., 2010_Ta
nodes.display_label: Ling-Yun et al., 2010 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Chijioke et al., 2005 | Max Pressure: 133 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The bcc phase of Ta was observed to remain stable up to 133 GPa using energy-dispersive synchrotron XRD in a modified Mao–Bell DAC with Ar as the pressure medium. Unit-cell volumes were measured and fit with the Vinet EOS, fixing V0 at 36.01 Å^3. The fit yields B0 = 192.65 ± 3.08 GPa and B0' = 3.58 ± 0.11. Hydrostaticity was evaluated by (1) the normalized d-spacing method for cubic crystals and (2) monitoring the ruby R1–R2 peak separation. Differences between (110) and other d-spacings remain <0.1%, indicating a well quasi-hydrostatic stress field over the full pressure range. Ruby R1–R2 splitting increases only slowly below ~40 GPa and more noticeably above this pressure, likely when ruby grains begin to bridge the anvils.

EOS parameters were obtained with the Vinet form using V0 fixed at 36.01 Å^3 from their own low-pressure volume determination. Results are broadly consistent with previous Ta XRD studies (Cynn et al. 1999; Dewaele et al. 2004) and with ultrasonic-based B0 ~194 GPa when comparing B0' values across datasets.

in situ measurements to 1673 K (P–V–T dataset)

nodes.label: Litasov et al., 2013_W
nodes.display_label: Litasov et al., 2013 | W | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Litasov et al., 2013; Litasov et al., 2013 | Max Pressure: 33.5 GPa | PTM: Graphite capsule; MgO medium

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Resistive heating in Kawai-type multi-anvil apparatus using LaCrO3 or graphite heaters. Temperatures reached 1673 K. Differential stress was minimized through high-temperature annealing.

Comprehensive P-V-T dataset collected from ambient pressure to 33.5 GPa and 1673 K using Au and MgO pressure markers. Room-temperature compression fit used the Vinet/Rydberg EOS. Authors report that differential stress was largely relaxed during heating based on stable diffraction peak widths. The 298 K compression curve was constrained together with thermodynamic optimization and comparison to previous static and shock datasets.

Fit applies to the same P–V dataset (0–350 kbar).

EOS fit to Bi bcc (Bi-V) P–V data collected by angular-dispersive
XRD in conventional diffraction geometry using argon as PTM (AXRD).
Fit pressure window reported in Table 2: 11.24–55.47 GPa.

not necessarily the full experimental loading path.

nodes.label: Liu et al., 2013_Bi_He
nodes.display_label: Liu et al., 2013 | Bi | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 50.78 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
EOS fit to Bi bcc (Bi-V) P–V data collected in conventional
diffraction geometry using helium as PTM (EXRD). Fit pressure
window reported in Table 2: 15.31–50.78 GPa.

Reported EOS parameters are from Table 2 (EXRD, He) using a
Vinet EOS. Low-pressure points are excluded because Bi is not
fully in the bcc phase until ~8–10 GPa; Table 2 reports only the
pressure interval used in the fit.

nodes.label: Liu et al., 2013_Bi_SO
nodes.display_label: Liu et al., 2013 | Bi | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 51.99 GPa | PTM: Silicone oil (SO)

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
EOS fit to Bi bcc (Bi-V) P–V data collected by angular-dispersive
XRD in conventional diffraction geometry using silicone oil as PTM (AXRD).
Fit pressure window reported in Table 2: 11.58–51.99 GPa.

Reported EOS parameters are from Table 2 (AXRD, SO) using a Vinet EOS.
Silicone oil is specified in the methods as Baysilone Oil M1000
(polydimethylsiloxane, viscosity 1000 cSt).

nodes.label: Loubeyre et al., 1993
nodes.display_label: Loubeyre et al., 1993 | He | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 58 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Helium DAC data show high precision but include several key uncertainties. Volume measurements have ~0.4% uncertainty from diffraction angle, energy calibration, and reflection centering, while pressure relies on the quasi-hydrostatic ruby scale, introducing calibration dependence.  Although He provides near-ideal hydrostatic conditions, its very low x-ray scattering leads to signal limitations, and small alignment or positioning errors can affect results. At higher pressures (>20 GPa), increased data scatter and deviations arise from thermal and many-body effects, and phase transitions show hysteresis and crystal break-up, complicating precise phase boundary determination.

Helium DAC measurements by Loubeyre et al. (1993) use single-crystal synchrotron X-ray diffraction over ~1–58 GPa and 46–400 K, producing high-quality P–V–T data with sharp reflections from in situ grown crystals. The hcp structure is stable across most conditions, with a limited fcc stability loop near melting and strong hysteresis in the fcc–hcp transition, indicating path-dependent phase boundaries and crystal break-up during transformation. The equation of state shows no significant change in c/a ratio but deviates from simple pair-potential models, demonstrating the increasing importance of many-body interactions at high density. Thermal expansion data (95–304 K) further reveal excess thermal pressure relative to theoretical predictions, suggesting non-phonon contributions.

nodes.label: Luo et al., 2023_MgO
nodes.display_label: Luo et al., 2023 | MgO | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 345 GPa | PTM: none

rows.value: Two-stage light gas gun shock compression + pyrometry + sound velocity measurements

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression experiments performed using a two-stage light gas gun with pyrometry and sound velocity measurements. The complete thermodynamic EOS was constructed from a quasi-Debye Helmholtz free-energy formalism constrained by Hugoniot P-V-T data, sound velocities, and thermal data.

The room-temperature Rydberg-Vinet EOS listed here was not directly fit by Luo et al. (2023). Instead, Shen and Smith (2026) fit a Rydberg-Vinet EOS to the tabulated compression data from this study. The underlying Luo et al. EOS is a thermodynamic Helmholtz free-energy model extending to 345 GPa and 8500 K.

Room-temperature Rydberg-Vinet EOS parameters:
  V0 = 74.572 Å^3/unit cell
  K0 = 161.2 ± 0.3 GPa
  K0' = 4.586 ± 0.007

These values are reported by Shen and Smith (2026)
from fitting a Vinet EOS to the tabulated compression
data generated from the Luo et al. (2023) thermodynamic EOS.

Luo et al. derived the MgO EOS from a quasi-Debye
Helmholtz free-energy framework with parameters (0K):
  K0 = 169.8 ± 0.5 GPa
  K0' = 4.501 ± 0.007

nodes.label: Mao and Bell 1979_Fe
nodes.display_label: Mao and Bell 1979 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Static X-ray diffraction measurements of Fe-hcp were conducted
in a diamond-window cell to pressures approaching 1 Mbar.
Volumes were determined with an accuracy of ±0.3%.
No phase transitions were observed over the investigated
pressure range.

ε-Fe does not exist at ambient pressure, so V0, K0, and
K0' were determined simultaneously by fitting combined
data from this study and Mao et al. (1967).

The resulting first-order Murnaghan EOS is:
V = 6.74(3)[1 + 3.4(3)P(Mbar)]^(-1/5.4).

This EOS predates modern hydrostatic techniques and
should be treated as a historical static reference rather
than a primary pressure standard.

nodes.label: Mao and Bell 1979_MgO
nodes.display_label: Mao and Bell 1979 | MgO | XRD | Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Static X-ray diffraction measurements of MgO were conducted
in a diamond-window cell to pressures approaching 1 Mbar.
Volumes were determined with an accuracy of ±0.3%.
No phase transitions were observed over the investigated
pressure range.

MgO data were fit using a first-order Murnaghan EOS.
The fitted parameters are B0 = 1.56(9) Mbar and
B0' = 4.7(2). The authors note that using ultrasonic
constraints on B0 yields B0' ≈ 4.56, consistent with
Anderson & Andreatch (1966).

This EOS represents one of the earliest static calibrations
of MgO to megabar pressures and is not hydrostatic by
modern standards.

nodes.label: Mao et al., 1978_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Mao et al., 1978 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: ME; None

rows.value: Pressures determined from Cu, Mo, Pd, and Ag volume compression relative to Carter et al. (1971) isotherms. R1 line broadens significantly above ~0.2 Mbar due to nonhydrostatic stress. Pressure uncertainties estimated at ±6% to 1 Mbar.

Mao et al. (1978) measured the specific volumes of Cu, Mo, Pd, and Ag under static compression in a diamond anvil cell and compared them with established shock-wave equations of state to determine absolute pressures. These measurements were used to calibrate the ruby R1 fluorescence pressure scale from 0.06 to ~1 Mbar, establishing a widely adopted empirical relationship between ruby fluorescence shift and pressure that became a standard for high-pressure experiments.

nodes.label: Mao et al., 1986_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Mao et al., 1986 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Zou et al., 1982; Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value:
This is the quasi-hydrostatic ruby pressure scale obtained using argon
as the PTM, which remains soft and nearly hydrostatic up to ~20 GPa and
still provides much lower deviatoric stress than NaCl or no PTM. The
authors used in-situ XRD volumes of Cu to calibrate pressure, anchored
to shock-wave–reduced isothermal Cu and Ag EOS.

The best-fit ruby equation:
    P (Mbar) = (A/B) * [ (1 + Δλ/λ0)^B – 1 ]
    A = 19.04 Mbar, B = 7.665

This scale differs systematically from the earlier nonhydrostatic
Mao et al. (1978) curve at P > 40–50 GPa.

This is the canonical quasi-hydrostatic “Mao86” ruby scale used up to
the early 2000s as the standard reference prior to Chijioke05, Dewaele08,
and Ruby2020.

nodes.label: Mao et al., 1988_He
nodes.display_label: Mao et al., 1988 | He | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 23.3 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Single-crystal X-ray diffraction measurements of solid helium
were performed between 15.6 and 23.3 GPa at 300 K.
Six diffraction peaks were indexed to the hcp structure
(space group P63/mmc) with ideal c/a ≈ 1.633.
Pressure homogeneity was confirmed by identical readings
from three ruby chips, indicating quasihydrostatic conditions.

This study provides the first direct single-crystal XRD
determination of the crystal structure and pressure–volume
relations of solid helium at high pressure. The authors do
not report a closed-form analytical EOS (e.g., BM or Vinet);
instead, tabulated P–V data at 300 K are compared against
theoretical EOS calculations.

nodes.label: Mashimo et al., 2016_W_BM3
nodes.display_label: Mashimo et al., 2016 | W | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 345 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression. Hydrostatic isothermal compression curve derived from Hugoniot data using Mie-Gruneisen thermal corrections and shear-strength correction.

EOS fit corresponds to the BM-3 fit in Table V (typo labeled as BM-2) using strength-corrected hydrostatic isothermal compression data. Fit performed up to 345 GPa. Authors state BM-3 fits well up to ~200 GPa but deviates to higher pressure at higher compression.

EOS parameters from Table V, BM-3 fit with strength correction.
Uses third-order Birch-Murnaghan formalism.
Assumes q = dln(gamma)/dln(V) = 1 and gamma/V = const.

nodes.label: Mashimo et al., 2016_W_BM4
nodes.display_label: Mashimo et al., 2016 | W | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 345 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression. Hydrostatic isothermal compression curve derived from Hugoniot data using Mie-Gruneisen thermal corrections and shear-strength correction.

EOS fit corresponds to the BM-4 fit in Table V (typo labeled as BM-3) using strength-corrected hydrostatic isothermal compression data. Fit performed up to 345 GPa. Authors report BM-4 produces pressures slightly too high below ~250 GPa and too low above ~300 GPa.

EOS parameters from Table V, BM-4 fit with strength correction.
Uses fourth-order Birch-Murnaghan formalism.
Assumes q = dln(gamma)/dln(V) = 1 and gamma/V = const.

nodes.label: Mashimo et al., 2016_W_Vinet
nodes.display_label: Mashimo et al., 2016 | W | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 345 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression. Hydrostatic isothermal compression curve derived from Hugoniot data using Mie-Gruneisen thermal corrections and shear-strength correction.

EOS fit corresponds to the V-2 fit in Table V using strength-corrected hydrostatic isothermal compression data. Authors conclude V-2 provides the best overall fit over the full pressure range to >350 GPa.

EOS parameters from Table V, V-2 fit with strength correction.
Uses second-order Vinet equation.
Assumes q = dln(gamma)/dln(V) = 1 and gamma/V = const.
Recommended EOS fit by the authors.

nodes.label: Mashimo et al., 2016_W_Vinet_nocorrection
nodes.display_label: Mashimo et al., 2016 | W | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 345 GPa | PTM: none

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression. Hydrostatic isothermal compression curve derived from Hugoniot data using Mie-Gruneisen thermal corrections without applying shear-strength correction.

EOS fit corresponds to the V-2 fit in Table V without strength correction. Authors note that neglecting shear strength produces a significantly larger bulk modulus than the strength-corrected EOS results.

EOS parameters from Table V, V-2 fit without strength correction.
Uses second-order Vinet equation.
Assumes q = dln(gamma)/dln(V) = 1 and gamma/V = const.
Authors compare this EOS against the preferred strength-corrected V-2 fit.

nodes.label: Matsui et al., 2009_Pt
nodes.display_label: Matsui et al., 2009 | Pt | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 290 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: yes (thermal expansion data to ~2000 K; T–P–V data to 1600 K)

The 300 K isotherm was derived using a Vinet EOS constrained
primarily by shock compression data to 290 GPa and by
ambient-pressure thermal expansion data between 100 and
nearly 2000 K. Additional T–P–V data collected in a
Kawai-type multi-anvil press to 42 GPa and 1600 K were used
as an independent consistency check.

The EOS was developed without reference to any external
pressure scale and is intended to function as a primary
pressure standard.

Optimized EOS parameters for the 300 K isotherm are:
V0 = 60.38 Å^3, K0 = 273 GPa, K0' = 5.20.

The full T–P–V EOS includes lattice thermal pressure
(Mie–Grüneisen–Debye with γ0 = 2.70, q = 1.10, θ0 = 230 K)
and an electronic thermal pressure term. These thermal
parameters are not included here and should be stored as
a separate thermal-EOS entry linked to this reference.

nodes.label: Matsui et al., 2012_NaCl
nodes.display_label: Matsui et al., 2012 | NaCl-B1 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 12.04 GPa | PTM: BN; Si3N4

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Annealed to 873 K before measurement to reduce deviatoric stress

Sample lengths measured directly with X-ray radiography, avoiding EOS-derived thickness errors.
Sound velocities measured after annealing, reducing non-hydrostatic effects.
One KT data point (ρ = 2.666 g/cm³) excluded from final EOS fit.

This study establishes a primary absolute pressure scale for NaCl at 300 K.
The EOS is determined using simultaneous ultrasonic P- and S-wave velocities 
and X-ray densities in a multi-anvil apparatus up to 12 GPa.
KT0 was fixed at 23.7 GPa (converted from KS0 = 25.0 GPa using α0γ0T).
Fitted parameters are: K0' = 5.14 ± 0.05 and K0'' = -0.392 ± 0.021 GPa^-1.
A high-temperature Mie–Grüneisen–Debye EOS was also developed using q = 0.96 
and Θ0 = 279 K, but only the 300 K EOS is included here unless otherwise requested.

nodes.label: Murakami and Takata 2018_NaCl_B2
nodes.display_label: Murakami and Takata 2019 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 120 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The pressure scale is determined absolutely from simultaneous  longitudinal and transverse acoustic velocities and XRD volumes  of NaCl-B2 up to ~1.2 Mbar without any prior pressure standard. Because NaCl-B2 cannot be quenched, the EOS is defined relative  to a reference point (Pr, Vr) instead of V0. The elastic moduli  (KSr, Gr) were derived by finite-strain fits and corrected to  isothermal values (KTr, K′Tr). Nonhydrostatic stress was low  (|t| = 2.1–3.3 GPa), comparable to Ne/He media (Fig. 3).

nodes.label: Nakano et al., 2002_Be
nodes.display_label: Nakano et al., 2002 | Be | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Not Specified; Not Specified | Max Pressure: 171 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Pressure is determined using mixed calibrations (ruby ≤80 GPa, Re gasket >80 GPa), introducing inconsistency at high pressure. At higher P, only a few diffraction peaks are observable and interference from gasket signals and Kossel lines further reduces data quality.

Nakano et al. (2002) present synchrotron powder X-ray diffraction measurements of beryllium up to 171 GPa at room temperature to investigate its structure and equation of state. The results show that Be remains in the hcp structure across the entire pressure range, with no evidence for previously proposed hcp–hexagonal or theoretically predicted hcp–bcc (or fcc) phase transitions. The lattice parameters decrease smoothly with pressure, and the c/a ratio increases slightly toward the ideal value. An equation of state fitted using the Birch–Murnaghan model gives K₀ ≈ 97 GPa and K′₀ ≈ 3.6, consistent with earlier static compression studies but lower than shock-wave results. Overall, the study provides high-pressure structural and compressibility data for Be, though interpretation is limited by non-hydrostatic conditions and mixed pressure calibration methods.

nodes.label: O'Bannon et al., 2021_Ag_au
nodes.display_label: O'Bannon et al., 2021 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 416 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: fcc (111) used exclusively for volume determination

No pressure-transmitting medium was used (uniaxial stress present).
Corrected volumes were obtained via line-shift analysis using 
calculated Cij elastic constants and α = 1 assumption. 
Only the (111) reflection was used to determine lattice parameters 
because it is least sensitive to nonhydrostatic effects.
Experiment 1 (Ag + Au) achieved 416 GPa, the highest pressure in the study.
Vinet EOS fits were performed with V0 fixed to the measured ambient 
atomic volume (17.031 Å^3) and K0 fixed to the ultrasonic value (101 GPa).

This YAML uses the corrected hydrostatic EOS parameters from Experiment 1 
(Ag + Au). The resulting K0' = 6.02 agrees well with both 
helium-medium DAC studies and reduced-shock/ultrasonic results.
Experiment 2 (Ag + Cu) yields a similar EOS (K0' = 6.10), but Experiment 1 
provides the highest-pressure dataset and the most stable pressure marker. Experiment 2 is slighlt lower pressure base on Fratanduono et al., 2020

nodes.label: O'Bannon et al., 2021_Ag_cu
nodes.display_label: O'Bannon et al., 2021 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 350 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Experiment 2 used Cu as the in situ pressure standard. Pressures were initially
nonhydrostatic due to the absence of a PTM; corrected volumes were computed via
elastic-strain line-shift analysis (Cij-based) assuming α = 1, following 
Singh’s method. Only the (111) reflection was used to extract Ag lattice 
parameters due to its minimal orientation sensitivity. Corrected P–V data were 
fit to a Vinet EOS with V0 and K0 fixed, yielding K0' = 6.10 ± 0.01.

This YAML corresponds strictly to the corrected EOS parameters for Experiment 2 
(Ag + Cu) from O’Bannon et al. (2021). Experiment 2 provides an independent EOS 
using Cu rather than Au as the pressure calibrant. The resulting Vinet slope 
K0' = 6.10 is consistent with Experiment 1 (K0' = 6.02) and with theoretical 
predictions.

Singh’s method. Only the (111) reflection was used to extract Ag lattice

nodes.label: O'Bannon et al., 2021_Ag_k0fix_au
nodes.display_label: O'Bannon et al., 2021 | Ag | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 416 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: fcc (111) used exclusively for volume determination

No pressure-transmitting medium was used (uniaxial stress present).
Corrected volumes were obtained via line-shift analysis using 
calculated Cij elastic constants and α = 1 assumption. 
Only the (111) reflection was used to determine lattice parameters 
because it is least sensitive to nonhydrostatic effects.
Experiment 1 (Ag + Au) achieved 416 GPa, the highest pressure in the study.
Vinet EOS fits were performed with V0 fixed to the measured ambient

(Ag + Au). The authors prefer fits where K0 is fixed due to strong 
K0–K0' correlation. The resulting K0' = 6.02 agrees well with both 
helium-medium DAC studies and reduced-shock/ultrasonic results.

nodes.label: Occelli et al., 2003_Diamond_XRD
nodes.display_label: Occelli et al., 2003 | Diamond | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Zha et al., 2000; Datchi et al., 1997 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Single-crystal diamond (1.1% 13C) was measured in helium PTM under quasi-hydrostatic conditions up to 140 GPa. Ruby pressures are constrained to ±1% to 55 GPa using the Zha et al. (2000) MgO elasticity scale and agree with the Au EOS in He to 124 GPa. Six diamond reflections were tracked, showing minimal broadening, confirming excellent hydrostaticity. The Vinet EOS fit to XRD data gives V0 = 3.4170(8) cm^3/mol, K0 = 446(1) GPa, K0' = 3.0(1). The fit reproduces pressures within <1% to 140 GPa.
This EOS corresponds to the static isothermal (300 K) diamond EOS. It is used for absolute pressure calibration and for comparison with diamond Raman gauge calibrations.

nodes.label: Oganov and Ono 2005_ Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Oganov and Ono 2005 | Al2O3 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Jamieson et al., 1982 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: Pressures were determined using gold as an internal calibrant, with an acknowledged uncertainty of ~10% due to variations among Au equations of state, while ruby fluorescence was avoided at ultrahigh pressures.

A CaIrO₃-type (postperovskite) phase of Al₂O₃ was observed only after high-temperature annealing at ~200 GPa, indicating strong kinetic barriers under cold compression, and was retained metastably upon quenching. The experimentally observed phase transition agrees closely with first-principles predictions, confirming that perovskite Al₂O₃ is not stable and establishing the CaIrO₃-type structure as the relevant high-pressure phase.

nodes.label: Olijnyk and Jephcot 2000_Be
nodes.display_label: Olijnyk and Jephcoat 2000 | Be | Raman | Raman_Polynomial_OJ | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Olijnyk et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 23 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: Raman spectra collected to 23 GPa using Ar+ 514.5 nm laser excitation. The transverse-optical (E2g) zone-center phonon is the only Raman-active mode in Be.Pressures were determined from the Relative Wavenumbers of a Zn reference loaded with Be. No anomalies or discontinuities in ν(P) were observed in the 8.6–14.5 GPa range, where an hcp → distorted-hcp transition had been previously claimed from XRD. The frequency shift is positive and smooth across the full range.

Equation (1) parameters δ0 and δ0' define the nonlinear ν(P) dependence. Authors relate the TO frequency to the elastic shear modulus C44.They find Be’s unusually low mode Grüneisen parameter arises from a high C44 relative to its bulk modulus. No structural transition is detected by Raman to 23 GPa.

nodes.label: Olijnyk et al., 2000_Zn
nodes.display_label: Olijnyk et al., 2000 | Zn | Raman | Raman_Polynomial_OJ | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 58 GPa | PTM: He; ME

rows.value:
Pressures were determined using ruby fluorescence (Mao et al., 1978).
Measurements were conducted up to 58 GPa at room temperature.
Helium was used as a pressure transmitting medium in one run to ensure
quasi-hydrostatic conditions; a second run used 4:1 methanol:ethanol.
The E2g (TO) Raman mode frequency increases monotonically with pressure.
No frequency anomaly was observed near 9–10 GPa where electronic
topological transitions had been predicted.
Linewidth shows an irregularity near ~10 GPa, possibly related to
phonon–phonon or electron–phonon interactions.

This study reports a Raman frequency–pressure calibration only. No volume EOS (V0, K0, K0') for Zn is derived in this work. A Birch–Murnaghan EOS from Takemura (1997) was used only for calculating mode Grüneisen parameters.

nodes.label: Olijnyk et al., 2001_Re
nodes.display_label: Olijnyk et al., 2001 | Re | Raman | Raman_Polynomial_OJ | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 60 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value:
Pressures were determined using ruby fluorescence (Mao et al., 1978).
The TO (E2g) phonon frequency increases monotonically with pressure to 60 GPa.
Eq. (1) provides an excellent representation of ν(P) with δ0 and δ0' from Table I.
No structural transitions or mode anomalies were observed.

nodes.label: Ono et al., 2006_NaCl_BM_and
nodes.display_label: Ono et al., 2006 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 134 GPa | PTM: MgO

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Photon Factory BL13A and SPring-8 BL10XU
laser heating after each pressure step to remove differential stress

CsCl-type NaCl (B2) was studied between 32 and 134 GPa at 300 K. Differential stress was removed via in-situ laser heating, and only heated diffraction patterns were used for EOS fitting. Pressure determinations depend on the choice of Au EOS, leading to a ~3–4% systematic discrepancy at >70 GPa. Because B2–NaCl is not stable below 30 GPa, the EOS was fit at 30 GPa, not extrapolated to 0 GPa. V0 cannot be obtained from this study.

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Photon Factory BL13A and SPring-8 BL10XU
laser heating after each pressure step to remove differential stress

CsCl-type NaCl (B2) was studied between 32 and 134 GPa at 300 K. Differential stress was removed via in-situ laser heating, and only heated diffraction patterns were used for EOS fitting. Pressure determinations depend on the choice of Au EOS, leading to a ~3–4% systematic discrepancy at >70 GPa. Because B2–NaCl is not stable below 30 GPa, the EOS was fit at 30 GPa, not extrapolated to 0 GPa. V0 cannot be obtained from this study.

nodes.label: Ono et al., 2006_NaCl_Vin_and
nodes.display_label: Ono et al., 2006 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 134 GPa | PTM: MgO

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Photon Factory BL13A and SPring-8 BL10XU
laser heating after each pressure step to remove differential stress

CsCl-type NaCl (B2) was studied between 32 and 134 GPa at 300 K. Differential stress was removed via in-situ laser heating, and only heated diffraction patterns were used for EOS fitting. Pressure determinations depend on the choice of Au EOS, leading to a ~3–4% systematic discrepancy at >70 GPa. Because B2–NaCl is not stable below 30 GPa, the EOS was fit at 30 GPa, not extrapolated to 0 GPa. V0 cannot be obtained from this study.

nodes.label: Ono et al., 2010_NaCl
nodes.display_label: Ono 2010 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Takemura 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 270 GPa | PTM: MgO

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: in situ laser annealing to remove deviatoric stress (not used for EOS fitting)

The B2-type NaCl EOS study by Ono (2010) includes several notable sources of uncertainty. Experimental data are affected by differential stress in the DAC, which requires laser annealing to reduce but may not be fully eliminated, introducing residual non-hydrostatic errors. Pressure determination depends on gold as an internal calibrant combined with revised ruby scales, meaning results are sensitive to the chosen pressure standard and known discrepancies between scales (up to ~5–10% at high pressure). The study also highlights that first-principles calculations carry significant uncertainty, with previously reported bulk moduli differing by up to ~50%, and results depending on computational choices (GGA, PAW, simulation conditions).

no (2010) investigates the equation of state of B2-type NaCl using a combination of high-pressure diamond anvil cell experiments and first-principles molecular dynamics calculations up to ~270 GPa and 3000 K. Room-temperature experimental data are used to constrain the static EOS, while simulations are used to model thermal pressure and high-temperature behavior. The results are fitted using a Vinet EOS, yielding bulk modulus and pressure derivative values consistent with corrected pressure scales. The study finds that thermal pressure has only weak volume dependence but strong temperature dependence, and that the Grüneisen parameter varies with temperature, indicating significant anharmonic effects. Overall, the work shows that conventional approaches like the Mie–Grüneisen approximation are insufficient for B2 NaCl at high P–T conditions, and that combining experimental and computational methods is necessary for a more reliable EOS.

nodes.label: Ono et al., 2015_cBN
nodes.display_label: Ono et al., 2015 | cBN | Raman | RamanDatchi04 | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007 | Max Pressure: 90 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.value: DAC (in situ Raman spectroscopy with simultaneous XRD at high P–T)

Raman TO-mode shifts of c-BN were measured up to 90 GPa and 800 K.
Pressure was determined independently from the unit-cell volume
of gold measured by in situ synchrotron XRD, using a published
gold equation of state. Ruby fluorescence was not used during
heating.

Polynomial fitting parameters follow Eq. (1) of Ono et al. (2015).
Temperature-dependent coefficients a and b were partially adopted
from Kawamoto et al. (2004) due to limited sensitivity at ambient
pressure.

This study extends the Raman calibration of the c-BN TO mode to
significantly higher pressures (90 GPa) than previous work.
A clear reduction in dν/dP with increasing pressure is observed,
confirming strong nonlinearity above ~40 GPa.

Unlike Datchi & Canny (2004), pressure is determined directly from
an internal gold standard via XRD, making this calibration less
sensitive to assumptions about ruby behavior at high temperature.

The polynomial form is valid only within the calibrated P–T range;
extrapolation beyond 90 GPa is strongly discouraged.

nodes.label: Piermarini et al., 1975_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Piermarini et al., 1975 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | LinearShift | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Decker 1971 | Max Pressure: 19.5 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.value: ruby R1 fluorescence line; NaCl (200) XRD

Ruby R1 wavelength shifts were calibrated against pressures
derived from NaCl compression using the Decker equation of
state. Measurements were hydrostatic to ~104 kbar and
quasi-hydrostatic from 104–195 kbar. Separate fits for
hydrostatic and combined datasets yield indistinguishable
slopes within uncertainty.

Quadratic terms in Δλ were evaluated but found to be statistically
insignificant over the full pressure range.

Recommended calibration (25 °C):
  P (kbar) = 2.740 · Δλ (Å)

This calibration defines pressures relative to the Decker EOS
for NaCl and does not constitute an absolute pressure standard.
The authors explicitly caution that systematic uncertainty is
dominated by the NaCl EOS rather than by ruby fluorescence
measurement precision.

This study establishes ruby as a secondary pressure standard
and forms the foundation for later ruby scales (e.g., Mao 1978,
Mao 1986), which adopt different functional forms and anchors.

nodes.label: Pigott et al., 2015_Ni
nodes.display_label: Pigott et al., 2015 | Ni | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Speziale et al., 2001 | Max Pressure: 109 GPa | PTM: MgO

rows.value: Laser-heated DAC + in situ synchrotron micro-diffraction

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: symmetric DAC with nanofabricated Ni/SiO2/Ni double hot-plate samples
yes (double-sided laser heating); annealing used to remove deviatoric stress

The 300 K EOS for Ni is derived from measurements taken during cooling after
laser annealing, ensuring deviatoric stresses were minimized. V0 was directly
measured from recovered nanofabricated samples (6.579(1) cm^3/mol).
Pressures were determined using the thermal MgO EOS (Speziale et al., 2001),
with propagated uncertainties of 3–13% in total pressure. The authors note
that their K0 and K0' differ from Dewaele et al. (2008) due to pressure-scale
differences and the strong correlation between K0 and K0'.

This YAML includes only the 300 K Birch-Murnaghan EOS: V0 = 6.579 cm^3/mol,
K0 = 201 GPa, K0' = 4.4 (Fig. 2 and Table 1). Pigott et al. also provide full
Mie-Grüneisen-Debye (MGD) and high-temperature Birch-Murnaghan (HTBM)
thermal EOS parameters (γ0, q, α0, α1, ∂K/∂T), but these are not included here
per user request. Their nanofabricated Ni/SiO2/Ni geometry provides extremely
uniform heating and reduces thermal gradients, yielding one of the highest-
quality thermal EOS datasets for Ni up to 109 GPa and ~3000 K.

nodes.label: Rashchenko et al., 2015_Datch97
nodes.display_label: Rashchenko et al., 2015 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Datchi97_Equation | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 1997; Trots et al., 2013 | Max Pressure: 120 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: Calibration performed in helium quasi-hydrostatic conditions using the Sm:YAG primary scale. Wavelength hysteresis below 35 GPa yields ±0.5 GPa uncertainty. Peak width increases monotonically with pressure. The extended calibration combines Rashchenko data (0–60 GPa) with corrected Datchi et al. (1997) points up to 120 GPa.

The use of Sm:YAG as a primary scale removes inconsistencies between the previous calibrations (Datchi 1997, Jing 2013). After correction for ruby scale biases in older work, all datasets agree within ±0.5 GPa below 60 GPa. The extended Pade-type equation should be used for pressures above 60 GPa.
