nodes.label: Ren et al., 2023_cBN
nodes.display_label: Ren et al., 2023 | cBN | Raman | RamanRen23 | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Datchi et al., 1997 | Max Pressure: 46 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: DAC (in situ Raman spectroscopy at ambient temperature)

Room-temperature Relative Wavenumberss of the c-BN TO mode were measured
from ambient pressure to 46 GPa using helium as the pressure
transmitting medium. Pressure was independently determined using
ruby and Sm2+:SrB4O7 fluorescence standards.

Below ~10 GPa the pressure dependence is effectively linear,
but significant nonlinearity is observed at higher pressure.
The quadratic form (Eq. 4) provides the best fit over the full
pressure range.

This YAML represents a strictly ambient-temperature (293 K)
Raman pressure calibration for c-BN derived from Ren et al. (2023)
Eq. (4):

  ν_TO(P) = 1055.37 + 3.35·P - 0.011·P^2

No temperature-dependent terms or P–T coupling terms are included.
This calibration should not be used at elevated temperature.

nodes.label: Richet et al., 1988_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Richet et al., 1988 | Al2O3 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986 | Max Pressure: 49.3 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value:
Pressures measured with automated ruby-fluorescence system (Mao et al., 1983).
The quasi-hydrostatic ruby scale of Mao et al., 1986 was used; pressures also checked against compression of argon, which gave consistent results.

Study performed high-pressure X-ray diffraction on corundum (Al2O3) in a Mao–Bell DAC.
Pressure determined using the ruby scale of Mao et al., 1986 and cross-checked against argon.
Equation of state analysis with a third-order Birch–Murnaghan fit gave K0=252(5) GPa, K0′=4.2(2).

nodes.label: Romanenko et al., 2018_SrB4O7
nodes.display_label: Romanenko et al., 2018 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Trots et al., 2013 | Max Pressure: 60 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: Pressure calibration for λ1 is reported for 0–60 GPa using helium as PTM. The study also reports temperature effects separately; λ1 is weakly temperature dependent.

Pressure equation form given in Table III:
P = A/B * [(1 + Δλ/λ0)^B − 1], where Δλ = λ − λ0.

nodes.label: Ross et al., 1986_Ar
nodes.display_label: Ross et al., 1986 | Ar | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Piermarini et al., 1975 | Max Pressure: 80.6 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Static compression data for solid argon were collected to
800 kbar at room temperature. Argon remains fcc over the
entire pressure range. Unit-cell volumes were determined
from the (111) and (200) reflections.

Pressure was determined using a ruby fluorescence scale
calibrated for hydrostatic and quasi-hydrostatic conditions,
appropriate for weakly bound solids such as Ar.

This study does not present a closed-form analytical EOS
(e.g., Birch–Murnaghan or Vinet) fit to the static Ar data.
Instead, static P–V measurements are compared directly
with isotherms reduced from shock Hugoniot data using an
exp-6 interatomic potential.

The agreement between static and shock-derived isotherms
is excellent up to ~400 kbar, supporting the validity of
the ruby pressure scale at these pressures to within ~10%.

The authors suggest that solid argon may serve as a static
pressure standard up to ~3–4 Mbar when combined with
theoretical reduction of shock data.

nodes.label: Sakai et al., 2011_BM_DO
nodes.display_label: Sakai et al., 2011 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 304 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: double-sided laser annealing to ~1500–2000 K (to minimize stress)

The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions

Overall, the Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007) Pt scale produces only moderate deviations compared to other calibrations, with pressure differences of about +12 GPa (~3.4%) at 364 GPa relative to the Matsui scale. The B–M EoS based on this calibration remains internally consistent and agrees well with experimental compression data across the studied range. Importantly, differences between B–M and Vinet EoS formulations are small (<2%), confirming that the dominant source of uncertainty is the choice of Pt pressure scale rather than the EoS model itself.

nodes.label: Sakai et al., 2011_BM_Fei
nodes.display_label: Sakai et al., 2011 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fei et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 304 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: double-sided laser annealing to ~1500–2000 K (to minimize stress)

The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions.

Using the platinum (Pt) pressure scale of Fei et al. (2007), the NaCl-B2 phase equation of state shows strong internal consistency and good agreement with the present high-pressure dataset. The Birch–Murnaghan (B–M) EoS fit with this calibration yields V0= 37.73 A^3 (fixed), bulk modulus K0 ≈47.80 GPa, and pressure derivative and K0'≈ 4.06, values that are very close to those obtained with other modern Pt scales. The resulting pressure scale aligns well with the current study, showing only a small deviation of about −4 GPa (~1.1%) at 364 GPa, indicating excellent agreement in the multi-megabar range.

nodes.label: Sakai et al., 2011_BM_Mat
nodes.display_label: Sakai et al., 2011 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Matsui et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 304 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: double-sided laser annealing to ~1500–2000 K (to minimize stress)

The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions

sing the platinum (Pt) pressure scale of Matsui et al. (2009), the NaCl-B2 equation of state provides a well-constrained and internally consistent reference across the full pressure range up to ~304 GPa. For the Birch–Murnaghan (B–M) EoS, the parameters are: zero-pressure volume V₀ ≈ 37.73 Å³ (fixed), bulk modulus K₀ ≈ 47.00 GPa, and pressure derivative K₀′ ≈ 4.10. These values represent a balanced compression behavior and are consistent with the experimental data as well as recalculated datasets from previous studies. The Matsui Pt scale also yields very small differences between the B–M and Vinet formulations (less than ~2 GPa up to 300 GPa), indicating strong agreement between models. Overall, this calibration is considered reliable and serves as a reference baseline, with other Pt scales (e.g., Fei et al.) showing only minor deviations relative to it.

nodes.label: Sakai et al., 2011_BM_hol
nodes.display_label: Sakai et al., 2011 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holmes et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 304 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: double-sided laser annealing to ~1500–2000 K (to minimize stress)

The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions.

Using the platinum (Pt) pressure scale of Holmes et al. (1989), the NaCl-B2 equation of state shows larger deviations compared to more recent calibrations. In the Birch–Murnaghan (B–M) EoS fit, the parameters are: zero-pressure volume V₀ ≈ 38.01 Å³ (fixed), bulk modulus K₀ ≈ 43.42 GPa, and pressure derivative K₀′ ≈ 4.36. These values indicate a slightly softer compression behavior (lower K₀) and higher curvature (higher K₀′) relative to other Pt scales. As a result, pressures calculated using the Holmes scale are systematically higher, with differences reaching about +37 GPa (~10%) at 364 GPa compared to the Matsui-based scale. Overall, while internally consistent, the Holmes et al. Pt calibration tends to overestimate pressure at extreme conditions.

Using the platinum (Pt) pressure scale of Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), the NaCl-B2 equation of state fitted with the Vinet formulation shows consistent high-pressure behavior with moderate deviations relative to other calibrations. Overall, the Dorogokupets and Oganov Pt calibration with the Vinet EoS provides a reasonable and internally consistent description of NaCl-B2 compression, though small systematic offsets remain at extreme pressures .

multiple diffraction peaks and high-pressure DAC experiments with laser annealing to reduce deviatoric stress. However, significant uncertainties arise from the indirect calibration strategy: pressures depend on external Pt-based scales (e.g., Dorogokupets and Oganov, 2007), introducing systematic bias tied to those references. The zero-pressure volume V₀ is not directly measured but inferred from g–G analysis, adding uncertainty to the EoS parameters. Additionally, differences between pressure scales can produce variations of several GPa (up to ~10% near 300 GPa), and residual nonhydrostatic stresses (estimated a few GPa) can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6% at extreme conditions.

In Sakai et al. (2011), the NaCl-B2 phase is established as a pressure scale using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan (3BM) equation of state, indirectly calibrated to platinum-based standards, including Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), despite Pt not being present in the experimental cell. For this calibration, the EoS parameters are approximately V₀ ≈ 37.76 Å³ (derived from the g–G method), K₀ ≈ 47.24 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.14. The pressure scale is constructed by combining new high-pressure compression data with recalibrated literature datasets, producing a consistent pressure framework up to multi-megabar conditions. The resulting NaCl-B2 scale aligns reasonably well with other Pt-based calibrations, with only moderate deviations at the highest pressures

However, uncertainties arise from the indirect nature of the pressure calibration: NaCl-B2 is tied to external Pt-based scales (Fei et al., 2007), introducing systematic dependence on those reference EoSs. The zero-pressure volume V₀ is not directly measured but derived from g–G analysis, contributing additional uncertainty. Differences between pressure scales can lead to deviations of several GPa (up to ~10% near 300 GPa), and residual nonhydrostatic stress (on the order of a few GPa) can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6% at high pressure. In Sakai et al. (2014), the NaCl-B2 phase is used as an internal pressure scale for DAC experiments, calibrated indirectly to Pt-based standards through previous work (Sakai et al., 2011), specifically adopting the Fei et al. (2007) calibration. The pressure is determined using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan equation of state without fixing K₀, allowing full parameter fitting. For the Fei-based calibration, the NaCl-B2 EoS parameters are approximately V₀ ≈ 37.73 Å³ (from g–G analysis), K₀ ≈ 47.80 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.06. This calibration provides a pressure scale consistent with internally self-consistent Pt standards and is applied to determine the compression behavior of Fe at high pressures.

ynchrotron XRD and laser annealing, which reduces deviatoric stress and allows accurate lattice parameter determination from multiple diffraction peaks. However, uncertainties are primarily linked to the indirect pressure calibration: NaCl-B2 is tied to external Pt scales (here Matsui et al., 2009), introducing systematic dependence on that reference. Although Matsui’s scale is relatively consistent with modern calibrations, pressure differences of several GPa can still occur at multi-megabar conditions. Residual nonhydrostatic stress in the DAC (estimated ~4–5 GPa) can influence volume by ~0.4–0.6%, and weak diffraction peaks (e.g., hcp 002) may affect precision in lattice refinement. Overall, the dataset is internally consistent and reliable, with uncertainties dominated by pressure scale choice rather than experimental measurement precision

In Sakai et al. (2014), the equation of state of hcp-Fe is determined from synchrotron XRD measurements in a symmetric-type DAC (300–150 µm culets), with pressure calibrated using the NaCl-B2 scale from Sakai et al. (2011), ultimately tied to the Matsui et al. (2009) Pt calibration. The pressure–volume data are fitted using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan equation of state with all parameters free. For this calibration (P1-scale), the fitted parameters are approximately V₀ ≈ 22.18 Å³, K₀ ≈ 179.6 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.91. The dataset extends to ~279 GPa and shows that while different pressure scales affect K₀ and K₀′, the resulting volumes at core pressures remain very similar (within ~0.3–0.4%), indicating robust compression behavior. This calibration is considered one of the more internally consistent Pt-based scales and provides a reliable reference for modeling Fe under inner core conditions .

synchrotron XRD measurements and the use of laser annealing to reduce deviatoric stress, combined with multiple diffraction peaks (4–8 lines) for robust volume determination. However, uncertainties arise primarily from pressure calibration and nonhydrostatic effects. The choice of NaCl-B2 (Dorfman et al., 2012) introduces systematic dependence on its EoS, and different pressure scales can lead to variations of up to ~10% in pressure at ~300 GPa. Residual uniaxial stress in the DAC (estimated ~4–5 GPa) can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6% at high pressure, while limited diffraction quality (e.g., weak 002 peak in hcp Fe) and peak selection can influence lattice parameter accuracy.

For hcp-Fe measured by XRD in a symmetric-type DAC (300–150 µm culets), the equation of state was determined using NaCl-B2 as a pressure calibrant following Dorfman et al. (2012). Using this calibration (P4-scale), the fitted EoS parameters for pure iron are approximately V₀ ≈ 22.07 Å³, K₀ ≈ 195.1 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.93, indicating relatively stiff compression at high pressures. The dataset extends to ~279 GPa and shows that different pressure scales mainly shift K₀ while producing very similar volumes at core conditions (differences ~0.3–0.4%).

XRD measurements combined with laser annealing, which reduces deviatoric stress and allows volume determination from multiple diffraction peaks. However, uncertainties are dominated by the pressure calibration choice. The Holmes et al. (1989) Pt scale is known to overestimate pressure (by several percent), and this propagates into the NaCl-B2 calibration, leading to systematic pressure offsets of up to ~10% at ~300 GPa. Additional uncertainties arise from nonhydrostatic stress in the DAC (estimated ~4–5 GPa), which can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6% at high pressure, as well as weak diffraction features (e.g., the hcp 002 peak) that influence lattice parameter determination.

In Sakai et al. (2014), the equation of state of hcp-Fe is determined using XRD measurements in a symmetric-type DAC (300–150 µm culets), with pressure calibrated via the NaCl-B2 scale from Sakai et al. (2011), ultimately tied to the Holmes et al. (1989) Pt calibration. The pressure is described using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan equation of state with free parameters. Under this calibration (equivalent to the highest-pressure P6-scale), the fitted EoS parameters for hcp-Fe are approximately V₀ ≈ 22.21 Å³, K₀ ≈ 171.7 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 5.49. Despite differences in K₀ and K₀′ relative to other calibrations, the resulting volumes at inner core pressures remain very similar (within ~0.3–0.4%), indicating that the Holmes-based calibration mainly shifts the pressure scale rather than the compression trend. This approach enables extension of Fe compression data to ~279 GPa under internally consistent calibration conditions.

synchrotron XRD measurements and laser annealing, which reduce deviatoric stress and improve lattice parameter accuracy. However, uncertainties are primarily associated with the indirect pressure calibration: the NaCl-B2 scale depends on external Pt-based calibrations (here Dorogokupets and Oganov, 2007), introducing systematic pressure uncertainties that can reach several GPa at multi-megabar conditions. Residual nonhydrostatic stress (~4–5 GPa) can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6%, and weak diffraction peaks (notably the hcp 002 reflection) may introduce additional scatter in lattice parameter determination. Furthermore, differences between EoS formulations (Vinet vs. Birch–Murnaghan) contribute to parameter variability, especially in K₀′. Despite these factors, the consistency of volume results across calibrations indicates that the dataset is robust, with uncertainties dominated by pressure scale selection rather than measurement precision.

In Sakai et al. (2014), the equation of state of hcp-Fe is derived from XRD measurements in a symmetric-type DAC (300–150 µm culets), with pressure calibrated using the NaCl-B2 scale (Sakai et al., 2011) linked to the Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007) Pt calibration. The compression data are fitted using the Rydberg–Vinet (Vinet) equation of state with free parameters. For this calibration (P3-scale), the fitted parameters are approximately V₀ ≈ 22.14 Å³, K₀ ≈ 180.0 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 5.40. The dataset extends to ~279 GPa and shows that the Vinet formulation yields slightly higher K₀′ and lower compressibility compared to Birch–Murnaghan fits, while maintaining very similar volumes at inner core pressures. This demonstrates that the Vinet EoS provides a consistent description of Fe compression under extreme conditions when coupled with modern NaCl-B2-based pressure scales

nodes.label: Sakai et al., 2018_Re
nodes.display_label: Sakai et al., 2018 | Re | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Max Pressure: 464 GPa | PTM: glycerin; silica glass

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 3–5 μm 2nd-stage micro-anvils; 40 μm double-beveled primary anvils

Rhenium was used both as a sample and as a pressure marker in double-stage DAC (ds-DAC) experiments with nano-polycrystalline diamond (NPD) 2nd-stage anvils, and in a conventional DAC experiment to 280 GPa with Pt as a co-loaded sample. Pressures in the ds-DAC runs were calculated from Re unit-cell volumes using the Re EOS of Anzellini et al. (2014). In the conventional DAC experiment (Run RP01), Re and Pt were compressed together with a silica glass pressure medium; pressures from the Pt EOS of Yokoo et al. (2009) and Dewaele et al. (2004) were compared.
To examine consistency between Pt and Re scales, Sakai et al. refit their Re compression data based on the Yokoo et al. (2009) Pt scale with a Vinet EOS, fixing V0 = 29.47 Å^3 (hcp unit cell), obtaining K0 = 358(10) GPa and K0' = 4.8(2). This EOS yields maximum ds-DAC pressures of ~464 GPa for V = 18.65 Å^3 (V/V0 = 0.633), whereas the Re EOS of Dubrovinsky et al. (2012) would give ~630 GPa for the same volume, which Sakai et al. argue is unreasonably high.
XRD peaks from Re in ds-DAC runs are very broad due to extreme pressure gradients within the ~3 μm X-ray beam footprint; the highest-pressure component is extracted by deconvolving the Re 101 peak into multiple Lorentzians. Conventional DAC data show sharper peaks (FWHM ~0.2°), providing the main constraint for the Vinet fit.

This YAML encodes the Re Vinet EOS (V0 fixed at 29.47 Å^3, K0 = 358 GPa, K0' = 4.8) derived
by Sakai et al. (2018) from combined Re+Pt compression data calibrated against the Yokoo
et al. (2009) Pt pressure scale. It is intended for use as an alternative high-pressure Re
EOS in ds-DAC contexts, particularly for pressures above ~200 GPa where discrepancies between
different Re scales become significant. Sakai et al. conclude that the Re EOS of Dubrovinsky
et al. (2012) substantially overestimates pressures in the 400–600 GPa range relative to Pt-
based calibrations.

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: laser annealing to ~1200 K at each pressure step (stress relaxation)

NaCl-B2 unit-cell volumes were measured from 30–68 GPa. Laser annealing significantly reduced differential stress, improving precision. Because B2-phase NaCl is not stable at low pressure, the authors used a modified third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS referenced at Vr=27.17 Å³ rather than the conventional zero-pressure form. Pt and MgO calibrations give consistent results. Zero-pressure V0 cannot be determined from B2 data.

EOS parameters listed are from the Pt-calibrated experiment (Table II). MgO calibration yields similar values: Pr=32.08 GPa, Kr=143.2 GPa, Kr′=3.94. The modified EOS should be evaluated exactly as given in Eq. (1a) rather than a standard BM3 form.

nodes.label: Schulte and Holzapfel 1996_H12
nodes.display_label: Schulte and Holzapfel 1996 | Zn | XRD | H12 | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Mao et al., 1978 | Max Pressure: 74 GPa | PTM: Liquid N2; mineral oil

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: HASYLAB DESY, EDS station F3; plus laboratory W-anode tube
Five hP2 Zn reflections (five dhkl values used to refine a and c at each pressure)

Zinc was studied up to 74 GPa in the hP2 structure with energy-dispersive XRD.
Pressures were measured using ruby luminescence on the Mao et al. (1978) nonlinear scale.
The axial ratio c/a decreases monotonically with pressure and crosses the ideal hcp value
(c/a = 1.633) near V/V0 ≈ 0.74. Previous low-pressure XRD anomalies in c/a are attributed
to deviatoric stress; the present data show only a smooth decrease. The authors compare
multiple EOS forms (MU2, BE2, MV2, H02, H12, H11) for Zn and conclude that the simple,
one-parameter Holzapfel H11 form gives an excellent description of the combined static,
shock, and ultrasonic data, with K0 ≈ 61 GPa and an implied K0′ ≈ 5.5.

This YAML records the Zn EOS using the Holzapfel H11 form, which the authors identify as
the most appropriate "simple" EOS for Zn when all available p–V data (static DAC, volumetric,
and shock-wave) are considered together. In H11, only K0 is an independent parameter; K0′ is
determined by the correlation K0′ = 3 - (2/3) ln(3K0 / pFG0), where pFG0 is the Fermi-gas
limiting pressure at V0. For Zn, this yields K0′ ≈ 5.5 for K0 ≈ 61 GPa. Alternative two-parameter
fits (MU2, BE2, MV2, H02, H12) give similar K0–K0′ pairs but diverge more strongly at extreme
compression. Zn behaves as a "soft" simple solid relative to the ideal Thomas–Fermi-like
asymptotic behavior in h–X plots, consistent with its unusual c/a and d-band contributions.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_Au_1
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The gold (Au) dataset exhibits high overall precision, though with slightly more variability compared to Pt due to experimental factors. Diffraction peaks remain sharp throughout compression to ~140 GPa, allowing reliable extraction of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Multiple fcc reflections [(111), (220), (222), and (311)] were used to improve accuracy and reduce sensitivity to stress anisotropy. Small discrepancies are observed between the two experimental runs (DAC-1 and DAC-2), primarily attributed to differences in grain size and initial loading conditions. In particular, the Au grain in DAC-2 showed signs of increased deviatoric stress and possible partial bridging, leading to slightly higher scatter in the measured volumes. Lattice-strain analysis confirms that Au experiences somewhat higher stress in DAC-2 relative to DAC-1, though still within acceptable limits for quasihydrostatic conditions. Consequently, the DAC-1 dataset, which shows lower scatter, is selected for primary analysis. Despite these minor variations, the Au data remain internally consistent, with deviations well within experimental uncertainty.

Gold (Au) is a widely used pressure calibrant and demonstrates consistent, well-characterized compressional behavior in this study. The experimentally determined volume–volume (V–V) relations show strong agreement with prior literature and confirm Au’s reliability as a secondary standard when referenced to Cu . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 167.5 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.85) closely match previous static and dynamic measurements, reinforcing confidence in its calibration performance. However, comparison with ramp-derived EOS models reveals a small systematic negative deviation (ΔV/V ≈ −0.4%), suggesting that the dynamic Au EOS predicts slightly larger volumes (i.e., a softer response) relative to the Cu-referenced scale. Despite this minor offset, the discrepancy remains small and within the broader uncertainty range of high-pressure EOS comparisons. DAC-1 was used for fitting.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_MgO
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | MgO | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The magnesium oxide (MgO) dataset shows good overall quality with reliable measurements across the full pressure range, though with slightly larger uncertainty compared to some metallic calibrants. Diffraction peaks remain well-defined up to ~140 GPa, allowing extraction of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.1% . Unit-cell volumes were determined using the (200) and (220) reflections, which provide sufficient constraint while minimizing peak overlap. Data were obtained from DAC-2, where MgO was co-loaded with other calibrants in a helium pressure medium, ensuring quasihydrostatic conditions. Deviatoric stress effects are present but remain small, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Compared to metals like Pt or Mo, MgO exhibits slightly greater scatter and sensitivity to stress due to its ionic structure, but the dataset remains internally consistent. Overall, the MgO data are of solid quality, with manageable uncertainties and no major anomalies.

Magnesium oxide (MgO) serves as an important ionic pressure calibrant and displays consistent compressional behavior in this study. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and well-defined, though comparisons with previous studies show larger discrepancies (exceeding ~1% in volume in some cases), suggesting greater sensitivity to experimental conditions or calibration methods . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 161.9 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.08) are generally consistent with prior work, supporting its validity as a secondary standard. In terms of stiffness scaling, MgO behaves similarly to bcc metals, showing moderate increases in bulk modulus with compression. While slightly less precise than some metallic calibrants, MgO remains a useful and widely applied standard, particularly for comparisons involving oxide materials. For fitting, DAC-2 was used.

rows.value: The molybdenum (Mo) dataset demonstrates high precision and strong internal consistency across the full pressure range investigated. Diffraction peaks remain sharp and well-defined up to ~140 GPa, enabling accurate determination of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Multiple bcc reflections were used to calculate unit-cell volumes, reducing sensitivity to peak overlap and improving robustness. Measurements from both DAC-1 and DAC-2 runs show excellent agreement, with relative differences generally <0.1%, indicating minimal influence from spatial pressure gradients or sample positioning. Deviatoric stress effects are small under helium quasihydrostatic conditions, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Overall, the Mo dataset is dense, low-scatter, and reproducible, making it one of the more reliable datasets in the study.

Molybdenum (Mo) exhibits stable and well-characterized compressional behavior, confirming its suitability as a high-pressure calibrant. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and consistent, reflecting reliable relative compressibility across the studied pressure range . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 260.6 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.06) are in close agreement with previous experimental and theoretical results, reinforcing confidence in the dataset. Comparisons with literature show good agreement at lower pressures, though small discrepancies (up to ~1% in volume) emerge at higher compression when compared to some neon-medium studies, suggesting minor medium- or methodology-dependent differences. Mo’s bulk modulus scaling behavior is consistent with other bcc metals, showing moderate stiffening with compression. For fitting, DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_NaC1-B1
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | NaCl-B1 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 31 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The NaCl-B1 (rocksalt) dataset shows moderate-to-good data quality within its stability range, with some limitations tied to phase behavior and fitting constraints. Diffraction peaks remain identifiable and usable up to ~30–31 GPa, allowing determination of unit-cell volumes with typical relative uncertainties on the order of ~0.1% . Volumes were derived using the (200) and (220) reflections, which provide sufficient constraint while minimizing peak overlap. A key limitation arises in the B1–B2 coexistence region (~30–35 GPa), where both phases exhibit inconsistent volume trends; data from this region are therefore excluded from fits to maintain accuracy. Additionally, extrapolation to zero pressure is not well constrained by the experimental dataset alone, so the reference volume (V₀) is fixed using literature values during fitting. Despite these constraints, the remaining dataset shows reasonable internal consistency under quasihydrostatic helium conditions, with only minor contributions from deviatoric stress. Overall, NaCl-B1 data are reliable within the single-phase region but require careful handling near the phase transition and at low-pressure extrapolation.

NaCl-B1 serves as a commonly used ionic pressure calibrant at lower pressures and demonstrates expected compressional behavior within its stability field. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are well-defined in the single-phase region and reflect consistent relative compressibility . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 23.5 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.23) align reasonably well with previous studies, confirming its general reliability. However, NaCl-B1 exhibits strong compressibility and increased sensitivity to pressure conditions compared to metallic calibrants, and discrepancies with other datasets can arise, particularly near the B1–B2 transition. Its relatively low bulk modulus and steep compression at low pressures also require more flexible fitting approaches. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_NaC1-B2
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The NaCl-B2 dataset shows good quality within the high-pressure phase field, though with some limitations related to phase transition behavior and structural effects. Diffraction peaks remain identifiable at pressures above ~33 GPa, enabling determination of unit-cell volumes with typical relative uncertainties of ~0.1% . Volumes were calculated using the (110) and (211) reflections, which are appropriate for the B2 structure. Data in the B1–B2 coexistence region (~30–35 GPa) exhibit inconsistent trends and are excluded from fitting to maintain accuracy. Compared to lower-pressure phases and metallic calibrants, NaCl-B2 shows slightly broader diffraction peaks, indicating modest increases in strain and/or grain-size effects at high pressure. Additionally, because NaCl-B2 is not stable at ambient conditions, its zero-pressure volume (V₀) is constrained using literature values during EOS fitting. Despite these factors, the dataset is internally consistent within the single-phase region and benefits from quasihydrostatic conditions provided by the helium medium. Overall, NaCl-B2 data are reliable at high pressures but require careful treatment near the phase transition and in parameter constraints.

NaCl-B2 represents the high-pressure phase of sodium chloride and provides useful calibration data in the upper pressure regime. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are well-defined in the B2 phase and allow characterization of its compressional behavior independent of an assumed pressure scale . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 24.9 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.63) are broadly consistent with prior experimental results, though some discrepancies exceeding ~1% in volume are observed when compared to other studies, reflecting sensitivity to experimental conditions and calibration pathways. NaCl-B2 exhibits higher stiffness than the B1 phase but remains significantly more compressible than metallic calibrants. Its bulk modulus scaling behavior is comparable to other ionic materials and some bcc metals. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | Pt | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The platinum (Pt) dataset demonstrates high precision and reliability across the full pressure range investigated. Diffraction peaks for Pt remain sharp and well-defined up to ~140 GPa, enabling accurate determination of lattice parameters with very low uncertainty (on the order of ~0.05–0.1% in volume) . The use of multiple reflections [(111), (220), (222), and (311)] further improves robustness by minimizing sensitivity to deviatoric stress effects. Comparison between independent experimental runs (DAC-1 and DAC-2) shows minor differences, primarily attributed to variations in grain size or initial loading conditions rather than systematic error. Pt exhibits very low deviatoric stress (t < 0.5 GPa), corresponding to a negligible volume bias (<0.2%), confirming near-hydrostatic conditions in the helium medium . The selected dataset (DAC-2) shows lower scatter and is therefore used for primary analysis, though inclusion of both runs does not significantly alter the fitted relations. Overall, the Pt data are internally consistent, reproducible, and among the highest-quality measurements in the study.

Platinum (Pt) serves as a key metallic pressure calibrant and exhibits stable, well-behaved compression under high-pressure conditions. In this study, Pt shows strong agreement with other calibrants through directly measured volume–volume (V–V) relations, indicating consistent relative compressibility when referenced to Cu . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters for Pt (K₀ ≈ 261 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.75) align closely with prior experimental and ramp-compression studies, supporting its reliability as a secondary standard. Additionally, cross-comparisons with dynamic EOS models show that Pt has negligible residual differences (ΔV/V ≈ 0), indicating excellent consistency with the Cu reference scale and reinforcing its role as a benchmark material. Its high bulk modulus and strong stiffening behavior under compression further highlight its suitability for high-pressure calibration work. Overall, Pt is confirmed as a robust, precise, and internally consistent calibrant within the experimental framework. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_Ta_1
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The tantalum (Ta) dataset shows notable variability and requires careful selection due to anomalous behavior observed in one experimental run. While diffraction data generally extend to high pressures with measurable peaks, the DAC-2 Ta dataset exhibits significant scatter, broadened diffraction peaks, and an anomalously stiff compression trend relative to both DAC-1 and literature values . This behavior is attributed to likely helium incorporation or defect-related lattice effects, supported by separate heating experiments in which Ta showed irreversible lattice expansion and abnormal thermal response compared to co-loaded materials. As a result, the DAC-2 Ta data are excluded from the primary analysis. In contrast, the DAC-1 dataset demonstrates more consistent behavior, with reasonable agreement with prior studies and acceptable scatter within experimental uncertainty. Although deviatoric stress effects are present, they remain within the quasihydrostatic regime of the helium medium. Overall, the reliability of the Ta dataset depends strongly on dataset selection, with DAC-1 providing usable, moderate-quality data and DAC-2 identified as compromised.

Tantalum (Ta) exhibits more complex and less stable behavior under high-pressure conditions compared to other metallic calibrants in this study. The accepted DAC-1 data produce a consistent volume–volume (V–V) relationship relative to Cu, though with slightly greater uncertainty than materials like Pt or Au . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 195 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 3.62) are broadly consistent with previous experimental results, supporting its general validity as a pressure calibrant. However, cross-comparison with ramp-derived EOS models reveals a modest, pressure-dependent positive deviation (ΔV/V up to ~+0.4%), indicating some inconsistency with the Cu-referenced scale at intermediate pressures. The anomalous behavior observed in DAC-2 highlights Ta’s sensitivity to experimental conditions, particularly possible interactions with helium, which may affect lattice properties. Overall, while Ta can serve as a useful calibrant, its data quality is more condition-dependent, and it requires careful validation compared to more robust standards like Pt or Au. For fiitng, DAC-1 used and DAC-2 excluded.

nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | W | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 140 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The tungsten (W) dataset demonstrates strong overall quality and consistency across the full pressure range, with only minor limitations at low pressures. Diffraction peaks for W remain sharp and well-resolved up to ~140 GPa, supporting precise lattice parameter determination with typical relative volume uncertainties on the order of ~0.1% . Unit-cell volumes were derived from multiple bcc reflections, improving robustness and reducing sensitivity to peak overlap. A known limitation occurs at lower pressures (<~20 GPa), where some diffraction peaks are affected by overlap with the rhenium gasket signal, leading to reduced reliability in that region. To address this, the zero-pressure volume (V₀) was constrained using literature values during fitting. Across higher pressures, the dataset shows minimal scatter and excellent agreement between DAC-1 and DAC-2 runs, indicating negligible sensitivity to spatial positioning or pressure gradients within the sample chamber. Deviatoric stress effects remain small under helium quasihydrostatic conditions, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Overall, aside from low-pressure overlap effects, the W dataset is high quality, internally consistent, and reliable for EOS analysis.

Tungsten (W) exhibits stable and well-characterized compressional behavior, making it a reliable high-pressure calibrant in this study. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations show strong agreement with previous measurements, particularly those obtained in helium media, reinforcing consistency across independent datasets . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 307 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.02) are consistent with literature values and reflect W’s high stiffness and moderate rate of stiffening under compression. Compared to other materials, W shows relatively small deviations when cross-compared with existing calibrations, indicating good internal consistency within the Cu-referenced framework. Its bulk modulus scaling behavior also aligns with other bcc metals, showing a more gradual increase in stiffness relative to close-packed metals like Pt or Au. Overall, W is confirmed as a robust and dependable calibrant with high data quality, minimal systematic issues, and strong agreement with prior high-pressure studies. For fitting, DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined.

nodes.label: Shen and Smith _2026_bcc-Fe
nodes.display_label: Shen and Smith 2026 | Fe-bcc | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020 | Max Pressure: 15.5 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: The body-centered cubic iron (bcc-Fe) dataset shows strong data quality within its stability range, with precise and consistent measurements under quasihydrostatic conditions. Diffraction peaks remain well-resolved throughout the low- to mid-pressure regime prior to the phase transition, allowing unit-cell volumes to be determined with typical relative uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Multiple bcc reflections were used to improve robustness and minimize the effects of peak overlap. Agreement between DAC-1 and DAC-2 runs is excellent, with relative volume differences generally below ~0.1%, indicating minimal influence from spatial pressure gradients or sample positioning. Deviatoric stress effects are small in the helium medium, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. However, data near the bcc-to-hcp phase transition must be treated carefully, as structural changes can introduce inconsistencies. Overall, within its single-phase stability field, the bcc-Fe dataset is high quality, internally consistent, and reliable.

Body-centered cubic iron (bcc-Fe) exhibits expected compressional behavior at lower pressures and serves as an important reference phase for iron under compression. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and consistent within the bcc stability range, reflecting reliable relative compressibility . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters are well constrained and align with previous studies, supporting confidence in the dataset. As pressure increases, Fe transitions to the hcp phase, marking the limit of applicability for the bcc dataset. Comparisons with other calibrants show good internal consistency, with only minor deviations within experimental uncertainty. For fiiting, DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined.

rows.value: The hexagonal close-packed iron (hcp-Fe) dataset demonstrates high data quality across the high-pressure range where this phase is stable. Diffraction peaks remain well-defined up to ~140 GPa, allowing accurate determination of unit-cell volumes with typical relative uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Volumes were calculated using multiple hcp reflections [(100), (002), (101), and (103)], improving robustness and reducing sensitivity to peak overlap. Compared to its bcc counterpart, hcp-Fe exhibits slightly broader diffraction peaks at high pressure, consistent with modest increases in strain and/or grain-size effects, but still within acceptable limits for precise analysis. Measurements from DAC-1 and DAC-2 show excellent agreement, with minimal scatter and relative differences generally below ~0.1%, indicating a uniform pressure environment. Deviatoric stress effects remain small under helium quasihydrostatic conditions, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Overall, the hcp-Fe dataset is dense, internally consistent, and reliable for EOS determination.

Hexagonal close-packed iron (hcp-Fe) represents the stable high-pressure phase of iron and is critical for understanding compression behavior at extreme conditions. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and well-defined, reflecting consistent relative compressibility across the studied pressure range . Cross-comparison with ramp-derived EOS models reveals a systematic positive deviation (ΔV/V up to ~+0.9%), indicating that the dynamic EOS predicts a slightly stiffer response (smaller volumes) relative to the Cu-referenced scale. Despite this offset, the discrepancies are modest and provide useful constraints for refining EOS models. In terms of stiffness scaling, hcp-Fe behaves similarly to other close-packed metals, showing relatively rapid stiffening with compression. DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined. eos

nodes.label: Shen et al., 2020_ruby
nodes.display_label: Shen et al., 2020 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Polynomial | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2004; Zha et al., 2000; Occelli et al., 2003 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: synchrotron-based XRD; facility not specified in Shen et al. (2020)

Ruby2020 was derived by combining three independent calibration
paths: (1) metals (Mo, Cu) reduced from dynamic compression and
converted via V–Δλ data; (2) MgO Brillouin+XRD with direct P–Δλ;
(3) diamond EOS combined with measured V–Δλ. All V–Δλ data were
collected with small-spot synchrotron XRD and ruby in He-loaded DACs.
Global fit: P = A(Δλ/λ0)[1 + B(Δλ/λ0)] with A=1870 GPa, B=5.63,
RMS ≈ 0.7 GPa (0–150 GPa). Estimated maximum uncertainty ±2.5%.

AIRAPT endorsed Ruby2020 in June 2020. Valid up to 150 GPa at room T.
Extrapolation above 150 GPa is discouraged; ruby fluorescence weakens
and ruby is metastable at very high pressures.

nodes.label: Shi et al., 2022_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Shi et al., 2022 | Al2O3 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fei et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 153 GPa | PTM: NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 5–8 diffraction peaks of corundum used for refinement

Pressure-volume-temperature data collected up to 153 GPa and 3400 K.
Thermal EoS derived by fitting with the Mie-Grüneisen-Debye formulation.
At 300 K, corundum EOS fit yielded K0=246(2) GPa, K0'=4 (fixed), V0=255.1 Å^3/hex cell (fixed).
Corundum stable up to 106 GPa at 300 K; above ~2300 K transforms to Rh2O3(II)-type phase.

Corundum powder (99.9%) mixed with 7.5 wt% Pt used as sample and pressure calibrant.
NaCl served as pressure medium and thermal insulator.
Observed transition to Rh2O3(II)-type Al2O3 above ~106 GPa at 2300 K.
Thermal EoS parameters reported in Table 1 and Table 2; corundum KT0=246(2) GPa, K0'=4 (fixed), V0=255.1 Å^3 (fixed).

nodes.label: Shim et al., 2002_Au
nodes.display_label: Shim et al., 2002 | Au | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Mao et al., 1986; Takemura 2001 | Max Pressure: 75 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: They constructed P–V–T equation of state (EoS) for gold by combining quasi-hydrostatic compression data (using helium as a pressure medium) with shock-wave (Hugoniot) data, significantly improving data reliability compared to earlier non-hydrostatic studies. The use of helium minimizes deviatoric stress, and multiple diagnostics (e.g., diffraction peak consistency) confirm near-hydrostatic conditions, enhancing confidence in the 300 K isotherm (page 2–3). However, uncertainties remain due to reliance on the ruby pressure scale (~1% uncertainty), assumptions in the Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model, and limitations in low-compression Hugoniot data where shear strength effects distort results.

Data from Takemura et al 2001 were fit. This paper develops an improved gold EoS by jointly inverting static compression and shock-wave data, yielding key parameters such as K₀′ ≈ 5.0 and Grüneisen parameter q ≈ 1.0, and incorporating both phonon and electronic effects. The resulting model provides a more accurate pressure scale, particularly at high P–T conditions relevant to Earth’s mantle. Compared to previous EoSs, the new formulation better reproduces shock data and reduces systematic errors, shifting calculated pressures by about +1 GPa at 660 km depth. Despite this improvement, a remaining ~1.5 GPa discrepancy persists between experimental phase boundaries and seismic observations, indicating that uncertainties in temperature measurements and other experimental factors are still significant.

nodes.label: Smith et al., 2018_Fe
nodes.display_label: Smith et al., 2018 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 1400 GPa | PTM: none

rows.value: Laser-driven ramp compression with VISAR + Lagrangian sound-speed analysis

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Near-isentropic ramp compression from an initial 60 GPa shock state. Thermal corrections applied for:
  - initial 60 GPa shock heating
  - plastic work heating from strength
  - deviatoric stress offset.
Estimated sample temperatures remain substantially below the Hugoniot and within the solid hcp-Fe stability field during compression.

Iron was precompressed into the hcp phase using an initial 60 GPa shock prior to ramp loading. Pressure-density path determined from free-surface velocity histories using a Lagrangian sound-speed analysis. Final isentrope corrected for strength and thermal effects. The resulting EOS extends to 1.4 TPa and ~20 g/cm^3, corresponding to estimated core pressures of ~3.5-4 Earth-mass rocky exoplanets.

The isentropic compression curve was fit using a third-order
Rydberg-Vinet equation of state:

P(X)=3K0X^-2(1-X)exp[(1.5K0'-1.5)(1-X)]

where:
  X = (rho0/rho)^(1/3)
  rho0 = 8.43 g/cm^3 for epsilon-Fe.

Best-fit parameters reported in the text:
  K0 = 177.7 ± 0.6 GPa
  K0' = 5.64 ± 0.01

The study states that extrapolation of the fitted EOS agrees with
first-principles calculations predicting hcp-Fe stability to ~6 TPa.

The low-pressure release isentrope and initial shocked state
analysis used SESAME EOS table 2150 for Fe (Kerley, 1993).

High-pressure strength corrections were constrained using:
  Smith et al., 2013
  Gleason and Mao, 2013
  Huntington et al., 2017

nodes.label: Sokolova et al., 2013_Ag_AP2
nodes.display_label: Sokolova et al., 2013 | Ag | XRD | AP2 | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Levashov et al., 2004 | Max Pressure: 400 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals to 4 Mbar and 3000 K

doi_lines.text: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals to 4 Mbar and 3000 K

rows.value: values from table 2 with V0 from table 1

absrtact: Based on the modified formalism of Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), we calculated the equation of state for diamond, MgO, Ag, Al,
Au, Cu, Mo, Nb, Pt, Ta, and W by simultaneous optimization of the data of shock-wave experiments and ultrasonic, X-ray diffraction,
dilatometric, and thermochemical measurements in the temperature range from ~100 K to the melting points and pressures of up to several
Mbar, depending on the material. The obtained room-temperature isotherms were adjusted with a shift of the R1 luminescence line of ruby,
which was measured simultaneously with the unit cell parameters of metals in the helium and argon pressure media. The new ruby scale is
expressed as P(GPa) = 1870⋅Δλ / λ0(1 + 6⋅Δλ / λ0). It can be used for correction of room-pressure isotherms of metals, diamond, and periclase.
New simultaneous measurements of the volumes of Au, Pt, MgO, and B2-NaCl were used for interrelated test of obtained equations of state
and calculation of the room-pressure isotherm for B2-NaCl. Therefore, the constructed equations of state for nine metals, diamond, periclase,
and B2-NaCl can be considered self-consistent and consistent with the ruby scale and are close to a thermodynamic equilibrium. The calculated
PVT relations can be used as self-consistent pressure scales in the study of the PVT properties of minerals using diamond anvil cell in a wide
range of temperatures and pressures.

rows.value: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals to 4 Mbar and 3000 K

doi_lines.text: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals to 4 Mbar and 3000 K

rows.value: values from table 2 with V0 from table 1

absrtact: Based on the modified formalism of Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), we calculated the equation of state for diamond, MgO, Ag, Al,
Au, Cu, Mo, Nb, Pt, Ta, and W by simultaneous optimization of the data of shock-wave experiments and ultrasonic, X-ray diffraction,
dilatometric, and thermochemical measurements in the temperature range from ~100 K to the melting points and pressures of up to several
Mbar, depending on the material. The obtained room-temperature isotherms were adjusted with a shift of the R1 luminescence line of ruby,
which was measured simultaneously with the unit cell parameters of metals in the helium and argon pressure media. The new ruby scale is
expressed as P(GPa) = 1870⋅Δλ / λ0(1 + 6⋅Δλ / λ0). It can be used for correction of room-pressure isotherms of metals, diamond, and periclase.
New simultaneous measurements of the volumes of Au, Pt, MgO, and B2-NaCl were used for interrelated test of obtained equations of state
and calculation of the room-pressure isotherm for B2-NaCl. Therefore, the constructed equations of state for nine metals, diamond, periclase,
and B2-NaCl can be considered self-consistent and consistent with the ruby scale and are close to a thermodynamic equilibrium. The calculated
PVT relations can be used as self-consistent pressure scales in the study of the PVT properties of minerals using diamond anvil cell in a wide
range of temperatures and pressures.

nodes.label: Speziale et al., 2001_MgO
nodes.display_label: Speziale et al., 2001 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 53 GPa | PTM: ME; He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: compression data for MgO under quasi-hydrostatic conditions (using helium up to 52 GPa), with very low lattice parameter uncertainty (~0.03%), demonstrating strong experimental control (page 1, 3). However, data quality limitations arise from inconsistencies between different datasets and methods, particularly between static compression and shock-wave (Hugoniot) data. The assumption of a constant Grüneisen parameter volume dependence (q) is shown to be inadequate, as it cannot simultaneously fit low-pressure static data and high-pressure shock data (pages 5–6). Additional uncertainties stem from pressure calibration differences (e.g., Au vs. NaCl standards showing up to ~6% discrepancy at high P–T, page 4), extrapolation of equations of state beyond measured ranges, and sensitivity to thermal parameters such as heat capacity and temperature estimates. These issues highlight that even with high-quality measurements, model assumptions and calibration choices remain major sources of systematic error .

This paper develops an improved P–V–T equation of state for MgO by combining precise quasi-hydrostatic compression data with existing high-temperature and shock-wave datasets within a Birch–Murnaghan–Debye framework. The study determines key parameters (e.g., K₀′ ≈ 3.99, γ ≈ 1.524) and shows that a single constant-q model is insufficient; instead, the Grüneisen parameter must decrease with compression to reconcile static and shock data. The resulting model successfully describes MgO behavior from ambient conditions up to ~200 GPa and ~3600 K, providing a unified framework consistent with both experimental and theoretical constraints.

nodes.display_label: Speziale et al., 2001 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Not Specified | Max Pressure: 53 GPa | PTM: ME; He

nodes.label: Sun et al., 2005_diamond
nodes.display_label: Sun et al., 2005 | Diamond | Raman | LinearShift | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Holmes et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 300 GPa | PTM: CH4

rows.value: Diamond Raman pressure scale calibrated against a primary Pt equation of state derived from shock Hugoniot data (Holmes et al., 1989). Relative Wavenumbers measured at the diamond–sample interface. High-frequency cutoff corrected by subtracting constant FWHM (8 cm^-1). Calibration valid from 50 to 300 GPa at 300 K.

nodes.label: Takemura 2007
nodes.display_label: Takemura 2007 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Zha et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 122 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Data were collected under quasi-hydrostatic conditions using He as a pressure
medium. Detectable uniaxial stress develops above ~30 GPa. EOS parameters are
derived by explicitly accounting for differential stress using gamma-plot
analysis. Gold is shown to be extremely sensitive to non-hydrostatic stress.

This paper does not present an independent full EOS fit with freely varying
parameters. Instead, it re-analyzes existing Au XRD datasets (Takemura 2001;
Dewaele et al. 2004) to demonstrate that discrepancies in reported EOS parameters
arise from differing uniaxial stress states in solid He.

The reported K0 = 167 GPa and K0' = 5.46(3) correspond to a *derived hypothetical
hydrostatic EOS*, obtained by averaging datasets with opposite-sign differential
stress. K0 is treated as fixed in the final analysis.

nodes.label: Takemura and Dewaele, 2008_maxP123
nodes.display_label: Takemura and Dewaele 2008 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Zha et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 123 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: helium pressure medium, multiple independent experimental runs, and detailed analysis of stress effects via diffraction peak behavior and ruby fluorescence. However, the study shows that even helium becomes nonhydrostatic above ~30 GPa, leading to measurable uniaxial stresses that can bias lattice parameters depending on crystallographic direction. The magnitude and even sign of stress vary between runs, introducing systematic uncertainty. Additional uncertainties arise from pressure calibration (different ruby scales can shift results by ~5–6% at 100 GPa) and from assumptions in stress correction (e.g., elastic parameters and model dependence).

Takemura and Dewaele (2008) present a high-precision isothermal equation of state for gold (Au) based on powder XRD measurements in a DAC using a helium pressure medium, extending to a maximum pressure of 123 GPa. The study emphasizes minimizing stress effects by selecting the (111) diffraction line, which is least sensitive to uniaxial stress, and fitting the resulting P–V data to a Vinet EoS with a fixed bulk modulus B₀ = 167 GPa. The resulting pressure derivative is B₀′ ≈ 5.5 using the conventional ruby scale, and ≈5.9 when using an updated pressure calibration, aligning well with ultrasonic and theoretical values. The work demonstrates that discrepancies in previous Au EoSs largely arise from stress effects and calibration differences rather than intrinsic material behavior .

nodes.label: Takemura and Dewaele, 2008_maxP131
nodes.display_label: Takemura and Dewaele 2008 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007 | Max Pressure: 131 GPa | PTM: He

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Helium pressure medium, synchrotron XRD (ESRF ID27), and careful experimental design including multiple runs and stress analysis. However, the study highlights that nonhydrostatic stress develops above ~30 GPa even in He, leading to uniaxial stresses that affect lattice spacings depending on crystallographic orientation. The choice to use the (111) reflection minimizes this bias, but residual uncertainties remain. Additional systematic uncertainty arises from pressure calibration, as different ruby scales can shift pressures by ~5–6% at ~100 GPa. Variations in stress state between runs (including both positive and negative uniaxial stress) further contribute to scatter. Overall, the measurements are precise, but accuracy is primarily limited by stress effects and pressure scale calibration rather than instrumental resolution.

Takemura and Dewaele (2008) determined the equation of state of Au using XRD measurements in a membrane DAC (100–300 µm culets) with a He pressure medium, reaching pressures up to ~131 GPa. The pressure scale is evaluated relative to updated calibrations (including Dorogokupets and Oganov, 2007), and the data are fitted using a third-order Rydberg–Vinet EoS with the bulk modulus fixed at K₀ = 167 GPa. The resulting parameters are V₀ ≈ 16.962 Å³ per atom and K₀′ ≈ 5.9 when using the revised pressure scale, in excellent agreement with ultrasonic and theoretical constraints. The study demonstrates that when stress effects are minimized (by using the (111) reflection), Au provides a reliable and internally consistent pressure standard at high pressures.

nodes.label: Tange et al., 2009_BM
nodes.display_label: Tange et al., 2009 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 300 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: limitations in high-pressure elastic (Kₛ) data above ~100 GPa and reliance on shock data for the highest pressures introduce some uncertainty in separating isothermal and thermal contributions. Despite these factors, the consistency across diverse datasets makes this one of the most reliable primary EoSs for MgO .

Tange et al. (2009) present a unified and self-consistent P–V–T equation of state for MgO based on multiple pressure-scale-independent datasets, including XRD, elastic (Kₛ), thermal expansion, and shock compression data. The 300 K isotherm is described using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan EoS with parameters V₀ ≈ 74.698 Å³, K₀ ≈ 160.64 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.221, while thermal effects are incorporated through a Mie–Grüneisen–Debye framework. The model successfully reproduces experimental data up to ~200 GPa (and extrapolated toward ~300 GPa) and high temperatures with high accuracy (RMS pressure residual ~0.8 GPa). Importantly, this EoS is developed as a primary pressure scale, independent of other calibrants, and is widely applicable for high-pressure and high-temperature experiments .

rows.value: limitations in high-pressure elastic (Kₛ) data above ~100 GPa and reliance on shock data for the highest pressures introduce some uncertainty in separating isothermal and thermal contributions. Despite these factors, the consistency across diverse datasets makes this one of the most reliable primary EoSs for MgO .

Tange et al. (2009) developed a primary, pressure-scale-independent equation of state for MgO by combining multiple datasets, including XRD, elastic (Kₛ), thermal expansion, and shock compression measurements. The 300 K isothermal compression is described using a third-order Rydberg–Vinet equation with parameters V₀ ≈ 74.698 Å³, K₀ ≈ 160.63 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.367. This model is embedded within a Mie–Grüneisen–Debye framework to account for thermal effects, allowing accurate reproduction of P–V–T behavior over a wide range of conditions up to ~200 GPa (and extended toward ~300 GPa). The resulting EoS provides a self-consistent and widely applicable primary pressure standard, independent of secondary calibrations

This EOS represents the most hydrostatic high-pressure measurement
of hcp Co up to 160 GPa. Torchio et al. merged their low-pressure data

and magnetic results show that the inversion in the pressure derivative
of c/a (around V/V0 ≈ 0.76) is linked to magnetism, not to the hcp–fcc
transition. The hcp phase remains stable to at least 160 GPa under
quasihydrostatic conditions.

rows.value: Reduced shock Hugoniot data for Be

The AP2 EOS parameters B0 = 125.7 GPa, B0' = 2.921, and C0 = 0.6465 were obtained by
reducing Be shock Hugoniot data (McQueen et al., 1970; Nellis et al., 1988) to a
300 K isotherm and fitting with an AP2 form. The resulting shock-reduced EOS is
significantly stiffer than the static DAC EOS and begins to deviate from it above
~20 GPa. The authors attribute the discrepancy to uncertainties in the Grüneisen
parameter and other assumptions in the shock reduction and emphasize that the
shock-based EOS should not be used interchangeably with the static one for
quasi-static compression studies.

rows.value:
The Cu EOS recorded here is not based on new Cu experiments in Velisavljević et al. (2002),
but on an AP2-form fit to existing Cu shock Hugoniot data. The authors state that fitting the
Cu shock data to the AP2-EOS yields K0 = 133 GPa and K0' = 5.4, and that these parameters
were used for pressure determination when Cu served as the internal standard in their Be
diamond-anvil cell experiments. The AP2 c0 parameter is constrained by K0, V0, and the
atomic number Z via the Fermi-gas constant a_FG and is not explicitly tabulated in this
paper. The pressure range of the underlying Cu shock data (McQueen et al., 1970; Nellis
et al., 1988) exceeds that of the Be static experiments and provides a high-pressure
reference EOS for Cu.

This YAML captures the AP2 EOS parameters for Cu (K0 = 133 GPa, K0' = 5.4) as used by Velisavljević et al. (2002) for pressure calibration. V0 is taken from standard ambient fcc Cu (0.011815 nm^3 per atom) consistent with later AP2-based Cu EOS work, though it is not explicitly refit in this study.

nodes.label: Vohra et al., 1987_Re
nodes.display_label: Vohra et al., 1987 | Re | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Bell et al., 1986; McQueen et al., 1970 | Max Pressure: 216 GPa | PTM: None

rows.value: DAC, effectivly a fit to Mcqueen et al., 1970 Shock Data
Data is uncertain between 140-280 GPa because the diamond signal is masking the ruby signal. The isothermal EOS of Re above 200 GPa is based on shock-wave measurements on Re (McQueeen et al., 1970)

This paper investigates the behavior of rhenium under extremely high pressures (up to 216 GPa) using energy-dispersive x-ray diffraction in a diamond-anvil cell.Their results show that rhenium maintains its hexagonal close-packed (hcp) structure throughout the entire range, with no phase transition observed, and that its axial ratio remains essentially constant with pressure. Additionally, the study presents one of the first x-ray-based pressure profiles across a diamond surface, revealing how pressure is distributed within the sample. Overall, the findings demonstrate both the structural stability of rhenium at extreme conditions and its suitability for use in high-pressure experimental setups.
