nodes.label: Wei et al., 2024_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Wei et al., 2024 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 58.6 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value: The ruby fluorescence dataset shows high signal quality and well-resolved R1 and R2 peaks at room temperature, but data quality degrades with increasing temperature due to thermal effects . Peak broadening, reduced intensity, and fluorescence quenching become significant above ~773–850 K, limiting measurement precision and eventually making ruby unusable at higher temperatures. Additionally, strong temperature-induced redshifts and pressure–temperature coupling introduce systematic errors if not corrected, with deviations increasing across the full P–T range. Even under quasi-hydrostatic conditions with Ar as a pressure-transmitting medium, nonhydrostatic effects may still contribute minor uncertainties. The use of cBN as an independent calibration standard improves reliability, but overall accuracy depends on applying temperature-corrected calibration relationships.

This study calibrates ruby fluorescence as a pressure sensor up to 58.6 GPa and 850 K using a power-law relationship between pressure and wavelength shift, with parameters A = 1881.16 GPa and B = 10.94 at 296 K . The R1 and R2 luminescence lines are used to determine pressure, with calibration tied to a cBN reference scale. Ruby exhibits strong fluorescence intensity and clear spectral features at ambient conditions, making it a widely used pressure sensor. However, it shows significant temperature sensitivity, including peak broadening and redshift, requiring temperature-dependent corrections for accurate pressure determination. Compared to other sensors in the study, ruby has the strongest signal but the highest temperature sensitivity, limiting its effectiveness at high temperatures.

nodes.label: Wei et al., 2024_Al2O3HP
nodes.display_label: Wei et al., 2024 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value: The ruby fluorescence pressure scale demonstrates strong signal intensity and well-defined R₁/R₂ peaks under ambient conditions, making it a reliable pressure calibrant in DAC experiments; however, its data quality degrades at elevated temperatures due to thermal broadening, reduced intensity, and significant redshift of the fluorescence lines . As shown in the spectra (e.g., Fig. 1 and Fig. 2), peak widths increase and signal-to-noise decreases with temperature, limiting precise measurements above ~773–850 K due to fluorescence quenching and peak overlap. Additionally, pressure calibration accuracy is affected by temperature–pressure coupling, meaning that neglecting temperature corrections can introduce systematic errors of several GPa at high P–T conditions. Calibration consistency also depends on the chosen ruby scale and pressure-transmitting medium, with non-hydrostatic effects (e.g., Ar vs He) introducing measurable deviations.

Ruby (Cr³⁺-doped Al₂O₃) is one of the most widely used optical pressure sensors in DAC experiments due to its strong fluorescence and clear pressure-dependent spectral shifts, particularly the R₁ line arising from crystal field splitting of Cr³⁺ ions . Its pressure calibration is well established up to tens of GPa and can be extended further with modern scales, making it a standard reference for high-pressure studies. However, the material exhibits strong temperature sensitivity, with fluorescence peaks shifting and broadening significantly at elevated temperatures, requiring temperature-corrected calibration models. Compared to newer sensors, ruby offers excellent signal strength but reduced reliability under high-temperature conditions, where alternative materials may provide better spectral stability.

nodes.label: Wei et al., 2024_SrB4O7
nodes.display_label: Wei et al., 2024 | SrB4O7 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 58.6 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value: Other fits are proposed in this study, and we have selected equation 10 and table 4. Among the sensors studied, Sm²⁺:SrB₄O₇ (λ₁ line) exhibits the best spectral quality, minimal thermal quenching, and lowest sensitivity to non-hydrostatic stress, making it the most robust fluorescence pressure marker for high-temperature DAC experiments, although the authors note that future refinement would benefit from calibration against primary EOS standards such as Au or Pt.

nodes.label: Wei et al., 2024_YAG-Y1
nodes.display_label: Wei et al., 2024 | YAG-Y1 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 58.6 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value: The Sm³⁺:YAG fluorescence sensor (particularly the Y₁ line) exhibits generally high data quality with relatively stable peak positions and lower temperature sensitivity compared to ruby, but several factors still limit precision under extreme conditions . As shown in the spectra and temperature trends (e.g., Fig. 6 and Fig. 9), peak broadening and intensity reduction occur at elevated temperatures, and line overlap (especially between Y₁ and Y₂) can complicate peak identification at high P–T conditions. Although temperature effects are smaller than in ruby, they are not negligible—ignoring them can introduce pressure errors of several GPa at high temperatures. Data consistency also depends on calibration choice and reference standards, with differences in prior studies attributed to synthesis variations, fitting methods (linear vs nonlinear), and pressure scales. Additionally, non-hydrostatic effects are present but generally smaller than for ruby, improving reliability in DAC environments.

Other fits are prospoed in this study we have selected equation 10 and table 4. Sm³⁺-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG) is an optical pressure sensor that uses luminescence peak shifts (notably the Y₁ transition) to determine pressure in high-pressure experiments . It is valued for its lower temperature sensitivity compared to ruby and its ability to operate over a wider temperature range, making it suitable for high-temperature DAC studies. The Y₁ line shows a predictable pressure-dependent shift and becomes more sensitive than ruby at higher pressures (above ~30 GPa), improving its usefulness in extreme conditions.

nodes.label: Wei et al., 2024_YAG-Y2
nodes.display_label: Wei et al., 2024 | YAG-Y2 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Datchi et al., 2007 | Max Pressure: 58.6 GPa | PTM: Ar

rows.value: The Y₂ fluorescence line of Sm³⁺:YAG provides generally reliable pressure measurements with good signal persistence at high pressures, but its data quality is influenced by temperature effects, peak evolution, and experimental conditions . Compared to Y₁, the Y₂ peak becomes increasingly dominant at higher pressures (surpassing Y₁ around ~40 GPa) and can remain observable to even higher pressures, which supports its usefulness in extreme regimes. However, temperature-induced peak shifts and broadening are still present, and neglecting temperature corrections can introduce multi-GPa errors under high P–T conditions. Additionally, at elevated temperatures, overlapping features and changes in relative peak intensity complicate accurate peak fitting. While non-hydrostatic effects exist, they are smaller than those affecting ruby, contributing to improved consistency. Variations across studies are also linked to calibration methods and sample differences. Overall, the Y₂ line maintains good usability at high pressure but requires careful spectral analysis and temperature-corrected calibration for high-precision work.

Other fits are prospoed in this study we have selected equation 10 and table 4. The Y₂ luminescence line of Sm³⁺:YAG is an important pressure-sensitive feature used in DAC experiments, particularly at higher pressures where it becomes stronger and more stable than the Y₁ line . It shows a clear and predictable pressure-dependent wavelength shift, enabling reliable pressure calibration across a wide range. Compared to ruby, Sm³⁺:YAG (including Y₂) exhibits lower temperature sensitivity and better performance in high-temperature environments. The Y₂ line is especially valuable at extreme pressures due to its persistence and increasing intensity, making it suitable for extending measurements beyond the limits of other sensors. Although temperature effects are smaller, they are still significant enough to require correction for accurate results.

nodes.label: Yagi 1978_KCl
nodes.display_label: Yagi 1978 | KCl-B2 | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: None | cal_to: Decker 1971 | Max Pressure: 9 GPa | PTM: ME

rows.value: up to 800 °C, but EOS given at 25 °C

B2 KCl appears above ~21 kbar with ΔV/V ≈ –0.108 across transition.
EOS parameters derived from second-order Birch–Murnaghan fits at 25, 100, 200 °C.
At 25 °C: K0 ≈ 370 kbar, K0′ = 5.4, fictive V0 ≈ 0.863 of B1 volume (≈31.8 cm³/mol).

The B1 → B2 transition in KCl was found at 21.2 ±0.3 kbar with ΔV/V ≈ –0.108.
For KCl-II (B2), Table 3 gives: at 25 °C, K0 = 370(20) kbar, K0′ = 5.4.
V0 is fictive, scaled from B1 volume using thermal expansion data.
EOS fits limited to ~90 kbar. Results consistent with earlier piston-displacement experiments.

nodes.label: Yamazaki et al., 2012_Fe
nodes.display_label: Yamazaki et al., 2012 | Fe-hcp | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Tsuchiya 2003 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: MgO + 5% Cr2O3

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Resistive heating using TiB2 + BN + AlN heater in a Kawai-type multianvil apparatus. Measurements performed up to 1900 K. Thermal pressure modeled using a Mie-Gruneisen-Debye formalism including quasi-harmonic, anharmonic, and electronic thermal pressure terms.

In situ synchrotron XRD measurements performed on epsilon-Fe up to 80 GPa and 1900 K. Pressure determined from Au using the Tsuchiya (2003) Au EOS. The Vinet and third-order Birch-Murnaghan fits produced nearly identical compression curves over the measured pressure range. Authors report the hcp phase remained stable throughout the measured high-pressure range.

Table 1 reports both Birch-Murnaghan and Vinet EOS fits.
This yaml corresponds to the Vinet fit:

  V0 = 22.17(6) Å^3
  KT0 = 196(8) GPa
  KT0' = 4.8(2)
  gamma0 = 3.2(2)
  theta0 = 1168(61) K
  q = 0.8(3)

Pressure was determined from Au diffraction peaks using
the Au EOS of Tsuchiya (2003).

The thermal EOS was modeled using:

  P(V,T) = PT0(V) + ΔPth(V,T)

where PT0(V) was fit using both:
  - third-order Birch-Murnaghan EOS
  - Vinet EOS

The authors note that both EOS forms produce nearly
identical compression curves within the experimental range.

Measurements were conducted at:
  - pressures up to 80 GPa
  - temperatures up to 1900 K
  - using the SPEED-Mk.II Kawai apparatus at SPring-8.

nodes.label: Ye et al., 2018_NaCl-B2_vin
nodes.display_label: Ye et al., 2018 | NaCl-B2 | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Ye et al., 2017 | Max Pressure: 240 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: B2 reflections refined in re-fit dataset (not directly measured in this study)
Laser annealing (1600–2600 K) improves stress state for calibrants

NaCl-B2 EOS parameters are not directly measured in this study.
Instead, Ye et al. (2018) re-fit the NaCl-B2 EOS from Dorfman et al. (2012)
after converting pressures to the MgO-Y17 scale. This gives a self-consistent
pressure calibration network with MgO, Pt, Au, Ne, and ruby.
V0 is fixed at 41.35 Å³/f.u. per the original NaCl-B2 dataset.

This NaCl-B2 EOS is part of the internally consistent pressure metrology
proposed in Ye et al. (2018). It is not a new measurement of NaCl-B2,
but a recalibration of existing NaCl-B2 compression data to the MgO-Y17 scale.
These parameters are recommended when NaCl-B2 is used as a pressure calibrant
together with MgO, Pt, Au, Ne, and ruby under the Ye et al. (2018) framework.

nodes.label: Ye et al., 2018_Ne_BM
nodes.display_label: Ye et al., 2018 | Ne | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Ye et al., 2017 | Max Pressure: 130 GPa | PTM: Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Double-sided laser heating to 1600–2600 K (annealing only)

Laser annealing significantly reduces deviatoric stress from ~8–24×10⁻³ (unannealed)
to ~2–9×10⁻³ (annealed) in S·t values (Figure 2). 
Uniaxial stress after annealing is <1 GPa below 75 GPa and ~2 GPa at 130 GPa.
Unit-cell volumes refined using both 111 and 200 peaks; similar to Dewaele 2008
and Takemura 2010 who used 111 only above 100 GPa.

- Solid Ne is fcc above 4.5 GPa and remains fcc throughout the 30–130 GPa range.
- Pressures are recalculated using the internally consistent MgO-Y17 scale.
- The study identifies a fully self-consistent set of EOSs for MgO, Pt, Au, Ne, 
  NaCl-B2, and ruby (Table 2), all agreeing within ~3% up to 240 GPa.
- This EOS is intended as a new reference for Ne-loaded DAC experiments
  where Ne is both sample environment and pressure medium.

nodes.label: Ye et al., 2018_ruby
nodes.display_label: Ye et al., 2018 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Polynomial | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Ye et al., 2017 | Max Pressure: 150 GPa | PTM: He; Ne

rows.value: APS (datasets from Speziale et al. 2001; Jacobsen et al. 2008)

Ruby scale refit based on simultaneous compression of MgO and ruby in He.
Parameters A = 1887(3) GPa and m = 5.4(1) match Dorogokupets & Oganov (2007)
within 0.5 GPa to 150 GPa. This scale is internally consistent with MgO-Y17,
Pt-Y17, Au-Y17, Ne, and NaCl-B2 calibrations identified in this study.

This ruby scale is not the Mao (1986) or Piermarini (1975) form. Instead, it is a 
refit of the Aleksandrov et al. (1987) / Dorogokupets–Oganov polynomial expression:
    P = A*(Δλ/λ0)*(1 + m*(Δλ/λ0)).
This scale is recommended for pressure determination when using the internally
consistent calibrant set MgO–Pt–Au–Ne–NaCl-B2 defined in Ye et al. (2018).

nodes.label: Yokoo et al., 2009_Au
nodes.display_label: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 550 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Yokoo et al. (2009) present a revised primary equation of state for gold using shock compression data up to ~550 GPa, incorporating thermal and electronic contributions within a Mie–Grüneisen framework . The EOS is constrained using both high-pressure shock data and independent ambient-pressure thermodynamic properties (e.g., thermal expansion, bulk modulus, and heat capacity), resulting in a self-consistent model. Compared to earlier studies, the revised EOS yields higher pressures, correcting systematic underestimations from older shock datasets. The model demonstrates excellent agreement with experimental Hugoniot data and thermodynamic measurements, with uncertainties typically within ~2%. Gold is reaffirmed as a reliable primary pressure standard over a broad P–T range, and consistency with a similarly derived platinum EOS further supports the approach.

nodes.label: Yokoo et al., 2009_Pt
nodes.display_label: Yokoo et al., 2009 | Pt | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 550 GPa | PTM: Not Applicable

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: Shock compression data extending to ~550–660 GPa, combined with independent thermodynamic constraints, allowing construction of a primary (scale-free) pressure standard . Uncertainties are largely controlled by the accuracy of the shock Hugoniot, with pressure errors estimated to be on the order of a few percent, and similar to those for gold. A key strength is the consistent modeling approach using the same framework as Au, which enables cross-validation between the two materials. However, as with all shock-based EOS, the data represent high-temperature Hugoniot states rather than isothermal conditions, requiring model-dependent thermal corrections (e.g., phonon and electronic contributions), which introduce systematic uncertainty. Additional uncertainty arises from fitting procedures and parameter correlations, particularly in determining K0 and K0 prime.

Yokoo et al. (2009) determine a revised equation of state for platinum using shock compression data combined with ambient-pressure thermodynamic measurements, applying a Mie–Grüneisen framework with a third-order formulation . The resulting EOS uses V0 = 15.1199 A^3 per atom, K0 = 276.4 GPa, and K0 prime ≈ 5.48, and extends reliably to ~550 GPa. Compared to previous Pt scales, this model yields slightly lower pressures (by up to ~3%), improving agreement with independent datasets. The Pt EOS is shown to be highly consistent with the revised Au EOS, with differences of only ~1–3% across a wide pressure range, reinforcing both as reliable primary pressure standards.

nodes.label: Yoo et al., 2000_Co
nodes.display_label: Yoo et al., 2000 | Co-hcp | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Holmes et al., 1989; Cynn and Yoo 1999 | Max Pressure: 100 GPa | PTM: Ar; NaCl

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: 300 μm flats (P < 70 GPa); beveled 300→100 μm at 8° (P > 70 GPa)

Pressures were determined from the equations of state of Pt or Ta using their 
in situ lattice parameters, with uncertainties of ~4 GPa at 100 GPa and ~6 GPa 
at 200 GPa. The ε(hcp) phase is observed up to ~100 GPa at room temperature and 
then gradually transforms to a new β(fcc) Co phase between ~105–150 GPa. 
Third-order Birch–Murnaghan fits to the ε-Co P–V data give B0 = 199 GPa and 
B0' = 3.6, in agreement with previous measurements and first-principles calculations.

This YAML describes the ε(hcp) Co equation of state at 300 K from Yoo et al. (2000).
The study also reports a martensitic transition to a β(fcc) phase near 105 GPa with 
negligible volume change; the fcc phase has V0 = 0.1056 cm^3/g, B0 = 224 GPa, 
and B0' = 5.8 (Table II), but those parameters are not included here.

nodes.label: Zha et al., 2000_Al2O3
nodes.display_label: Zha et al., 2000 | Al2O3 | Luminescence | Power | K0 Fixed:  | cal_to: Zha et al., 2000 | Max Pressure: 55 GPa | PTM: He

rows.value: SLS X17C (for XRD); optical Brillouin system at Carnegie/CHiESS

Zha et al. (2000) derive a primary pressure–density scale for MgO to 55 GPa
by integrating single-crystal Brillouin velocities with polycrystalline x-ray
diffraction densities under nearly hydrostatic He conditions. Using this MgO
primary scale, they recalibrate the ruby R1 fluorescence pressure scale and
fit Eq. (7): P(GPa) = (A/B)*[(λ/λ0)^B - 1], with A fixed at 1904 GPa and
B = 7.715. This functional form is identical to the Mao et al. (1986) ruby
scale but with slightly larger curvature B, reflecting the more hydrostatic
MgO-based primary calibration. The authors state that the resulting ruby
scale is accurate to within ~1% up to 55 GPa under hydrostatic conditions.

This YAML records the MgO-based ruby scale from Zha et al. (2000), which
refines the curvature parameter B in the standard P = (A/B)[(λ/λ0)^B – 1]
form. The initial slope A = 1904 GPa is retained from earlier ruby scales,
while B = 7.715 is determined by fitting ruby shifts to the MgO primary
pressure–density relation. λ0 is taken as the conventional ambient R1
wavelength (~694.2 nm) consistent with Piermarini et al. (1975) and
subsequent ruby work.

nodes.label: Zha et al., 2000_MgO
nodes.display_label: Zha et al., 2000 | MgO | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Absolute | Max Pressure: 55 GPa | PTM: He; ME

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: polycrystalline MgO diffraction lines (NaCl-type B1 structure)

Zha et al. (2000) determine a primary pressure–density equation of state for MgO
to 55 GPa by combining single-crystal Brillouin scattering (elastic tensor C11,
C12, C44) with polycrystalline x-ray diffraction densities under nearly hydrostatic
helium conditions. The isothermal bulk modulus and its pressure derivative at 300 K
are K0T = 160.2(7) GPa and K0T' = 4.03(3), respectively, obtained by converting the
adiabatic moduli to isothermal and integrating KT = ρ (dP/dρ)T. The reference density
is ρ0 = 3.585 g/cm^3 for MgO at 300 K, from which V0 ≈ 18.67 Å^3 per formula unit is
derived. The authors estimate that the resulting MgO-based primary pressure scale
has an accuracy of ~1% to 55 GPa under hydrostatic conditions.

This YAML captures the 300 K primary MgO equation of state from Zha et al. (2000), which is obtained by integrating Brillouin-derived elastic moduli with XRD densities and fitting the resulting pressure–density relation to a third-order Birch–Murnaghan form. The resulting MgO EOS is then used in the same paper to recalibrate the ruby fluorescence pressure scale, providing a hydrostatic primary reference for pressures up to 55 GPa.

nodes.label: Zha et al., 2004_Re
nodes.display_label: Zha et al., 2004 | Re | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Anderson et al., 1989 | Max Pressure: 8.47 GPa | PTM: SiO2 glass

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Deviatoric stresses largely reduced at high T; random peak strain observed.
Volume uncertainty estimated at ΔV/V ≈ 0.0006 for Re.
Overall pressure uncertainty estimated at ~5% across P–T range.

This study constructs a full P–V–T EOS for Re using a Mie–Grüneisen linear
thermal pressure model combined with a fixed 300 K Birch–Murnaghan isotherm.
The ambient EOS parameters (K0 = 360 GPa, K0' = 4.5) were chosen for
consistency with shock data. Thermal parameters were obtained by fitting 
Eq. (6) to isochoric lines generated from simultaneous high P–T diffraction
measurements.

The Re EOS agrees well with shock Hugoniots up to ~150 GPa and ~3000 K, 
with deviation < ~3%. Larger deviations likely result from extrapolation 
outside the measured P–T range.

nodes.label: Zha et al., 2008_Pt
nodes.display_label: Zha et al., 2008 | Pt | XRD | Birch-Murnaghan | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Speziale et al., 2001 | Max Pressure: 80 GPa | PTM: MgO (mixed with Pt)

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
Room-temperature EOS data were collected after thermal
annealing cycles to minimize deviatoric stress. The 300 K
data from this study are consistent with quasihydrostatic
DAC data of Dewaele et al. (2004) and multianvil data of
Fei et al. (2004) in the overlapping pressure range.

The final 300 K EOS parameters were obtained by fitting
combined data from this study, Dewaele et al. (2004),
and Fei et al. (2004), with V0 fixed at 60.38 A^3.

The preferred 300 K EOS for Pt is a third-order
Birch-Murnaghan form with:
  V0 = 60.38 A^3 (fixed),
  K0 = 273.5(1.0) GPa,
  K0' = 4.70(6).

This EOS serves as the reference isotherm for the full
P–V–T EOS inversion using both thermodynamic and
Mie–Gruneisen–Debye approaches. Thermal parameters
(gamma0, q0, q1, theta0) are reported separately and
should be stored in a linked thermal-EOS YAML.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Au_K0fixed_mp275
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 275.1 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The gold equation-of-state data derived from XRD in the toroidal DAC (tDAC) demonstrates generally high quality but is influenced by nonhydrostatic compression conditions and peak overlap at extreme pressures . Volumes were calculated using only the (111) diffraction peak, which is least sensitive to uniaxial stress, improving reliability compared to multi-peak approaches; however, residual stress effects are still present due to the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium. At pressures above ~275 GPa, overlap between Au and Pt peaks required fixing peak widths during fitting, introducing additional uncertainty in peak position determination. Despite these challenges, the Au P–V data show a smooth compression trend and agree within ~1% in volume with hydrostatic reference equations of state up to ~4 Mbar, indicating strong internal consistency. Pressure uncertainties propagate from calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021), and residuals of up to ~4% relative to this scale are observed at the highest pressures. Overall, the dataset is robust for ultrahigh-pressure EOS fitting, but accuracy is limited by nonhydrostatic stress, peak overlap, and dependence on external calibration.

This study uses synchrotron XRD measurements of gold in a toroidal DAC to constrain its equation of state up to ~275–414 GPa, applying a third-order Rydberg–Vinet model with fixed V0, K0, and a fitted K0' of ~6.05 . Gold is used as a secondary material calibrated against a platinum pressure scale, enabling cross-validation of pressure at multi-megabar conditions. The use of the (111) lattice plane provides a closer approximation to hydrostatic compression under nonhydrostatic conditions. Results show that Au compression follows a smooth trend and matches previous hydrostatic and dynamic EOS studies, supporting its reliability as a reference material. The work also demonstrates that large-culet toroidal DACs can generate high-quality Au data at pressures exceeding 4 Mbar, extending static compression capabilities.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Au_K0fixed_mp414
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 414 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The gold EOS dataset extending to 414 GPa maintains overall good quality but is more affected by high-pressure limitations compared to lower-pressure fits . Volumes are derived from the (111) diffraction peak to reduce sensitivity to uniaxial stress under nonhydrostatic conditions, though the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium still introduces residual stress. Above ~275 GPa, significant peak overlap between Au and Pt requires fixing peak widths during fitting, which increases uncertainty in peak position and volume determination. Despite this, the compression trend remains smooth, and the data are consistent with lower-pressure fits and prior studies. Agreement with hydrostatic EOS references is within ~1% in volume, though pressure residuals can reach up to ~4% at the highest pressures. Calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021) remains a key source of systematic uncertainty.

This study determines the equation of state of gold up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, applying a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 16.962 A^3 per atom, K0 = 157 GPa, and K0 prime = 6.35 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and the (111) lattice plane is used to approximate hydrostatic compression behavior. The results extend Au EOS constraints into the multi-megabar regime and remain consistent with both lower-pressure fits and previous hydrostatic and dynamic studies. Allowing K0 to vary enables fitting across the full pressure range, including regions affected by peak overlap.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Au_K0unfixed_mp275
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 275.1 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The gold EOS dataset shows good overall quality, with volumes derived from the (111) diffraction peak to minimize sensitivity to nonhydrostatic stress, though measurements were still conducted without a pressure-transmitting medium . The absence of a PTM introduces residual uniaxial stress, but the (111) plane helps approximate hydrostatic behavior. Data up to 275.1 GPa avoid the strongest effects of peak overlap with Pt, improving reliability compared to higher-pressure fits. The compression trend is smooth and consistent across runs, and agreement with previous hydrostatic studies is within ~1% in volume, indicating strong internal consistency. Uncertainty arises primarily from calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021) and from fitting choices, with K0 treated as a free parameter introducing additional variability.

This study determines the equation of state of gold using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, fitting a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 16.962 A^3 per atom, K0 = 163 GPa, and K0 prime = 6.18 . Pressure is calibrated to platinum using the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and only the (111) lattice plane is used to approximate hydrostatic compression behavior. The results show a smooth pressure-volume relationship up to 275.1 GPa and are consistent with prior hydrostatic and dynamic studies, supporting gold as a reliable reference material. Allowing K0 to vary provides flexibility in fitting but introduces some uncertainty compared to fixed-parameter approaches. Overall, the study reinforces the validity of Au as a secondary standard and demonstrates reliable EOS determination under nonhydrostatic DAC conditions.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Au_K0unfixed_mp414
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Au | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 414 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The gold EOS dataset extending to 414 GPa maintains overall good quality but is more affected by high-pressure limitations compared to lower-pressure fits . Volumes are derived from the (111) diffraction peak to reduce sensitivity to uniaxial stress under nonhydrostatic conditions, though the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium still introduces residual stress. Above ~275 GPa, significant peak overlap between Au and Pt requires fixing peak widths during fitting, which increases uncertainty in peak position and volume determination. Despite this, the compression trend remains smooth, and the data are consistent with lower-pressure fits and prior studies. Agreement with hydrostatic EOS references is within ~1% in volume, though pressure residuals can reach up to ~4% at the highest pressures. Calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021) remains a key source of systematic uncertainty.

This study determines the equation of state of gold up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, applying a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 16.962 A^3 per atom, K0 = 157 GPa, and K0 prime = 6.35 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and the (111) lattice plane is used to approximate hydrostatic compression behavior. The results extend Au EOS constraints into the multi-megabar regime and remain consistent with both lower-pressure fits and previous hydrostatic and dynamic studies. Allowing K0 to vary enables fitting across the full pressure range, including regions affected by peak overlap.

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value:
EOS-1 reflects the preferred Vinet fit where K0 is fixed to the ultrasonic
value of 261 GPa. Mo(200) provides the least deviatoric strain and yields 
the most hydrostatic volumes. The Cu EOS from Dewaele et al. (2004) was
used for pressure calibration.

EOS-1 is the recommended calibration for Mo because fixing K0 reduces
covariance between K0 and K0'. This fit reproduces all Mo(200) volumes 
up to 328 GPa

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Re_K0fixed_mp414
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Re | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: True | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 414 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The rhenium EOS dataset obtained from XRD in the DAC shows consistent compression behavior up to 414 GPa, though it is influenced by nonhydrostatic conditions and calibration dependence . Volumes are derived from multiple lattice planes ((100), (101), (102)), improving robustness compared to single-peak approaches, but residual uniaxial stress is present due to the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium. The pressure calibration relies on the Pt standard, introducing systematic uncertainty tied to that EOS. Despite these factors, the Re P–V data display a smooth trend and agree closely (within ~0.75% in volume) with hydrostatic reference equations of state, indicating high internal consistency. No major peak overlap issues are reported for Re, supporting more stable fitting at ultrahigh pressures compared to Au. Overall, the dataset is reliable for EOS determination at multi-megabar conditions, with remaining uncertainties primarily from nonhydrostatic stress and calibration transfer.

This study determines the equation of state of rhenium up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, applying a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 14.733 A^3 per atom, K0 = 360 GPa (fixed), and K0 prime = 4.73 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and multiple diffraction peaks are used to determine volume. The results show a smooth compression curve that agrees well with previous hydrostatic and theoretical studies, particularly those using helium as a pressure medium. Fixing K0 based on ultrasonic data improves stability of the fit while allowing K0 prime to capture high-pressure behavior. Overall, rhenium is confirmed as a reliable material for high-pressure EOS studies and as a potential calibration reference at extreme conditions.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2024_Re_K0unfixed_mp414
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2024 | Re | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2021 | Max Pressure: 414 GPa | PTM: None

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The rhenium EOS dataset shows consistent compression behavior up to 414 GPa, though uncertainties are higher when both K0 and K0 prime are treated as free parameters . Volumes are determined from multiple lattice planes, which improves reliability, but the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium introduces nonhydrostatic stress that can affect lattice spacing measurements. Calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021) introduces an additional systematic dependency. Compared to fixed-K0 fits, allowing K0 to vary increases uncertainty (e.g., larger K0 uncertainty of ±17 GPa), reflecting sensitivity to fitting choices and data scatter at extreme pressures. Despite this, the P–V trend remains smooth and agrees within ~1% in volume with hydrostatic reference data, indicating strong internal consistency

This study determines the equation of state of rhenium up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, fitting a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 14.733 A^3 per atom, K0 = 374 GPa, and K0 prime = 4.5 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and volumes are derived from multiple diffraction peaks to improve accuracy. Allowing K0 to vary provides flexibility in capturing compression behavior at extreme pressures but introduces larger uncertainties compared to fixed-K0 approaches. The results remain consistent with previous hydrostatic and theoretical studies, supporting the reliability of rhenium as a high-pressure reference material.

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2026_Ta_vinet
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2026 | Ta | XRD | Rydberg-Vinet | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020; Campbell et al., 2023 | Max Pressure: 433 GPa | PTM: none, Cu, Bi, Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: The authors tested this third-order Vinet fit but did not use it as their preferred EoS. They report that the pressure residuals increase substantially above 300 GPa, and that the third-order fit does not reproduce the ultrasonically derived ambient isothermal bulk modulus within uncertainty. The fourth-order Vinet fit is preferred.

The abstract "The volume compression of body-centered cubic tantalum has been measured to 433 in the standard bevel and toroidal diamond anvil cell (DAC) using synchrotron-based angle-dispersive powder x-ray diffraction. Six experiments were carried out achieving a maximum pressure of 433(6) GPa. Pressures were estimated using an equation of state (EoS) of Cu or Bi for all experiments. We additionally calculated the elastic constants of Ta and Bi and used our previously calculated values for Cu to inform the stress state of the sample and an EoS of Ta for comparison to our experimental data. Our experimental pressure–volume data for Ta was fit with a fourth-order Vinet EoS, resulting in parameters K0 = 192(5) GPa, η  = 6.4(6), β = −35(5), and Ψ  = 141(13). This Ta EoS is consistent within uncertainty with the ultrasonically derived ambient isothermal bulk modulus, with existing DAC data up to 100 GPa, and with Ta ramp EoS measurements that exceed the pressures of the current study."

nodes.label: Zurkowski et al., 2026_Ta_vinet_extended
nodes.display_label: Zurkowski et al., 2026 | Ta | XRD | Vinet-Extended | K0 Fixed: False | cal_to: Fratanduono et al., 2020; Campbell et al., 2023 | Max Pressure: 433 GPa | PTM: none, Cu, Bi, Ne

rows.label_html: Is the Initial Bulk Modulus (K₀) Fixed?

rows.value: For the Cu and Bi pressure-medium experiments, Ta volumes from individual lattice planes agree within about 1%, which they interpret as evidence for a more uniform compression environment. In contrast, the no-PTM tDAC data show larger nonsystematic mismatch between lattice-plane-derived volumes, especially above ~200 GPa. They interpret this as a stress-state effect, where different Ta lattice planes respond differently to deviatoric stress. Despite that lattice-plane mismatch, the least-squares-fitted Ta volumes from different runs define a common P–V trend. In Fig. 4a, the residuals relative to the fourth-order Vinet fit remain within ~9 GPa across the full pressure range. They also note that the residuals do not show a clear systematic dependence on pressure medium, which supports their claim that Cu, Bi, Ne, and no-PTM experiments produce broadly consistent bulk compression behavior.

The abstract "The volume compression of body-centered cubic tantalum has been measured to 433 in the standard bevel and toroidal diamond anvil cell (DAC) using synchrotron-based angle-dispersive powder x-ray diffraction. Six experiments were carried out achieving a maximum pressure of 433(6) GPa. Pressures were estimated using an equation of state (EoS) of Cu or Bi for all experiments. We additionally calculated the elastic constants of Ta and Bi and used our previously calculated values for Cu to inform the stress state of the sample and an EoS of Ta for comparison to our experimental data. Our experimental pressure–volume data for Ta was fit with a fourth-order Vinet EoS, resulting in parameters K0 = 192(5) GPa, η  = 6.4(6), β = −35(5), and Ψ  = 141(13). This Ta EoS is consistent within uncertainty with the ultrasonically derived ambient isothermal bulk modulus, with existing DAC data up to 100 GPa, and with Ta ramp EoS measurements that exceed the pressures of the current study."
